The rest of this section is dedicated to the proof of this theorem. We write $\theta^{ \pm}$instead of $\theta_{\bar{f}}^{ \pm}$.
6.1. Two lemmas. Start with the following lemma.

Lemma 6.3. If $\overline{\mathscr{D}}$ is a model vertical divisor, then

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{n}}\left(f^{n}\right)^{*} \overline{\mathscr{D}} \rightarrow 0
$$

in $\widehat{\operatorname{Div}}_{\infty}\left(\mathcal{M}_{D} / \mathcal{O}_{\mathbf{K}}\right)$.
Proof. Let $g$ be the Green function induced by $\overline{\mathscr{D}}$ over $\mathcal{M}_{D}^{\text {an }}$. It is a continuous bounded function over $\mathcal{M}_{D}^{\text {an }}$. Therefore

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{n}} g \circ\left(f^{\mathrm{an}}\right)^{n}
$$

converges uniformly to zero over $\mathcal{M}_{D}^{\text {an }}$. Thus it also converges to zero for the boundary topology.

From now on, we fix a compactification $X$ of $\mathcal{M}_{D}$ that satisfies Proposition 5.2. We also replace $f$ by one of its iterates $s:=f^{\mathcal{N}_{0}}$ such that $s^{ \pm 1}$ contracts $X \backslash \mathcal{M}_{D}$ to $p_{ \pm}$. We will show Theorem 6.1 for $s$ and then deduce the result for $f$. Notice that we have $\theta_{s}^{ \pm}=\theta_{f}^{ \pm}$so we will keep the notation $\theta^{ \pm}$.

Lemma 6.4. Let $D \in \operatorname{Div}_{\infty}(X)_{\mathbf{R}}$ be a $\mathbf{R}$-divisor at infinity such that $s_{X}^{*} D=\mu D$ for some $\mu \in \mathbf{R}$. Let $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D})$ be a model of $(X, D)$ over $\mathcal{O}_{\mathbf{K}}$. Let $V \subset \operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_{\mathbf{K}}$ be an open subset such that
(1) The indeterminacy locus $I^{ \pm}$of the rational map $s^{ \pm 1}: \mathscr{X} \rightarrow \mathscr{X}$ satisfies

$$
I_{|V}^{ \pm}=\left(I \cap \overline{\left\{p_{ \pm}\right\}}\right)_{|V}
$$

(2) $\mathscr{D}$ is horizontal over $V$.

Then, for every finite place $v$ above $V$, let $U_{v}^{-}$be the open subset

$$
U_{v}^{-}:=\left\{x \in X_{v}^{\mathrm{an}}: r_{\mathscr{X}_{v}}(x)=r_{\mathscr{X}_{v}}\left(p_{-}\right)\right\}
$$

Then, $s^{-1}$ is defined over $U_{v}^{-}, U_{v}^{-}$is $s^{-1}$-invariant and if $W_{v}^{-}=\left(X_{v}^{\mathrm{an}} \backslash U_{v}^{-}\right) \cap \mathcal{M}_{D, v}^{\mathrm{an}}$, then

$$
\left(g_{\left(\mathscr{X}_{v}, \mathscr{D}_{v}\right)} \circ s^{\mathrm{an}}\right)_{\mid W_{v}^{-}}=\mu \cdot g_{\left(\mathscr{X}_{v}, \mathscr{D}_{v}\right) \mid W_{v}^{-}}
$$

Proof. Let $\pi: \mathscr{Y} \rightarrow \mathscr{X}$ be the normalised blow up of the indeterminacy locus of $s: \mathscr{X} \rightarrow \mathscr{X}$ such that the lift $S: \mathscr{Y} \rightarrow \mathscr{X}$ of $s$ is regular. We have that

$$
g_{(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D})} \circ s^{\mathrm{an}}=g_{\left(\mathscr{Y}, S^{*} \mathscr{D}\right)}
$$

Recall that both sides of this equality are functions over $\mathcal{M}_{D}^{\text {an }}$ which is an open subset of both $X^{\text {an }}$ and $Y^{\text {an }}$. Now, over $V$ the birational morphism $\pi$ consists only of horizontal blow ups that is blow ups of $\bar{p}$ where $p$ is a closed point of the generic fiber. Furthermore these

points $p$ are all above $p_{-}$. By hypothesis, $\pi$ induces an isomorphism between $\mathscr{X}_{V} \backslash\left\{\overline{p_{-}}\right\}$ and $\pi^{-1}\left(\mathscr{X}_{V} \backslash \overline{\left\{p_{-}\right\}}\right)$ because all the indeterminacy points of $s: X \rightarrow X$ are above $p_{-}$. Plus, looking at the horizontal part of our divisors we get that

$$
E=\left(S^{*} \mathscr{D}\right)_{\text {hor }}-\mu\left(\pi^{*} \mathscr{D}\right)_{\text {hor }}
$$

is an $\mathbf{R}$-Weil divisor over $Y$ that has support only over the exceptional divisors of $\pi$ (we also write $\pi: Y \rightarrow X$ for the sequence of blow-ups induced on the generic fiber) which are all above $p_{-}$. Thus we get that the $\mathbf{R}$-Weil divisor

$$
\left(S^{*} \mathscr{D}-\mu \pi^{*} \mathscr{D}\right)_{\mid \pi^{-1}\left(\mathscr{X}_{V} \backslash \overline{\left\{p_{-}\right\}}\right)}
$$

is trivial, hence we get (97) for every finite place $v$ over $V$ by Corollary 2.6.

# 6.2. An iterative process. 

Proposition 6.5. Let $D \in \operatorname{Div}_{\infty}(X)$ be such that $f_{X}^{*} D=\mu D$ for some $\mu \in \mathbf{R}$. Let $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D})$ be a model of $(X, D)$, then

- If $\mu=\lambda_{1}$, then $D=\theta_{f, X}^{+}$up to renormalisation and $\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{n}}\left(s^{n}\right)^{*} \mathscr{D}$ converges towards an element $\bar{\theta}^{+}(X)$ such that $s^{*} \bar{\theta}^{+}(X)=\lambda_{1} \bar{\theta}^{+}(X)$ which a priori depends on $X$.
- Else $|\mu|<\left|\lambda_{1}\right|$ and $\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{n}}\left(s^{n}\right)^{*} \mathscr{D}$ converges towards 0 .

To prove the proposition, we study the sequence of adelic divisors

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{n}}\left(s^{n}\right)^{*} \mathscr{D}-\left(\frac{\mu}{\lambda_{1}}\right)^{n} \mathscr{D}
$$

We show that if $|\mu|<\lambda_{1}$, then this sequence tends to 0 and if $\mu=\lambda_{1}$, then this sequence converges towards an adelic divisor. Looking at Green functions, we need to show that the sequence

$$
u_{n}:=\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{n}} g_{(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D})} \circ\left(s^{n}\right)^{\mathrm{an}}-\left(\frac{\mu}{\lambda_{1}}\right)^{n} g_{(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D})}
$$

converges over $\mathcal{M}_{D}^{\text {an }}$ with respect to the boundary topology. We split the proof into two parts. First we show that away from the indeterminacy point $p_{-}$, the convergence is actually uniform. Then, we will study in more details what happens near $p_{-}$and show the convergence for the boundary topology there.
6.3. Convergence away from $p_{-}$. Since $p_{-} \in X(\mathbf{K})$ we write $p_{-}^{\nu}$ for its image in $X_{v}^{\text {an }}$. There exists an open neighbourhood $U^{-}:=\bigsqcup_{v} U_{v}^{-}$of $\bigcup_{v}\left\{p_{-}^{\nu}\right\}$ in $X^{\text {an }}$ such that $s^{-1}$ is defined over $U^{-}$and $U^{-}$is $s^{-1}$-invariant. Indeed, let $V \subset \operatorname{Spec} O_{\mathbf{K}}$ be an open subset that satisfies the hypothesis of Lemma 6.4. For every finite place $v$ above $V$ we set $U_{v}^{-}=\left\{x: r_{\mathscr{X}_{v}}(x)=r_{\mathscr{X}_{v}}\left(p_{-}^{\nu}\right)\right\}$ as the open subset of $X_{v}^{\text {an }}$ defined in Lemma 6.4. For the finitely many remaining places $v$ (including also all the infinite ones), we know by Proposition 5.2 that $p_{-}^{\nu}$ is an attracting

fixed point of $\left(s_{v}^{-1}\right)^{\text {an }}$. Let $U_{v}^{-}$be an $\left(s_{v}^{-1}\right)^{\text {an }}$ invariant open neighbourhood of $p_{-}^{v}$ such that $\left(s_{v}^{-1}\right)^{\text {an }}\left(U_{v}^{-}\right) \Subset U_{v}^{-}$. Define

$$
U^{-}:=\bigsqcup_{v \in M(\mathbf{K})} U_{v}^{-}
$$

It is an $\left(s^{-1}\right)^{\text {an }}$ invariant open subset. Let $W^{-}$be its complement, it is $s^{\text {an }}$-invariant.
Set

$$
h=u_{1}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}} g_{(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D})} \circ s^{\mathrm{an}}-\frac{\mu}{\lambda_{1}} g_{(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D})}
$$

Lemma 6.6. The function $h$ extends to a continuous bounded function over $W^{-}$and

$$
h_{W_{V[f]}} \equiv 0
$$

Proof. First, by Lemma 6.4 we have $h \equiv 0$ over $W_{v}^{-}$for every finite place $v \in V[\mathrm{f}]$. If $v \notin V[\mathrm{f}]$, then since $f_{X}^{*} D=\mu D$, we have that $h$ extends to a continuous function over $W_{v}^{-}$because $p_{-}^{v} \notin W_{v}^{-}$. Since $W^{-} \backslash W_{V[f]}^{-}$is compact we have that $h$ is a bounded continuous function over $W^{-}$.

Proposition 6.7. If $|\mu|<\lambda_{1}$, then $u_{n}$ converges uniformly to 0 over $W^{-}$.
If $\mu=\lambda_{1}$, then $u_{n}$ converges uniformly towards a continuous function $h^{+}$over $W^{-}$such that
(1) $h_{\mid W_{V[f]}^{-}}^{+} \equiv 0$.
(2) If $G^{+}=h^{+}+g_{(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D})}$, then $G^{+} \circ s^{\mathrm{an}}=\lambda_{1} G^{+}$.

Proof. We have

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{n} & =\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{n-1}}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}} g_{(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D})} \circ s\right) \circ s^{n-1}-\left(\frac{\mu}{\lambda_{1}}\right)^{n} g_{(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D})} \\
& =\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{n-1}}\left(h+\frac{\mu}{\lambda_{1}} g_{(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D})}\right) \circ s^{n-1}-\left(\frac{\mu}{\lambda_{1}}\right)^{n} g_{(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D})} \\
& =\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{n-1}} h \circ s^{n-1}+\frac{\mu}{\lambda_{1}} u_{n-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore,

$$
u_{n}=\sum_{\ell=0}^{n-1} \frac{\mu^{\ell}}{\lambda_{1}^{n-1}} h \circ s^{n-1-\ell}
$$

If $|\mu|<\lambda_{1}$, let $M=\max _{W^{-}}|h|$, then

$$
\sup _{W^{-}}\left|u_{n}(x)\right| \leqslant \frac{M}{\lambda_{1}^{n-1}} \frac{|\mu|^{n}-1}{|\mu|-1} \xrightarrow[n \rightarrow+\infty]{ } 0
$$

If $\mu= \pm 1$, then

$$
\sup _{W^{-}}\left|u_{n}(x)\right| \leqslant \frac{M n}{\lambda_{1}^{n-1}} \underset{n \rightarrow+\infty}{\longrightarrow} 0
$$

If $\mu=\lambda_{1}$, then write $\mathscr{D}^{+}$for $\mathscr{D}$. Equation (109) becomes

$$
u_{n}=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{k}} h \circ s^{k}
$$

Since $h$ is bounded over $W^{-}, u_{n}$ converges uniformly over $W^{-}$towards a continuous function $h^{+}$. By Lemma 6.4, it is clear that $u_{n \mid W_{V[Y]}^{-}} \equiv 0$, thus $h_{W_{V[Y]}^{+}}^{+} \equiv 0$. If $G^{+}=h^{+}+g_{\left(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D}^{+}\right)}$, then it is defined on $W^{-} \cap \mathcal{M}_{D}^{\text {an }}$ and satisfies $G^{+} \circ s^{\mathrm{an}}=\lambda_{1} G^{+}$.

If $\mathscr{D}^{-}$is a model of $\theta_{\bar{f}, X}^{-}$, we construct in the same fashion an open $s^{\text {an }}$-invariant neighbourhood $U^{+}$of $\bigcup_{v}\left\{p_{+}^{v}\right\}$ with $W^{+}:=X^{\text {an }} \backslash U^{+}$and the function $G^{-}$which is defined over $W^{+} \cap \mathcal{M}_{D}^{\text {an }}$, satisfies $G^{-} \circ\left(s^{-1}\right)^{\text {an }}=\lambda_{1} G^{-}$and is such that $G^{-}-g_{\left(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D}^{-}\right)}$extends to a continuous function $h^{-}$over $W^{+}$that satisfies

$$
h_{\left.\right|_{W_{V[Y]}^{+}}}^{-} \equiv 0
$$

Furthermore, we can choose $U^{-}$and $U^{+}$such that $U^{-} \Subset W^{+}$and $U^{+} \Subset W^{-}$. For $v \in V[\mathrm{f}]$ we already have $U_{v}^{-} \cap U_{v}^{+}=\varnothing$. For the places outside $V[\mathrm{f}]$ we can shrink $U_{v}^{+}, U_{v}^{-}$such that it is the case. We also shrink $U_{v}^{ \pm}$for $v \notin V[\mathrm{f}]$ such that $G_{\left|U_{v}\right|}^{+} \geqslant 1, G_{\left|U_{v}^{-}\right}^{-} \geqslant 1$, this is always possible because $G^{ \pm}-g_{\left(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D}^{ \pm}\right)}$ extends to a continuous function at $p_{ \pm}$and $\theta_{\bar{f}, X}^{ \pm}$is effective.

# 6.4. Convergence everywhere. Define 

$$
\mathscr{U}=\mathscr{X} \backslash\left(\overline{X \backslash \mathcal{M}_{D}} \cup \bigcup_{\mathrm{m} \notin V} \mathscr{X}_{\mathrm{m}}\right)
$$

where $\mathscr{X}_{\mathrm{m}}=\mathscr{X} \times_{\operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_{\mathbf{K}}} \operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_{\mathbf{K}} / \mathrm{m}$. This is a quasiprojective model of $\mathcal{M}_{D}$ over $\mathcal{O}_{\mathbf{K}}$. Let $\mathscr{D}_{0}$ be a model of $\theta_{\bar{f}, X}^{-}$and a boundary divisor of $\mathscr{U}$ in $\mathscr{X}$. If $g_{0}$ is the Green function of $\mathscr{D}_{0}$ over $\mathcal{M}_{D}^{\text {an }}$ then we can suppose without loss of generality that for all $v \notin V[\mathrm{f}], g_{0, v} \geqslant 1$. We have already constructed the Green functions $G^{ \pm}$away from $p_{\mp}$.

Lemma 6.8. For every place $v \notin V[\mathrm{f}]$, over $U_{v}^{-} \cap \mathcal{M}_{D}^{\text {an }}$ the functions

$$
\frac{G^{-}}{g_{0}}, \quad \frac{g_{0}}{G^{-}}
$$

are continuous and bounded.

Proof. With the notations of $\S 6.3$, there exists a constant $A>0$ such that

$$
-A \mathscr{D}_{0} \leqslant \mathscr{D}^{-} \leqslant A \mathscr{D}_{0}
$$

and over $U_{v}^{-}$we have by Proposition 6.7 that

$$
G^{-}=h^{-}+g_{\left(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D}^{-}\right)}
$$

where $h^{-}$is continuous and bounded. This shows the result.
Let $\pi: \mathscr{Y} \rightarrow \mathscr{X}$ be a resolution of indeterminacies of $s: \mathscr{X} \rightarrow \mathscr{X}$ and let $\mathscr{S}: \mathscr{Y} \rightarrow \mathscr{X}$ be a lift of $s$, write $S: Y \rightarrow X$ for the restriction to the generic fiber. Then, $h$ is a green function of $\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}} S^{*} D-\frac{\mu}{\lambda_{1}} \pi^{*} D$. Therefore, there exists a constant $A$ such that

$$
-A \overline{\mathscr{D}_{0}} \leqslant \frac{1}{\lambda_{1}} S^{*} \overline{\mathscr{D}}-\frac{\mu}{\lambda} \pi^{*} \overline{\mathscr{D}} \leqslant A \overline{\mathscr{D}_{0}}
$$

Thus, $\frac{h}{g_{0}}$ is a continuous bounded function over $\mathcal{M}_{D}^{\text {an }}$. We show that the sequence $u_{n}$ converges with respect to the boundary topology. Set the following constants

$$
\begin{gathered}
M_{0}=\sup _{\mathcal{M}_{D}^{\mathrm{an}}}\left|\frac{h}{g_{0}}\right|, \quad M_{1}=\sup _{\mathcal{M}_{D, V[ \mathrm{f} ]^{\mathrm{c}}}^{\mathrm{an}}} \frac{1}{\left|g_{0}\right|}, \quad M_{2}=\sup _{W^{-}}|h| \\
M_{3}=\sup _{U_{V[ \mathrm{f} ]^{\mathrm{c}}}^{-}} \cap \mathcal{M}_{D}^{\mathrm{an}}\left|\frac{g_{0}}{G^{-}}\right|, \quad M_{4}=\sup _{U_{V[ \mathrm{f} ]^{\mathrm{c}}}^{-}} \cap \mathcal{M}_{D}^{\mathrm{an}}\left|\frac{G^{-}}{g_{0}}\right|
\end{gathered}
$$

where $V[\mathrm{f}]^{c}$ is the set of places of $\mathbf{K}$ outside $V[\mathrm{f}]$, including the archimedean ones.
Claim 6.9. Set $M:=\max \left(M_{2} M_{1}, M_{0} M_{3} M_{4}\right)$, then for every $k \geqslant 0$

$$
-M g_{0} \leqslant h \circ\left(s^{\mathrm{an}}\right)^{k} \leqslant M g_{0}
$$

over $\mathcal{M}_{D}^{\text {an }}$.
Proof. We will write $s$ instead of $s^{\text {an }}$ to avoid heavy notations. Let $k \geqslant 0$ and $x \in \mathcal{M}_{D}^{\text {an }}$. Suppose first that $s^{k}(x) \in W^{-}$. If $x$ lies above $v \in V[\mathrm{f}]$, then $h\left(s^{k}(x)\right)=0$ by Lemma 6.6 and (121) is obvious. Otherwise we have

$$
\left|\frac{h\left(s^{k}(x)\right)}{g_{0}(x)}\right| \leqslant M_{2} M_{1}
$$

and (121) is satisfied.
If $s^{k}(x) \notin W^{-}$, then $x, s^{k}(x) \in U^{-} \subset W^{+}$. If $x$ lies above $V[\mathrm{f}]$, then by Proposition 6.7 ,,

$$
G_{\left[W_{V[\mathrm{f}]}^{-}\right.}^{-}=g_{\left(\mathscr{X}_{V[\mathrm{f}]} \mathscr{D}_{V[\mathrm{f}]}^{-}\right)}=g_{0 \mid V[\mathrm{f}]}
$$

thus

$$
\left|h\left(s^{k}(x)\right)\right| \leqslant M_{0} g_{0}\left(s^{k}(x)\right)=\frac{M_{0}}{\lambda_{1}^{k}} g_{0}(x)
$$

Suppose $x$ does not lie above $V[\mathrm{f}]$, let $y=s^{k}(x)$, then

$$
\left|\frac{h\left(s^{k}(x)\right)}{g_{0}(x)}\right|=\left|\frac{h(y)}{g_{0}\left(s^{-k}(y)\right)}\right| \leqslant M_{4}\left|\frac{h(y)}{G^{-}\left(s^{-k}(y)\right)}\right|=M_{4}\left|\frac{h(y)}{\lambda_{1}^{k} G^{-}(y)}\right|
$$

Thus,

$$
\left|\frac{h\left(s^{k}(x)\right)}{g_{0}}\right| \leqslant \frac{M_{4}}{\lambda_{1}^{k}}\left|\frac{g_{0}(y)}{G^{-}(y)}\right|\left|\frac{h(y)}{g_{0}(y)}\right| \leqslant \frac{M_{0} M_{3} M_{4}}{\lambda_{1}^{k}}
$$

End of proof of Proposition 6.5. (1) If $\mu=\lambda_{1}$, then $u_{n}$ converges with respect to the boundary topology because by the claim

$$
\sup _{\mathcal{M}_{D}^{\text {an }}}\left|\frac{h \circ s^{k}}{g_{0}}\right|
$$

is the term of a converging sum.
(2) If $|\mu|<\left|\lambda_{1}\right|$, then $\left|\frac{u_{n}}{g_{0}}\right|$ converges uniformly towards 0 over $\mathcal{M}_{D}^{\text {an }}$. Indeed,

$$
\sup _{\mathcal{M}_{D}^{\text {an }}}\left|\frac{u_{n}}{g_{0}}\right| \leqslant M \sum_{\ell=0}^{n-1} \frac{|\mu|^{\ell}}{\lambda_{1}^{n-1}} \leqslant \frac{M}{\lambda_{1}^{n-1}} \frac{\left|\mu^{n}-1\right|}{|\mu-1|} \xrightarrow[n \rightarrow+\infty]{ } 0
$$

if $\mu \neq \pm 1$ and otherwise

$$
\sup _{\mathcal{M}_{D}^{\text {an }}}\left|\frac{u_{n}}{g_{0}}\right| \leqslant \frac{M n}{\lambda_{1}^{n-1}} \xrightarrow[n \rightarrow+\infty]{ } 0
$$

# 6.5. Proof of Theorem 6.1. 

Proposition 6.10. For any cyclic compactification $X$ of $\mathcal{M}_{D}$ that satisfies Propostion 5.2, let $s=f^{N_{0}}$ be an iterate that contracts $X \backslash \mathcal{M}_{D}$. For any divisor $D \in \operatorname{Div}_{\infty}(X)_{\mathbf{R}}$ and any model adelic extension $\overline{\mathscr{D}}$ of $D$, we have

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{N}}\left(s^{N}\right)^{*} \overline{\mathscr{D}} \rightarrow\left(D \cdot \theta^{-}\right) \bar{\theta}^{+}(X)
$$

Proof. Using Proposition 5.4 (4) we write

$$
D=\alpha \theta_{X}^{+}+\beta D^{-}+R
$$

where $E_{+}, F_{+} \notin \operatorname{Supp} R$ with the notations of Proposition 5.4. Intersecting with $\theta^{-}$and using Proposition 5.4 (1) and (3) we get $\alpha=D \cdot \theta^{-}$. For any model adelic extension $\overline{\mathscr{D}}$ of $D$ we get

by Proposition 6.5 and Lemma 6.3 that

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{N}}\left(s^{N}\right)^{*} \overline{\mathscr{D}} \rightarrow\left(D \cdot \theta^{-}\right) \bar{\theta}^{+}(X)
$$

Proposition 6.11. Let $X$ be any cyclic completion of $\mathcal{M}_{D}$ and let $D \in \operatorname{Div}_{\infty}(X)_{\mathbf{R}}$ such that $D \cdot \theta^{-}=0$. For any model adelic extension $\overline{\mathscr{D}}$ of $D$ we have

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{N}}\left(f^{N}\right)^{*} \overline{\mathscr{D}} \underset{N \rightarrow+\infty}{\longrightarrow} 0
$$

Proof. We can assume up to blowing up that $X$ satisfies Proposition 5.2. Let $s=f^{N_{0}}$ be an iterate of $f$ that contracts $X \backslash \mathcal{M}_{D}$. We have by Proposition 6.10 that

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{N_{0} k}}\left(s^{k}\right)^{*} \overline{\mathscr{D}} \underset{k \rightarrow+\infty}{\longrightarrow} 0
$$

This means that if $\overline{\mathscr{D}}_{0}$ is a boundary divisor, there exists a sequence $\varepsilon_{k} \rightarrow 0$ such that

$$
-\varepsilon_{k} \overline{\mathscr{D}}_{0} \leqslant \frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{N_{0} k}}\left(s^{k}\right)^{*} \overline{\mathscr{D}} \leqslant \varepsilon_{k} \overline{\mathscr{D}}_{0}
$$

We can assume without loss of generality that $-\overline{\mathscr{D}}_{0} \leqslant \overline{\mathscr{D}} \leqslant \overline{\mathscr{D}}_{0}$. Now, for every $N \geqslant 0$ write the Euclidian division of $N$ by $N_{0}, N=a_{n} N_{0}+b_{n}$ and let $C>0$ be a constant such that for every $b=0, \ldots, N_{0}-1$

$$
0 \leqslant \frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{b}}\left(f^{b}\right)^{*} \mathscr{D}_{0} \leqslant C \overline{\mathscr{D}}_{0}
$$

We have

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{N}}\left(f^{N}\right)^{*} \overline{\mathscr{D}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{N_{0} a_{N}}} s^{a_{N}}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{b_{N}}}\left(f^{b_{N}}\right)^{*} \overline{\mathscr{D}}\right)
$$

Now since $f^{*}$ preserves effectiveness we have by (135) and (136) that

$$
-C \varepsilon_{a_{N}} \overline{\mathscr{D}}_{0} \leqslant \frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{N}}\left(f^{N}\right)^{*} \overline{\mathscr{D}} \leqslant C \varepsilon_{a_{N}} \overline{\mathscr{D}}_{0}
$$

Corollary 6.12. Let $X$ be a cyclic completion of $\mathcal{M}_{D}$. If $D \in \operatorname{Div}_{\infty}(X)_{\mathbf{R}}$ and $\overline{\mathscr{D}}$ is a model adelic extension of $D$, then

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{N}}\left(f^{N}\right)^{*} \overline{\mathscr{D}} \underset{N \rightarrow+\infty}{\longrightarrow}\left(D \cdot \theta^{-}\right) \bar{\theta}^{+}(X)
$$

Proof. We can assume that $X$ satisfies Proposition 5.2. Let $s=f^{N_{0}}$ be an iterate that contracts $X \backslash \mathcal{M}_{D}$. We have by Proposition 6.10 that

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{N_{0} k}}\left(s^{k}\right)^{* \overline{\mathscr{D}}} \rightarrow\left(D \cdot \theta^{-}\right) \overline{\theta}^{+}(X)
$$

Write the Euclidian division $N=a_{N} N_{0}+b_{n}$, we have

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{N}}\left(f^{N}\right)^{*} \overline{\mathscr{D}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{N_{0} a_{N}}}\left(s^{a_{N}}\right)^{*}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{b_{N}}}\left(f^{b_{N}}\right)^{*} \overline{\mathscr{D}}\right)
$$

Notice that $\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{N}}\left(f^{b}\right)^{*} D \cdot \theta^{-}=D \cdot \theta^{-}$. For $b=0, \ldots, N_{0}-1$, define

$$
\overline{\mathscr{D}}_{b}=\overline{\mathscr{D}}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{b}}\left(f^{b}\right)^{*} \overline{\mathscr{D}}
$$

This is a model adelic divisor that satisfies $D_{b} \cdot \theta^{-}=0$, by Proposition 6.11 we have

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{k}}\left(s^{k}\right)^{*} \overline{\mathscr{D}}_{b} \rightarrow 0
$$

Thus, for every $b=0, \ldots, N_{0}-1$ we have by (140)

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{N_{0} k}}\left(s^{k}\right)^{*}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{b}}\left(f^{b}\right)^{*} \overline{\mathscr{D}}\right) \rightarrow\left(D \cdot \theta^{-}\right) \overline{\theta}^{+}(X)
$$

and this combined with (141) shows the result.
We now finish the proof of Theorem 6.1 which follows from the following theorem.
Theorem 6.13. The adelic divisor $\overline{\theta}^{+}(X)=: \overline{\theta}^{+}$does not depend on $X$. It is a strongly nef and effective adelic divisor and for any adelic divisor $\bar{D} \in \overline{\operatorname{Div}}_{\infty}\left(\mathcal{M}_{D} / \mathcal{O}_{\mathbf{K}}\right)$ we have

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{N}}\left(f^{N}\right)^{*} \bar{D} \rightarrow\left(D \cdot \theta^{-}\right) \overline{\theta}^{+}
$$

Proof. We show that the adelic divisor $\overline{\theta}^{+}(X)$ defined in Corollary 6.12 does not depend on the completion $X$. It suffices to show that $\overline{\theta}^{+}(X)=\overline{\theta}^{+}(Y)$ for any compactification $Y$ above $X$. Let $D=\theta_{X}^{+} \in \operatorname{Div}_{\infty}(X)$, then $\pi^{*} D \in \operatorname{Div}_{\infty}(Y)$ and satisfies $\pi^{*} D \cdot \theta_{Y}^{-}=\theta_{X}^{+} \cdot \theta_{X}^{-}=\theta^{+} \cdot \theta^{-}=1$. Thus we get by Corollary 6.12 that $\overline{\theta}^{+}(X)=\overline{\theta}^{+}(Y)$.

We now show that $\overline{\theta}^{+}$is strongly nef and effective. Let $H$ be the ample divisor on $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{D} \subset \mathbf{P}^{3}$ defined by $H=\{X=T=0\}+\{Y=T=0\}+\{Z=T=0\}$. Let $\bar{H}$ be a semipositive effective model of $H$ which exists by Lemma 2.4. Since $H$ is ample we have $H \cdot \theta^{-}>0$. By Proposition 2.11 and Corollary 6.12 we have that $\overline{\theta}^{+}$is strongly nef and effective.

Finally, let $\overline{\mathscr{D}} \in \widehat{\operatorname{Div}}_{\infty}\left(\mathcal{M}_{D} / O_{\mathbf{K}}\right)$ be an adelic divisor. For every $\varepsilon>0$, there exists a model adelic divisor $\mathscr{D}_{\varepsilon}$ such that

$$
-\varepsilon \overline{\mathscr{D}}_{0} \leqslant \bar{D}-\overline{\mathscr{D}}_{\varepsilon} \leqslant \varepsilon \overline{\mathscr{D}}_{0}
$$

Since $D_{\varepsilon} \cdot \theta^{-} \rightarrow D \cdot \theta^{-}$and $f^{*}$ preserves effectiveness we have

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{N}}\left(f^{N}\right)^{*} \bar{D} \rightarrow\left(D \cdot \theta^{-}\right) \bar{\theta}^{+}
$$

Remark 6.14. Let $X$ be the compactification $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{D} \subset \mathbf{P}^{3}$ and $H$ the ample divisor $H=\{X=T=0\}+$ $\{Y=T=0\}+\{Z=T=0\}$. Write $D=A / B$ where $A, B \in O_{\mathbf{K}}$, we have a natural model $\left(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{H}\right)$ of $(X, H)$ given by

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathscr{X}=\operatorname{Proj} O_{K}[X, Y, Z, T] /\left(B T\left(X^{2}+Y^{2}+Z^{2}\right)-B X Y Z-A T^{3}\right) \\
& \mathscr{H}=\{X=T=0\}_{R}+\{Y=T=0\}_{R}+\{Z=T=0\}_{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying Corollary 6.12 with $\mathscr{H}$ yields the definitions of the Green functions from the introduction.

Proposition 6.15. Let $G^{+}$be the Green function of $\overline{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{+}$, then
(1) $G^{+} \geqslant 0$.
(2) $G^{+} \circ f^{\mathrm{an}}=\lambda_{1} G^{+}$.
(3) $G^{+}(x)=0$ if and only if the forward $f^{\mathrm{an}}$-orbit of $x$ is bounded.
(4) If $X$ is a compactification of $\mathcal{M}_{D}$, then for any Green function $g$ of $\theta_{f, X}^{+}, G^{+}-g$ extends to a continuous function over $X_{v}^{\mathrm{an}} \backslash\left\{p_{-}\right\}$.
(5) If $v$ is archimedean, then $G_{v}^{+}$is plurisubharmonic and pluriharmonic over the set $\left\{G_{v}^{+}>0\right\}$.

Proof. (1) follows from $\overline{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{+}$being effective. (2) follows from $f^{*} \overline{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{+}=\lambda_{1} \boldsymbol{\theta}^{+}$.
If $X$ is any compactification of $\mathcal{M}_{D}$, we can suppose that it satisfies Proposition 5.2 up to blowing up. Let $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D})$ be a model of $\left(X, \theta_{f, X}^{+}\right)$for a compactification $X$ that satisfies Proposition 5.2. Fix a place $v$ of $\mathbf{K}$, let $U^{+}$be the open neighborhood of $p_{+}$in $X^{\mathrm{an}}$ constructed in $\S 6.3$. Since $G^{+}-g_{(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{D})}$ extends to a continuous function over $U^{+}$and $\theta_{f, X}^{+}$is effective, we can shrink $U_{v}^{+}$such that $G_{U_{v}^{+}}^{+}>0$.

By (2), we have that $G^{+}>0$ over

$$
U_{v}:=\bigcup_{n \geqslant 0}\left(f^{-n}\right)^{\mathrm{an}}\left(U_{v}^{+} \cap \mathcal{M}_{D}^{\mathrm{an}}\right)
$$

To show (3), let $x \in \mathcal{M}_{D}^{\text {an }}$ and let $v$ be the place over which $x$ lies. It suffices to show that if the forward orbit of $x \in\left(\mathcal{M}_{D}\right)_{v}^{\text {an }}$ is unbounded, then there exists $N_{0}$ such that $\left(f^{N_{0}}\right)^{\text {an }}(x) \in$

$U_{v}^{+}$. Since $X_{v}^{\mathrm{an}}$ is compact, the sequence $\left(\left(f^{n}\right)^{\mathrm{an}}(x)\right)$ must have an accumulation point $q \in$ $X_{v}^{\mathrm{an}} \backslash\left(\mathcal{M}_{D}\right)_{v}^{\mathrm{an}}$

If $q \neq p_{-}^{\nu}$, then since an iterate of $f: X \backslash\left\{p_{-}^{\nu}\right\} \rightarrow X \backslash\left\{p_{-}^{\nu}\right\}$ contracts the complement of $\mathcal{M}_{D}$ to $p_{v}^{\nu}$ we must have $\left(f^{k}\right)^{\text {an }}(q)=p_{+}^{\nu}$ for some $k \geqslant 1$ thus by continuity there exists $N_{0}$ such that $\left(f^{N_{0}}\right)^{\text {an }}(x) \in U_{v}^{+}$.

Otherwise $\left(f^{n}\right)^{\text {an }}(x) \rightarrow p_{-}^{\nu}$. Since $p_{-}^{\nu}$ is an attracting fixed point for $\left(f^{-1}\right)^{\text {an }}$, there exists a basis of neighbourhood $U_{v}^{k}$ of $p_{-}^{\nu}$ in $X_{v}^{\text {an }}$ such that for all $N$ large enough, $\left(f^{-N}\right)^{\text {an }}\left(U_{v}^{k}\right) \in U_{v}^{k}$ and we would get that $x \in U_{v}^{k}$ for all $k \geqslant 0$, this is absurd.

To show (5), let $H$ be the ample divisor on $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{D} \subset \mathbf{P}^{3}$ defined by $\{X=T=0\}+\{Y=T=0\}+$ $\{Z=T=0\}$ and let $\bar{H}$ be the semipostive effective model of $H$ defined in Remark 6.14 and $g_{H}$ the associated Green function. Suppose $v$ is archimedean. Since $g_{H}$ is plurisubharmonic over $\mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C}), \frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{n}} g_{H} \circ f^{n}$ also is. By local uniform convergence, we get that $G^{+}$ is plurisubharmonic over $\mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C})$. To show the pluriharmonicity, it suffices to show that $G_{\left[U_{v}^{+} \cap \mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C})\right.}^{+}$is pluriharmonic by the proof of (3). We can suppose that $g_{H}$ is pluriharmonic over $U_{v}^{+} \bigcap \mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C})$ up to shrinking $U_{v}^{+}$, then since $U_{v}^{+}$is $f^{\text {an }}$-invariant, $\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{n}} g_{H} \circ\left(f^{\text {an }}\right)^{n}$ is also pluriharmonic over $U_{v}^{+} \cap \mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C})$ and $G_{\left[U_{v}^{+} \cap \mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C})\right.}^{+}$is pluriharmonic as the local uniform limit of pluriharmonic functions.

To show (4), Let $v$ be any place of $\mathbf{K}$, let $q \in X_{v}^{\mathrm{an}} \backslash\left(\mathcal{M}_{D}\right)_{v}^{\mathrm{an}}$ such that $q \neq p_{-}$. From Proposition 5.2, we can find an open neighbourhood $U_{v}^{-}$of $p_{-}$such that $q \notin U_{v}^{-}$and $\left(f^{-1}\right)^{\text {an }}\left(U_{v}^{-}\right) \in$ $U_{v}^{-}$. The proof of $\S 6.3$ shows that if $g_{+}$is a model Green function of $\theta_{f, X}^{+}$, then

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}^{n}} g_{+} \circ\left(f^{n}\right)^{\mathrm{an}}-g_{+}
$$

converges uniformly over $W_{v}^{-}=X_{v}^{\mathrm{an}} \backslash U_{v}^{-}$to a continuous function which is equal to $G^{+}-g_{+}$. Since two Green functions of the same divisor differ by a continuous function we get the result for any Green function of $\theta_{f, X}^{+}$.

Remark 6.16. In [CD12], Chambert-Loir and Ducros developed a theory of plurisubharmonic functions, currents and differential forms on Berkovich spaces. Following their definitions, (5) also holds for non-archimedean places with the same proof.

# 7. PERIODIC POINTS AND EQUILIBRIUM MEASURE 

7.1. Equidistribution. Let $\left(x_{n}\right)$ be a sequence of $X(\bar{K}) \subset X\left(\overline{\mathbf{K}}_{v}\right)$ and let $\mu_{v}$ be a measure on $X_{v}^{\text {an }}$. We say that the Galois orbit of $\left(x_{n}\right)$ is equidistributed with respect to $\mu_{v}$ if the sequence of measures

$$
\delta\left(x_{n}\right):=\frac{1}{\operatorname{deg}\left(x_{n}\right)} \sum_{x \in \operatorname{Gal}\left(\overline{\mathbf{K}}: \mathbf{K}\right): x_{n}} \delta_{x}
$$

weakly converges towards $\mu_{v}$, where $\delta_{x}$ is the Dirac measure at $x$.
We say that a sequence of points $\left(x_{n}\right)$ of $X(\overline{\mathbf{K}})$ is generic if no subsequence of $\left(x_{n}\right)$ is contained in a strict subvariety of $X$. In particular, a generic sequence is Zariski dense.
Lemma 7.1. Let $X$ be a projective variety over a number field $\mathbf{K}$ and let $\left(x_{n}\right)$ be a Zariski dense sequence of $X(\overline{\mathbf{K}})$, then one can extract a generic subsequence of $\left(x_{n}\right)$.
Proof. The set of strict irreducible subvarieties of $X$ is countable because $\mathbf{K}$ is a number field. Let $\left(Y_{q}\right)_{q \in \mathbf{N}}$ be the set of strict irreducible subvarieties of $X$. We construct a generic subsequence $\left(x_{q}^{\prime}\right)_{q \in \mathbf{N}}$ as follows. Set $Y_{q}^{\prime}=\bigcup_{k \leqslant q} Y_{k}$. This is a strict subvariety of $X$. Let $n(1)$ be such that $x_{n(1)} \notin Y_{1}=Y_{1}^{\prime}$ and suppose we have constructed $n(1)<\cdots<n(q)$ such that $x_{n(i)} \notin Y_{i}^{\prime}$. Since $\left(x_{n}\right)$ is Zariski dense, there exists an integer $n(q+1)>n(q)$ such that $x_{n(q)} \notin Y_{q}^{\prime}$. This defines an increasing sequence $n(q)$ and we set $x_{q}^{\prime}=x_{n(q)}$, The sequence $\left(x_{q}^{\prime}\right)$ is a subsequence of $\left(x_{n}\right)$ which is clearly generic.

We will use the following arithmetic equidistribution theorem from Yuan and Zhang.
Theorem 7.2 ([YZ23] Theorem 5.4.3). Let $X$ be a quasiprojective variety over a number field $\mathbf{K}$ and let $\bar{D}$ be a nef adelic divisor over $X$ such that $D^{\operatorname{dim} X}>0$. Let $\left(x_{n}\right) \in X(\overline{\mathbf{K}})$ be a generic sequence such that $\lim _{n} h_{\bar{D}}\left(x_{n}\right) \rightarrow h_{\bar{D}}(X)$, then at every place $v$ the Galois orbit of the sequence $\left(x_{n}\right)$ is equidistributed with respect to the equilibrium measure $\mu_{\bar{D}, v}$ over $X_{v}^{\mathrm{an}}$.
7.2. Equidistribution of periodic points. Suppose $D \in \mathbf{C}$ is algebraic. Let $f \in \operatorname{Aut}\left(\mathcal{M}_{D}\right)$ be loxodromic. Let $\overline{\theta_{f}^{+}}, \overline{\theta_{f}^{-}}$be the two strongly nef adelic divisors provided by Theorem 6.1 for $f$ and $f^{-1}$. Recall that $\theta_{f}^{+} \cdot \theta_{f}^{-}=1$. Set

$$
\overline{\theta}_{f}=\frac{\overline{\theta}_{f}^{+}+\overline{\theta_{f}^{-}}}{2}
$$

It is a strongly nef adelic divisor over $\mathcal{M}_{D}$ and satisfies $\theta_{f}^{2}=1$. For every place $v$, we write $\mu_{f, v}$ for the equilibrium measure of $\overline{\theta}_{f}$. We also write $h_{f}:=h_{\overline{\theta}_{f}}$ and $G_{f}=\frac{G_{f}^{+}+G_{f}^{-}}{2}$ for the Green function of $\overline{\theta}_{f}$.
Theorem 7.3. If $\left(p_{n}\right)$ is a generic sequence of $\mathcal{M}_{D}(\overline{\mathbf{K}})$ of periodic points of $f$, then for every place $v$ of $\mathbf{K}$ the Galois orbit of $\left(p_{n}\right)$ is equidistributed with respect to the measure $\mu_{f, v}$ over $\mathcal{M}_{D, v}^{\mathrm{an}}$.
Proof. We apply Yuan-Zhang's equidistribution theorem to the adelic divisor $\overline{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$. We need to show that the sequence $h_{f}\left(p_{n}\right)$ converges to $h_{f}\left(\mathcal{M}_{D}\right)$. Since the points $p_{n}$ are periodic, we have for all $v, G_{v}\left(p_{n}^{v}\right)=0$ by Proposition 6.15 (3). Thus we need to show that $h_{f}\left(\mathcal{M}_{D}\right)=0$. To do so we apply Theorem 5.3.3 of [YZ23]. Namely, let

$$
e\left(\mathcal{M}_{D}, \overline{\theta}_{f}\right):=\sup _{U \subset \mathcal{M}_{D}} \inf _{p \in U} h_{f}(p)
$$

where $U$ runs through open subsets of $\mathcal{M}_{D}$. This quantity is called the essential minimum of $\overline{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$. Since we have a generic sequence of periodic points, we get $e\left(\mathcal{M}_{D}, \overline{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{f}\right)=0$. Theorem 5.3.3 of [YZ23] states that

$$
e\left(\mathcal{M}_{D}, \overline{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{f}\right) \geqslant h_{f}(X)
$$

Therefore we get $h_{f}\left(\mathcal{M}_{D}\right)=0$ and Yuan's equidistribution theorem gives the desired result.

Corollary 7.4. If $D$ is algebraic and $f, g$ are two loxodromic automorphisms of $\mathcal{M}_{D}$ such that $\operatorname{Per}(f) \cap \operatorname{Per}(g)$ is Zariski dense, then for every place $v$ of $\mathbf{Q}(D)$,

$$
\mu_{f, v}=\mu_{g, v}
$$

Proof. Let $\left(x_{n}\right)$ be a Zariski dense sequence of $\operatorname{Per}(f) \cap \operatorname{Per}(g)$. By Lemma 7.1, we can suppose that $\left(x_{n}\right)$ is generic. By Theorem 7.3, for every place $v$ of $\mathbf{K}$, the Galois orbit of the sequence $\left(x_{n}\right)$ equidistributes with respect to both the measures $\mu_{f, v}$ and $\mu_{g, v}$. Thus, they must be equal.
7.3. Ergodic properties of the equilibrium measure. Let $D \in \mathbf{C}$ and consider the embedding $\mathbf{Q}(D) \hookrightarrow \mathbf{C}$. This corresponds to an archimedean place of $\mathbf{Q}(D)$. Let $X$ be a compactification of $\mathcal{M}_{D}$ (over $\mathbf{C}$ ) that satisfies Proposition 5.2. The measure $\mu_{f}=d d^{c} G_{f}^{+} \wedge d d^{c} G_{f}^{-}$that we construct in this paper is exactly the measure associated to the birational transformation $f: X \rightarrow X$ constructed in [BD05]. Indeed, The quantitative condition (3) in loc.cit is satisfied because the indeterminacy points of $f$ are intersection points of $X \backslash \mathcal{M}_{D}$. In particular the main theorem of [BD05] implies the following.

Theorem 7.5. For every $D \in \mathbf{C}$, for every archimedean place $v$ of $\mathbf{Q}(D)$, the measure $\mu_{f, v}$ is ergodic, mixing and hyperbolic.

A saddle point is a point where the differential of $f$ does not have any eigenvalue of modulus 1 .

Corollary 7.6. For every archimedean place $v, \operatorname{Supp} \mu_{f, v}$ is contained in the closure of the saddle periodic points.

Proof. This follows from Theorem 4.2 of [Kat80] because $\mu_{f, v}$ is ergodic, hyperbolic and not supported on a finite orbit.

# 8. SADDLE PERIODIC POINTS ARE IN THE SUPPORT OF THE EQUILIBRIUM MEASURE 

Let $D \in \mathbf{C}$ be algebraic. Fix an archimedean place $v$ of $\mathbf{Q}(D)$. For this section we work only over this place so we will drop the index $v$ and fix an embedding $\mathbf{Q}(D) \hookrightarrow \mathbf{C}$.

Theorem 8.1. Let $f$ be a loxodromic automorphism of $\mathcal{M}_{D}$. The support of the measure $\mu_{f}$ is the closure of the saddle periodic points of $f$.

By Corollary 7.6, it suffices to show that every saddle periodic point is in the support of $\mu_{f}$. This theorem, stated in [Can01] and [Can09], follows directly from the work of Dinh and Sibony in [DS13], which extends [BS91a], and an argument of [BLS93] for Hénon type automorphisms of the complex affine plane. A sketch of proof is given in [Can09, §3.1 and 3.2]. We provide a more detailed proof here.
8.1. Green functions and bounded orbits. First, let us summarize some of the properties of the function $G_{f}^{+}: \mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C}) \rightarrow \mathbf{R}^{+}$of $\overline{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{f}^{+}$from Proposition 6.15. The stable manifold of a point $q \in \mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C})$ is the set of points $p$ such that

$$
\operatorname{dist}\left(f^{n}(q), f^{n}(p)\right) \underset{n \rightarrow+\infty}{\longrightarrow} 0
$$

Fix a compactification $X$ of $\mathcal{M}_{D}$ that satisfies Proposition 5.2. We have
(a) $\left\{G_{f}^{+}=0\right\}$ coincides with the set $K^{+}(f)$ of points with a bounded forward orbit;
(b) $G_{f}^{+}$is plurisubharmonic, and is pluriharmonic on the set $\left\{G_{f}^{+}>0\right\}$;
(c) the set $K^{+}(f)$ is closed in $\mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C})$, its closure in $X(\mathbf{C})$ coincides with $K^{+}(f) \cup\left\{p_{-}\right\}$;
(d) locally, near every point $q \neq p_{-}$of $X(\mathbf{C}) \backslash \mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C})$,

$$
G_{f}^{+}(x)=-\sum_{i} a_{i} \log \left|z_{i}\right|+u(x)
$$

where the functions $z_{i}$ are holomorphic equations of the boundary components containing $q$, the real numbers $a_{i} \geqslant 0$ are the weight of $\theta_{f, X}^{+}$, and $u(x)$ is a continuous (pluriharmonic) function.
(e) there is an open neighborhood $U^{-}$of $p_{-}$in $X(\mathbf{C})$ such that $f^{-1}\left(U^{-}\right) \Subset U^{-}$and $U^{-}$ is contained in the basin of attraction of $p_{-}$for the backward dynamics; there is an open neighborhood $U^{+}$of $p_{+}$with similar properties for $f$ instead of $f^{-1}$;
(f) If $q$ is a saddle periodic point, its stable manifold $W^{s}(q)$ is contained in $K^{+}(f)$; in fact, the proof of Proposition 5.1 in [BS91b] shows that $W^{s}(q)$ is contained in the boundary of $K^{+}(f)$;
(g) $f$ does not preserve any algebraic curve $C_{0} \subset \mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C})$.
In particular, if $S$ is a closed positive current supported by $\overline{K(f)}$, then its support does not intersect the open set $U^{-}$.
8.2. Rigidity of $\overline{K^{+}(f)}$ and equidistribution of stable manifolds. The properties (a) to (g) are sufficient to apply the arguments of Sections 4, 5, 6 of [DS13]. More precisely, one first obtains Theorem 6.6 of [DS13], because its proof relies only on the above properties and general results concerning closed positive currents (in particular Corollary 3.13 of [DS13]). ${ }^{2}$

[^0]
[^0]:    ${ }^{2}$ The only changes in this proof are that (1) $\mathbf{P}^{2}(\mathbf{C})$ should be replaced by $X(\mathbf{C})$ and the line at infinity by $X \backslash \mathcal{M}_{D}$; and (2) the function $\log \left(1+\|z\|^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}$ should be replaced by a smooth Green function associated to the $\mathbf{R}$-divisor $\theta_{f, X}^{+}$.

Then, one gets directly the following fact (which corresponds to a weak version of Theorem 6.5 of [DS13], with the same proof):

Theorem 8.2. The set $\overline{K^{+}(f)}$ (resp. $\overline{K^{-}(f)}$ ) supports a unique closed positive current, namely $T_{f}^{+}=d d^{c} G_{f}^{+}$(resp. $T_{f}^{-}$) up to multiplication by a positive constant.

Consequently, we get the following result: Given any algebraic curve $C_{0} \subset \mathcal{M}_{D}$, the sequence of currents $\lambda_{1}(f)^{-n}\left\{\left(f^{n}\right)^{*} C_{0}\right\}$ converges towards a positive multiple of $T_{f}^{+}$as $n$ goes to $+\infty$ (see Corollary 6.7 of [DS13]). Thus, $T_{f}^{+}$can be approximated by a sequence of currents of integration on algebraic curves of a fixed genus (properly renormalized); in this context, one can apply the theory of strongly approximable laminar currents, as developed by Dujardin (see [Can14, Duj04, Duj05] for an introduction and §8.3).

This rigidity results provides automatic equidistribution theorems for $(1,1)$ positive currents. We shall need the following specific application.

If $q$ is a saddle periodic point of $f$, then its stable manifold $W^{s}(q)$ is biholomorphic to the complex line ${ }^{3}$. Denote by $\xi: \mathbf{C} \rightarrow W^{s}(q) \subset \mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C})$ a one to one holomorphic parametrization of $W^{s}(q) ; \xi$ is an entire holomorphic curve. To such a curve, one can associate a family of currents of mass 1, constructed as follows. One fixes a Kähler form $\kappa$ on $X(\mathbf{C})$ and one measures lengths, areas and volumes with respect to this form. For instance, if $\mathbb{D}_{r} \subset \mathbf{C}$ is the disk of radius $r$ centered at the origin, then

$$
\operatorname{Area}\left(\xi\left(\mathbb{D}_{r}\right)\right)=\int_{\xi\left(\mathbb{D}_{r}\right)} \kappa=\int_{\mathbb{D}_{r}} \xi^{*} \kappa
$$

is the area of the image of $\mathbb{D}_{r}$ by $\xi$. Averaging with respect to $d r / r$, one introduces the function

$$
N(R)=\int_{t=0}^{R} \operatorname{Area}\left(\xi\left(\mathbb{D}_{t}\right)\right) \frac{d t}{t}
$$

Now, for each disk $\mathbb{D}_{r}$, one can consider the current of integration over $\xi\left(\mathbb{D}_{r}\right)$ : to a smooth form $\alpha$ of type $(1,1)$, this current $\left\{\xi\left(\mathbb{D}_{r}\right)\right\}$ associates the number

$$
\left\langle\left\{\xi\left(\mathbb{D}_{r}\right)\right\}\right| \alpha\right\rangle=\int_{\xi\left(\mathbb{D}_{r}\right)} \alpha=\int_{\mathbb{D}_{r}} \xi^{*} \alpha
$$

[^0]
[^0]:    ${ }^{3}$ Indeed, it is a Riemann surface, it is homeomorphic to $\mathbf{R}^{2}$, and $f$ acts on it as a contraction fixing $q$, so $W^{s}(q)$ cannot be a disk and Riemann uniformization theorem says that it is a copy of $\mathbf{C}$

Taking averages with respect to the weight $d r / r$ one obtains the following family of currents, parametrized by a radius $R>0$ :

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\langle N_{\xi}(R) \mid \alpha\right\rangle & =\frac{1}{N(R)} \int_{t=0}^{R}\left\langle\left\{\xi\left(\mathbb{D}_{r}\right)\right\} \mid \alpha\right\rangle \frac{d t}{t} \\
& =\frac{1}{N(R)} \int_{t=0}^{R} \int_{\xi\left(\mathbb{D}_{R}\right)} \alpha \frac{d t}{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

The normalization by $1 / N(R)$ assures that the mass $\left\langle N_{\xi}(R) \mid \kappa\right\rangle$ is equal to 1 for every $R>0$. From an inequality of Ahlfors, and from the compactness of the space of positive currents of mass 1 , there are sequences of radii $\left(R_{n}\right)$ such that $N_{\xi}\left(R_{n}\right)$ converges to a closed positive current $S$. A priori, such a closed positive current $S$ depends on the choice of the sequence $R_{n}$; if there is a unique closed positive current $S$ that can be obtained as such a limit, one says that there is a unique Ahlfors-Nevanlinna current (namely $S$ ) associated to $\xi$.
Corollary 8.3 (Proposition 4.10, Corollary 4.11 [DS13]). Let q be a saddle periodic point of $f$. Let $\xi: \mathbf{C} \rightarrow \mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C})$ be a holomorphic parametrization of the stable manifold of $f$. Then, there is a unique Ahlfors-Nevanlinna current associated to $\xi$, and this current is equal to $T_{f}^{+}$.
8.3. Laminarity, Pesin theory and consequence. The measure $\mu_{f}=T_{f}^{+} \wedge T_{f}^{-}$is an ergodic measure of positive (and maximal) entropy for $f$, and tools from Pesin theory can be used to describe the dynamics of $f$ with respect to this measure. In particular, in our setting, one can apply the work of Bedford, Lyubich, and Smillie in [BLS93] or the work of Dujardin in [Duj04].
Definition 8.4. (i) A family of disjoint horizontal graphs $\Gamma$ in $\mathbb{D} \times \mathbb{D}$ is called a flow box. If it is equipped with a measure $\lambda$ on $\{0\} \times \mathbb{D}$ we call it a measured flow box. It defines a closed positive current $T_{\Gamma, \lambda}$ in $\mathbb{D} \times \mathbb{D}$ defined by

$$
\left\langle T_{\Gamma, \lambda}, \alpha\right\rangle=\int_{a \in \mathbb{D}} \int_{\Gamma_{a}} \alpha \mathrm{~d} \lambda(a)
$$

(ii) A current $T$ is uniformly laminar if for every $x \in \operatorname{Supp} T$, there exist an open subset $V \ni x$ such that $V$ is biholomorphic to a bidisk and a measured flow box $(\Gamma, \lambda)$ in $V$ such that $T_{\mid V}=T_{\Gamma, \lambda}$.
(iii) A current is laminar if there exists a family of disjoint measured flow boxes $\left(\Gamma_{i}, \lambda_{i}\right)$ such that

$$
T=\sum_{i} T_{\Gamma_{i}, \lambda_{i}}
$$

(iv) A current is strongly approximable if it is the weak limit of a sequence of integration currents $\frac{1}{d_{n}}\left[C_{n}\right]$ such that

$$
\operatorname{genus}\left(C_{n}\right)+\sum_{p \in \operatorname{Sing}\left(C_{n}\right)} n_{p}\left(C_{n}\right)=O\left(d_{n}\right)
$$

(v) A current is diffuse if it does not charge algebraic curves.

The main result of [Duj05] is that if $X$ is a projective rational surface and $T$ is a strongly approximable diffuse current on $X$, then $T$ is laminar and for every flow box $\Gamma, T_{\mid \Gamma}$ is uniformly laminar. The discussion after Theorem 8.2 shows that $T_{f}^{+}$and $T_{f}^{-}$are strongly approximable currents. They are also diffuse by Proposition 6.3 of [DF01].
Definition 8.5. If $S_{1}, S_{2}$ are two uniformly laminar diffuse currents with a representation

$$
S_{i}=\int_{A_{i}}\left[D_{a, i}\right] d \mu_{i}(a)
$$

then we define the geometric intersection of $S_{1}, S_{2}$ as

$$
S_{1} \bigwedge S_{2}:=\int_{A_{1}} \int_{A_{2}}\left[D_{a, 1} \cap D_{b, 2}\right] d \mu_{1}(a) \otimes d \mu_{2}(b)
$$

where $\left[D_{a, 1} \cap D_{b, 2}\right]$ is the sum of Dirac masses at the intersection point if the intersection is finite and 0 otherwise. We extend the definition of geometric intersection to sums of uniformly laminar currents by taking geometric intersection with respect to each term of the sum. We say that a product is geometric if $S_{1} \wedge S_{2}=S_{1} \bigwedge S_{2}$.
Definition 8.6. A Pesin box is a pair $(U, K)$ where $U$ is an open subset isomorphic to a bidisk $\mathbb{D} \times \mathbb{D}$ and a compact $K \subset U$ of positive $\mu_{f}$-measure such that
(i) Every point in $K$ is a hyperbolic point of $f$.
(ii) The local stable and unstable manifolds of the points of $K$ are vertical and horizontal graphs in $U$.
(iii) For all pair of distinct points $(x, y) \in K^{2}, W_{\text {loc }}^{s}(x) \cap W_{\text {loc }}^{u}(y)$ is a singleton contained in $K$.

In particular, the local stable and unstable manifolds define a lamination $K^{s}$ and $K^{u}$ in $U$. By the main theorem of [Duj05], $T_{f \mid K^{s}}^{+}$is uniformly laminar so there exists a transverse measure $\lambda_{K}^{+}$such that

$$
T_{f \mid K^{s}}^{+}=T_{K^{s}, \lambda_{K}^{+}}
$$

Theorem 8.7 (Theorem 1, Theorem 5.2 of [Duj04]). The currents $T_{f}^{+}, T_{f}^{-}$are diffuse strongly approximable and therefore laminar and the laminar structure is compatible with Pesin theory. The current $T_{f}^{+}$is equal to

$$
T_{f}^{+}=\sum_{(U, K)} T_{K^{s}, \lambda_{K}^{+}}
$$

and since the potentials of $T_{f}^{ \pm}$are continuous, the product $T_{f}^{+} \wedge T_{f}^{-}$is geometric. Thus, the measure $\mu_{f}$ has a product structure with respect to the laminations induced by the local stable and unstable manifolds.

Proof of Theorem 8.1. We apply the following argument taken from Section 9 of [BLS93] and [Duj04] §5.2. Pick a saddle periodic point $q$ of $f$, take a small neighborhood $W$ of $q$, and consider its stable manifold, parametrized by $\xi: \mathbf{C} \rightarrow W^{s}(q)$. Take a Pesin box $(U, K)$. Since the Ahlfors-Nevanlinna current of $\xi$ coincides with $T_{f}^{+}$, each disk of $K^{s}$ is a limit of disks $\xi\left(D_{i}\right)$, for some topological disks $D_{i} \subset \mathbf{C}$. Now, $\mu_{f}(K)>0$ and by the product structure we have

$$
\mu_{f \mid K}=T_{f \mid K^{s}}^{+} \bigwedge^{1} T_{f \mid K^{u}}^{-}
$$

Thus $T_{f}^{+}$and $T_{f}^{-}$give mass to $K^{s}$ and $K^{u}$ respectively. Since the laminations $K^{u}$ and $K^{s}$ intersect transversaly, one finds a disk $\xi\left(D_{i}\right)$ that intersects $K^{u}$ transversaly. Then, if one applies $f^{N}$ with $N$ large, the preimages of $\xi\left(D_{i}\right) \cap K^{u}$ approach the point $q$, and the Inclination lemma (or Lambda lemma see [PDM82] §7.1) implies that the images of the leaves of $K^{u}$ are (very large) disks which, in the neighborhood $W$ of $q$, converge towards $W^{u}(q)$ (in the $C^{1}$ topology). We thus obtain a sequence of uniformly laminar currents

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{f}^{n}}\left(f^{n}\right)_{*}\left(T_{f \mid K^{u}}^{-}\right)_{\mid W} \leqslant T_{f}^{-}
$$

Doing the same with the unstable manifold $W^{u}(q)$ and the dynamics of $f^{-N}$, one pulls back $K^{s}$ near $q$. Thus we get the sequence of measures

$$
\mu_{n}:=\frac{1}{\lambda_{f}^{2 n}}\left(\left(f^{n}\right)^{*} T_{f \mid K^{s}}^{+}\right)_{\mid W} \bigwedge^{1}\left(\left(f^{n}\right)_{*} T_{f \mid K^{u}}^{-}\right)_{\mid W} \leqslant \mu_{f}
$$

that gives mass to $W$. Since this work for any neighborhood of $q$, this point is in the support of $\mu_{f}$ and the closure of the saddle periodic points are contained in the support of $\mu_{f}$. Thus, Theorem 8.1 is proven.

# 9. Proof of Theorem A 

The proof of Theorem A relies on the following proposition.
Proposition 9.1. Let $f \in \operatorname{Aut}\left(\mathcal{M}_{D}\right)$ be a loxodromic automorphism with $D=0$ or $D=2-$ $2 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{q}\right)$ and let $v$ be an archimedean place. Then, $f$ admits a periodic saddle fixed point $q(f) \in \mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C})$ such that
(1) $q(f) \in \operatorname{Supp}\left(\mu_{f, v}\right)$
(2) If $g \in \operatorname{Aut}\left(\mathcal{M}_{D}\right)$ is loxodromic such that $f$ and $g$ do not share a common iterate, then $\left(g^{n}(q(f))\right)$ is unbounded.
Item (1) follows from Theorem 8.1.
Assuming the proposition, suppose that $f, g$ share a Zariski dense subset of periodic points, we can suppose that they share a generic sequence of periodic points by Lemma 7.1. Then

by Theorem 7.3 we have equality of the equilibrium measures of $f$ and $g$ at every place so in particular at every archimedean place. Fix $v$ one of them. Suppose that $f$ and $g$ do not share a common iterate, then $\left(g^{n}(q(f))\right)_{n}$ is unbounded. Let $\mu=\mu_{f, v}=\mu_{g, v}$. Since $\operatorname{Supp} \mu=\operatorname{Supp} \mu_{f, v}=\operatorname{Supp} \mu_{g, v}$, we have that $\operatorname{Supp} \mu$ is a compact subset of $\mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C})$ invariant by $f$ and $g$. Since $q(f) \in \operatorname{Supp} \mu_{f, v}=\operatorname{Supp} \mu$ we get that $\left(g^{n}(q(f))\right) \subset \operatorname{Supp} \mu$ which is a contradiction.
9.1. Construction of the saddle fixed point $q(f)$. Suppose first that $D=0$. Up to taking an iterate of $f$ we can suppose that there exists a loxodromic element $\Phi_{f} \in \mathrm{SL}_{2}(\mathbf{Z})$ such that $f=f_{\Phi_{f}}$. Denote by $p(f)=p\left(f_{\Phi_{f}}\right)$ and $q(f)=q\left(f_{\Phi_{f}}\right)$ the fixed points constructed using Minsky theorem. These two fixed point are saddle fixed points by [McM96] Corollary 3.19. The fixed point $q(f)$ corresponds to a representation $\rho_{\infty}: F_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{PSL}_{2}(\mathbf{C})$, one can show that $\rho_{\infty}$ also satisfies Theorem 4.4 even though the punctured torus is not compact. One can also show that for any automorphism $g$ of $\mathcal{M}_{0}$ the differential of $g$ at $(0,0,0)$ has order 1 or 2 , thus $p(f), q(f) \neq(0,0,0)$ and it is a smooth point of $\mathcal{M}_{0}$.

Suppose now that $D=2-2 \cos \frac{\pi}{q}$. Following $\S 4.5$, for any $\Phi_{f} \in \mathrm{SL}_{2}(\mathbf{Z})$ we define $q(f)=$ $\rho_{\infty}\left(\Phi_{f}\right) \in \mathcal{M}_{D}$.
9.2. The sequence $\left(g^{n}(q(f))\right)$ is unbounded. Suppose $D=0$ we can consider $S$ as the flat torus $T=\mathbf{R}^{2} / \mathbf{Z}^{2}$ with a puncture at the origin, i.e. $S=T \backslash\{0\}$, or as a complete hyperbolic surface $X$ of finite area (we fix such a hyperbolic structure, it corresponds to some point $X$ in the Teichmüller space $\operatorname{Teich}(S) \simeq \mathbb{D})$.

We refer to $\S 1.4$ and $\S 1.5$ of [Ota96] for the defintions of measured laminations and the classifications of elements in $\operatorname{Mod}(S)$ for $S$ a real compact surface with negative Euler characteristic. An element $f$ of $\mathrm{Out}^{+}\left(F_{2}\right)$ is pseudo-Anosov if the corresponding matrix $A_{f} \in \mathrm{SL}_{2}(\mathbf{Z})$ has $\operatorname{Tr}\left(A_{f}\right)^{2}>4$. In that case, the matrix has two eigenvalues $\lambda(f)>1$ and $1 / \lambda(f)<1$ and the mapping class is represented by a linear automorphism of the torus $T$ (fixing the origin $o$ ) with stable and unstable linear foliations. In the hyperbolic surface $X$, these foliations give rise to two measured laminations $F_{-}$and $F_{+}$(by geodesic lines). If $C \subset S$ is a closed curve (represented by some geodesic in $X$ ), one can define two intersection numbers $i\left(C, F_{+}\right)$and $i\left(C, F_{-}\right)$; they depend only on the free homotopy class of $C$. The product $j(C)=i\left(C, F_{+}\right) i\left(C, F_{-}\right)$is $f$-invariant, because $f$ stretches $F_{+}$by a dilatation factor $\lambda(f)>1$, and contracts $F_{-}$by $1 / \lambda(f)$; if $C$ is not homotopic to a loop around the puncture $j(C)$ is strictly positive (any closed geodesic is transverse to $F_{+}$and $F_{-}$).

If $D=2-2 \cos (\pi / q)$, let $S$ be the genus one torus with an orbifold singularity of order $q$. We have seen that there exists a characteristic finite covering $\tilde{S} \rightarrow S$ with $\tilde{S}$ a compact surface of negative Euler characteristic. We let $X=\mathbb{H}^{2} / \Gamma$ be a hyperbolic surface homeomorphic to $\tilde{S}$ (i.e $X \in \operatorname{Teich}(\tilde{S})$ ). If $f \in \mathrm{Out}^{+}\left(F_{2}\right)$ is pseudo-Anosov then it lifts to a pseudo-Anosov $\tilde{f} \in$ $\operatorname{Mod}(X)=\operatorname{Out}^{+}\left(F_{2}\right)$ pseudo-Anosov also. In that case, there exist two measured laminations $F_{+}$and $F_{-}$over $\tilde{S}$ (the stable and the unstable one) Proposition 1.5.1 of [Ota96]. We have

that for any geodesic $\gamma \in \bar{S}$,

$$
\frac{(\bar{f})_{*}^{ \pm k} \gamma}{\ell\left((\bar{f})_{*}^{ \pm k} \gamma\right)} \xrightarrow[k \rightarrow+\infty]{ } F_{ \pm}
$$

in the sense of measured laminations. (This also holds in the case $D=0$ ). Here $\ell$ is the length induced by the hyperbolic structure from the quotient $\mathbb{H}^{2} / \Gamma$ so $\ell\left((\bar{f})_{*}^{ \pm k} \gamma\right)$ grows like $\lambda(\bar{f})^{k}$. We also have that $j(\gamma)=i\left(\gamma, F_{+}\right) i\left(\gamma, F_{-}\right)$is $f$-invariant as $i\left(\tilde{f}_{*}(\gamma), F_{ \pm}\right)=\lambda(\bar{f})^{\mp 1} i\left(\gamma, F_{ \pm}\right)$and if $\gamma$ is a geodesic, then $j(\gamma)>0$. To unify the notations we will still denote by $f$ the lift $\bar{f}$ of $f$ to $X$.

Lemma 9.2. If $f$ and $g$ are two loxodromic elements of $\mathrm{Out}^{+}\left(F_{2}\right) \simeq \mathrm{SL}_{2}(\mathbf{Z})$ generating a non-elementary subgroup of $\mathrm{SL}_{2}(\mathbf{Z})$, then given any geodesic $\gamma \subset X, j\left(g^{n}(\gamma)\right)$ goes to $+\infty$ as $n$ goes to $+\infty$.

Proof. Let $G_{+}$and $G_{-}$be the unstable and stable laminations associated to $g$ in $X$. Since $f$ and $g$ generate a non-elementary subgroup of $G L_{2}(\mathbf{Z}), G_{+}$is transverse to both $F_{+}$and $F_{-}$(equivalently, the four fixed points of $A_{f}$ and $A_{g}$ on $\mathbf{P}^{1}(\mathbf{R})$ are distinct). Thus, by Equation (174) $j\left(g^{n}(C)\right) \simeq \lambda(g)^{n} i\left(G_{+}, F_{+}\right) i\left(G_{-}, F_{-}\right)$by continuity of the intersection number (see [Ota96] p.151).

Lemma 9.3. Let $f$ and $g$ be two loxodromic elements of $\mathrm{Out}^{+}\left(F_{2}\right) \simeq \mathrm{SL}_{2}(\mathbf{Z})$ generating a non-elementary subgroup of $\mathrm{SL}_{2}(\mathbf{Z})$. Let $\gamma \subset X$ be a geodesic, and let $[\gamma]$ be its free homotopy class. Then the sequence $g^{n}[\gamma]$ intersects each orbit of $f$ only finitely many times.

Proof. This follows from the previous lemma and the fact that $j(\cdot)$ is $f$-invariant so it is constant in each orbit of $f$.

Recall the definition of $M_{\Phi_{f}}, \bar{M}_{\Phi_{f}}, \rho_{\infty}$ and $\alpha_{f}$ from Theorem 4.4 (here we consider $f \in$ $\operatorname{Mod}(\bar{S})$ if we are in the orbifold case). In $M_{\Phi_{f}}$, the number of simple closed geodesics of length $\leqslant L$ is finite (for every $L>0$ ); thus, in $\bar{M}_{\Phi_{f}}$, given any upper bound $L$, there are only finitely many homotopy classes of simple closed curves up to the action of $f^{\mathbf{Z}}$ (Note that, since $\alpha_{f}$ acts by isometry, each closed geodesic $C \subset \bar{M}_{f}$ gives rise to infinitely many geodesics $\alpha_{f}^{n}(C)$ with the exact same length).
Proof of Proposition 9.1 (2). Fix a generator $a$ in $\pi_{1}(S)$ where $S$ is either the punctured torus or the genus 1 torus with an orbifold singularity of index $q$. Set $k$ to be the index of $\pi_{1}(\bar{S})$ in $\pi_{1}(S)$. and $k=1$ otherwise. The element $a^{k}$ gives rise to a closed geodesic $A$ in $\bar{M}_{\Phi_{f}}$. From these preliminaries and the previous lemma, the sequence of homotopy classes $g^{n}\left(a^{k}\right)$ corresponds to a sequence of closed geodesics in $\bar{M}_{\Phi_{f}}$, with length going to infinity because $f$ acts by isometry on $\bar{M}_{\Phi_{f}}$.

Now, $g^{n}\left(a^{k}\right)$ corresponds to a (conjugacy class of a) matrix $\rho_{\infty}\left(g^{n}\left(a^{k}\right)\right) \in \mathrm{SL}_{2}(\mathbf{C})$, and the trace of this matrix is related to the length of the geodesic by a simple formula; in particular, the fact that the length goes to infinity implies that the modulus of the trace goes to $+\infty$. Since

for any matrix $A \in \mathrm{SL}_{2}(\mathbf{C}), \operatorname{Tr} A^{k}$ is a polynomial in $\operatorname{Tr} A$ we get that $\operatorname{Tr}\left(\rho_{\infty}\left(g^{n}(a)\right)\right)$ goes to infinity. This implies that the orbit of $q(f)$ under the action of $g$ on $\mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C})$ is discrete, going to infinity.

# 10. FOR A TRANSCENDENTAL PARAMETER $D$ 

We finish this paper by proving Theorem B which we restate.
Theorem 10.1. Let $D \in \mathbf{C}$ be transcendental and let $f, g \in \operatorname{Aut}\left(\mathcal{M}_{D}\right)$ be loxodromic automorphisms. The following assertions are equivalent:
(1) $\operatorname{Per}(f)=\operatorname{Per}(g)$.
(2) $\exists N, M \in \mathbf{Z} \backslash\{0\}, f^{N}=g^{M}$.

Proof. We can suppose that $f, g \in \mathrm{SL}_{2}(\mathbf{Z})$, for any parameter $w \in \mathbf{C}$, we denote by $f_{w}$ the automorphism induced by $f \in \mathrm{SL}_{2}(\mathbf{Z})$ over $\mathcal{M}_{w}$. For the parameter $w=0$, we have constructed a hyperbolic fixed point $q(f) \in \mathcal{M}_{0}(\mathbf{C})$. Because $q(f) \neq(0,0,0)$, it a smooth point of $\mathcal{M}_{0}(\mathbf{C})$ and we can find local analytic coordinates $u, v, w$ at $q(f) \in \mathbf{C}^{3}$ with $w=\kappa+2$ (recall the notations from the introduction) such that $f_{w}$ is locally of the form

$$
f_{w}(u, v, w)=\left(\lambda u, \frac{1}{\lambda} v, w\right)
$$

where $\lambda, \frac{1}{\lambda}$ are the two eigenvalues of the differential of $f_{0}$ at $q(f)$. By the analytic implicit function theorem, there exists $\varepsilon>0$ a local analytic curve $c_{\varepsilon}: w \in \mathbb{D}(0, \varepsilon) \mapsto c_{\varepsilon}(w) \in \mathcal{M}_{w}$ such that $c_{\varepsilon}(0)=q(f)$ and $f_{w}\left(c_{\varepsilon}(w)\right)=c_{\varepsilon}(w)$. Now, if $f, g$ do not share a common iterate, then the orbit of $q(f)$ under $g_{0}$ is unbounded by Proposition 9.1. Thus, for all $k \in \mathbf{Z}$, we have $g_{0}^{k}(q(f)) \neq q(f)$. We show the following.
Lemma 10.2. If $D \in \mathbf{C}$ is transcendental, then for all $k \in \mathbf{Z}_{\geqslant 0}$, there exists $p \in \mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C})$ such that

$$
f_{D}(p)=p \text { and } g_{D}^{\ell}(p) \neq p, \forall 1 \leqslant \ell \leqslant k
$$

Using the lemma, we can conclude because $f_{D}$ admits a finite number of fixed points since $f_{D}$ does not admit an invariant curve, thus we must have $\operatorname{Per}\left(f_{D}\right) \neq \operatorname{Per}\left(g_{D}\right)$.

Proof of the lemma. Notice that this statement does not depend on the transcendental parameter $D$. Indeed, let $D^{\prime}$ be another transcendental parameter, then there exists a Galois automorphism $\sigma \in \operatorname{Gal}(\mathbf{C} / \overline{\mathbf{Q}})$ that exchange $D$ and $D^{\prime}$. Since the family of surfaces $\mathcal{M}_{D}$ gives a foliation of $\mathbf{C}^{3}$ we can view a point $p \in \mathcal{M}_{D}(\mathbf{C})$ as a point in $\mathbf{C}^{3}$ and apply $\sigma$ to each coordinates, we denote by $p^{\sigma} \in \mathbf{C}^{3}$ the new obtained point. We apply $\sigma$ to (176) to get

$$
f_{\sigma(D)}\left(p^{\sigma}\right)=p^{\sigma} \text { and } g_{\sigma(D)}^{\ell}\left(p^{\sigma}\right) \neq p^{\sigma}, \forall 1 \leqslant \ell \leqslant k
$$

Now, fix $k \geqslant 1$. For any transcendental parameter $t$ small enough we have

$$
f_{t}\left(c_{\varepsilon}(t)\right)=c_{\varepsilon}(t)
$$

by construction of the curve $c_{\varepsilon}$ and

$$
\forall 1 \leqslant \ell \leqslant k, g_{t}^{k}\left(c_{\varepsilon}(t)\right) \neq c_{\varepsilon}(t)
$$

by continuity since we have $g_{0}^{m}(q(f)) \neq q(f)$ for all $m \in \mathbf{Z}$. Thus, the lemma is shown.
