# 2 Leave-Two-Out Placebo Test 

Consider a design-based setup similar to that of the placebo test. We observe a panel $Y_{i t}$ of $N$ units, $T$ time periods, where the $I$ th unit is treated from time $T_{0}+1, \ldots, T$. In potential outcomes, the data is given as:

$$
\left[\begin{array}{cccccccc}
Y_{1,1}(0) & Y_{1,2}(0) & \ldots & Y_{1, T_{0}+1}(0) & \ldots & Y_{1, T-1}(0) & Y_{1, T}(0) \\
Y_{2,1}(0) & Y_{2,2}(0) & \ldots & Y_{2, T_{0}+1}(0) & \ldots & Y_{2, T-1}(0) & Y_{2, T}(0) \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\
Y_{I, 1}(0) & Y_{I, 2}(0) & \ldots & Y_{I, T_{0}+1}(1) & \ldots & Y_{I, T-1}(1) & Y_{I, T}(1) \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\
Y_{N, 1}(0) & Y_{N, 2}(0) & \ldots & Y_{N, T_{0}+1}(0) & \ldots & Y_{N, T-1}(0) & Y_{N, T}(0)
\end{array}\right]
$$

We assume that the treatment index $I$ is uniformly assigned across the $N$ units. Synthetic control methods estimate the average treatment effect on the treated, $\tau_{I, t}=Y_{I, t}(1)-Y_{I, t}(0)$, by using the synthetic control $\hat{Y}_{I, t}$ to estimate the control counterfactual. Following the literature [ADH10, ADH15, FP18], the primary inference task of interest is to test the sharp null hypothesis

$$
H_{0}: Y_{i, t}(1)=Y_{i, t}(0), \quad \forall i, \forall t \geq T_{0}+1
$$

Extensions to sharp nulls of the form $H_{0}: Y_{i, t}(1)=Y_{i, t}(0)+\tau_{i, t}$ for known quantities $\tau_{i, t}, i=1, \ldots, N$, and $t \geq T_{0}+1$ can also be handled.

### 2.1 Naive LTO Placebo Test

Our LTO placebo test procedure works as follows:

1. For every pair of distinct units $(i, j)$ from $1, \ldots, N$ and unequal to $I$, create the synthetic control with control units $1, \ldots, N$ excluding points $i, j, I$ to obtain $\hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{k}^{-(i, j, I)}, k \in\{i, j, I\}$, the vector of synthetic control outcomes for unit $k$ using controls $[N] \backslash\{i, j, I\}$. For some statistic $S(\cdot, \cdot)$, define the residual

$$
R_{i, j, I ; k}=\left|S\left(\mathbf{Y}_{k}, \hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{k}^{-(i, j, I)}\right)\right|
$$

where $k \in\{i, j, I\}$ and $\mathbf{Y}_{k}$ is the observed data for unit $k$.
A common choice is the post-period to pre-period Ratio of Mean Square Prediction Error (RMSPE) statistic proposed in [ADH15]:

$$
S_{\text {ratio-RMSPE }}\left(\mathbf{Y}_{k}, \hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{k}\right)=\frac{S_{\text {post-RMSPE }}\left(\mathbf{Y}_{k}, \hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{k}\right)}{S_{\text {pre-RMSPE }}\left(\mathbf{Y}_{k}, \hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{k}\right)}=\frac{\sum_{t=T_{0}+1}^{T}\left(Y_{k, t}-\hat{Y}_{k, t}\right)^{2} /\left(T-T_{0}\right)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T_{0}}\left(Y_{k, t}-\hat{Y}_{k, t}\right)^{2} / T_{0}}
$$

2. For every pair of indices in the first step, compute the Leave-Two-Out residual

$$
R_{i, j, I}^{\mathrm{LTO}}:=\max \left(R_{i, j, I ; i}, R_{i, j, I ; j}\right)
$$

Denote the event that $I$ 's residual is larger than that of $i, j$ by $\mathbb{I}\{I>i, j\}$, so that

$$
\mathbb{I}\{I>i, j\}:=\mathbf{1}\left\{R_{i, j, I ; I}>R_{i, j}^{\mathrm{LTO}}\right\}
$$

3. Compute the quantity

$$
p_{\text {naive-LTO }}:=\frac{1}{(N-1)(N-2)} \sum_{\substack{i, j \in[N] \backslash I \\ i \neq j}} \mathbb{I}\{I \neq i, j\}
$$

which functions as an approximate $p$-value under the null hypothesis.
Remark 2.1. Thinking of the LTO placebo test p-value in terms of tournaments is useful. Consider a tournament between all of the $N$ units, where matches are held for every triple of units. A unit $i$ wins a match amongst units $i, j, k$ if its residual $R_{i, j, k ; i}$ is the largest. Then $p_{\text {naive-LTO }}$ after renormalization counts the number of matches in which unit I did not win.

By approximate $p$-value, we mean that the LTO $p$-value satisfies the following Type-I error guarantee.

Theorem 2.1. Let $N \geq 3$. Then the following results hold.
(a) When $\alpha \leq \frac{1}{N-1}, \mathbb{P}_{H_{0}}\left(p_{\text {naive-LTO }} \leq \alpha\right) \leq \frac{1}{N}$.
(b) When $N>6$,

$$
\mathbb{P}_{H_{0}}\left(p_{\text {naive-LTO }} \leq \alpha\right) \leq \frac{\lfloor N \alpha\rfloor+1}{N}, \quad \text { for any } N \alpha<4\left(1-\frac{2}{(N-1)(N-2)}\right)
$$

(c) For any integer $k$ such that $\frac{k}{N-1}\left(\frac{N}{N-2} \frac{k-1}{3}-1\right)<1$,

$$
\mathbb{P}_{H_{0}}\left(p_{\text {naive-LTO }} \leq \alpha\right) \leq \frac{k}{N}, \quad \text { for any } N \alpha \in\left(k-1, k-\frac{k}{N-1}\left[\frac{N}{N-2} \frac{k-1}{3}-1\right]\right) \text { and } \alpha<2 / 3
$$

The proof of Theorem 2.1 is presented in Appendix B.2. It is a (non-trivial) consequence of the following more general result. We sketch its proof at the end of the section.

Theorem 2.2. Under the assumption of uniform treatment, for any $\alpha<2 / 3$,

$$
\mathbb{P}_{H_{0}}\left(p_{\text {naive-LTO }} \leq \alpha\right) \leq \frac{|N f(N, \alpha)|}{N}
$$

where

$$
f(N, \alpha):=\frac{3-\frac{3}{N}-\sqrt{9\left(1-\frac{1}{N}\right)^{2}-12\left(-\frac{4}{3 N^{2}}+\frac{1}{N}+\alpha\left(1-\frac{1}{N}\right)\left(1-\frac{2}{N}\right)\right)}}{2}
$$

Theorem 2.1 guarantees that $p_{\text {naive-LTO }}$ never has worse conditional Type I error than $p_{\text {app-placebo }}$, for a wide range of parameters. Specifically, by Theorem 2.1 (b), when $\alpha=0.05$, it implies that the LTO $p$-value has the same theoretical guarantee with the placebo $p$-value for any $N \leq 79$. This covers a wide range of settings where the synthetic control method is applied; see Appendix A. By Theorem 2.1 (c), we can easily check if the naive LTO placebo test has the same Type-I error guarantee as the approximate placebo test for any $N$ and $\alpha$. In particular, for all $6<N<200$, this equivalence holds for $\alpha \in\{0.01,0.02\}$. For $\alpha=0.05$, it holds except for $N \in\{80,100,120,139,140,159,160,179,180,198,199\}$. To further gain some insight for larger $\alpha$, we plot the Type-I error bound in Theorem 2.2 for $N=17$ in Figure 3. The LTO p-value offers guarantees that are comparable to or slightly stronger than those of the approximate placebo test for all practical levels, i.e., when $\alpha \leq 0.25$. Lastly, when $N$ is large, the bound is close to $\frac{3-\sqrt{9-12 \alpha}}{2}$, which is very close to $\alpha$ when $\alpha$ is small. For $\alpha=0.05$, the right hand side is around 0.0508 . For $\alpha=0.1$, the bound comes out to 0.104 .

On the other hand, as suggested by our empirical results in Section 4, the actual Type-I error of the LTO $p$-value can be strictly lower than the bound while the bound for the placebo $p$-value is tight. Combined with the improved granularity and improved power documented in Sections 4.1 and 6.3, $p_{\text {LTO }}$ provides a compelling alternative to $p_{\text {app-placebo }}$ for randomization inference.

Proof Sketch of Theorem 2.2. The proof of the coverage guarantee relies on analyzing combinatorial structures related to tournaments. We present the proof here in order to introduce the main ideas of the analysis; it is an interesting extension of the proof of coverage in the Jackknife+ [BCRT21]. Let us recall the notation

$$
\mathbb{I}(k>i, j):=\mathbf{1}\left\{R_{i, j, k ; k}>\max \left(R_{i, j, k ; i}, R_{i, j, k ; j}\right)\right\}
$$

Call a unit $k$ strange if

$$
\sum_{\substack{i, j \in[N] \backslash k \\ i \neq j}} \mathbb{I}(k>i, j) \geq(1-\alpha)(N-1)(N-2)
$$

Intuitively, the LTO $p$-value can be seen as the normalized rank of the unit $I$ in a tournament where matches are held between every triple of distinct units $i, j, k$. The winner of the match is the unit with the largest residual. We call a unit strange if it wins too many matches, quantified above. In such a tournament, not everyone can win too often; following this intuition, we upper bound the number of strange units.

Let $\mathcal{S}$ denote the set of strange units, and let $s:=|\mathcal{S}|$. Summing the definition of strangeness on both sides, we see that

$$
\sum_{k \in \mathcal{S}} \sum_{i, j \in[N], i \neq j} \mathbb{I}(k>i, j) \geq(1-\alpha) s(N-1)(N-2)
$$

![img-2.jpeg](img-2.jpeg)

Figure 3: The red dashed line shows the Type-I error bounds $\left\lfloor\frac{N f(N, \alpha)}{N}\right\rfloor$ in Theorem 2.2 of the Naive LTO procedure, which is a non-decreasing piecewise-constant function in $\alpha$. Here, $N=17$. The stepwise constant solid blue line is the approximate placebo guarantee, $\frac{[N \alpha]+1}{N}$.

The sum on the left hand side may be split into three terms:

$$
\overbrace{\sum_{i, j, k \in \mathcal{S}, i \neq j \neq k}^{\text {(I) }} \mathbb{I}(k>i, j)}+2 \overbrace{\sum_{i, k \in \mathcal{S}, i \neq k, j \in \mathcal{S}^{c}}}^{\text {(II) }} \mathbb{I}(k>i, j)+\overbrace{\sum_{i, j \in \mathcal{S}^{c}, i \neq j, k \in \mathcal{S}}}^{\text {(III) }} \mathbb{I}(k>i, j)
$$

where $i \neq j \neq k$ is shorthand notation for mutually distinct $i, j, k$. For the first sum, note by renaming the labels that $\sum_{i, j, k \in \mathcal{S}, i \neq j \neq k} \mathbb{I}(k>i, j)=\sum_{i, j, k \in \mathcal{S}, i \neq j \neq k} \mathbb{I}(i>j, k)=\sum_{i, j, k \in \mathcal{S}, i \neq j \neq k} \mathbb{I}(j>k, i)$. Thus

$$
\text { (I) } \leq \sum_{i, j, k \in \mathcal{S}, i \neq j \neq k} \frac{1}{3}(\mathbb{I}(k>i, j)+\mathbb{I}(i>j, k)+\mathbb{I}(j>k, i)) \leq s(s-1)(s-2) / 3
$$

Crucially, we use the fact that there can be at most one winner in a triple comparison: $\mathbb{I}(k>$ $i, j)+\mathbb{I}(i>j, k)+\mathbb{I}(j>k, i) \leq 1 .{ }^{1}$ For the second sum, swap the naming of the $i, k$ labels and use the bound $\mathbb{I}(k>i, k)+\mathbb{I}(i>k, j) \leq 1$ to see that

$$
\text { (II) } \leq 2 \sum_{i, k \in \mathcal{S}, i \neq k, j \in \mathcal{S}^{c}} \frac{1}{2}(\mathbb{I}(k>i, k)+\mathbb{I}(i>k, j)) \leq s(s-1)(N-s)
$$

Finally, in the last sum, we use the naive bound (III) $\leq s(N-s)(N-1-s)$.
Combining these bounds and working through algebra, we arrive at a quadratic inequality in the variable $\beta:=s / N$ :

$$
\frac{\beta^{2}}{3}-\beta\left(1-\frac{1}{N}\right)-\frac{4}{3 N^{2}}+\frac{1}{N}+\alpha\left(1-\frac{1}{N}\right)\left(1-\frac{2}{N}\right) \geq 0
$$

[^0]
[^0]:    ${ }^{1}$ We allow for zero winners in the triple comparison if there are ties in the residuals.

We can solve this by quadratic formula, yielding that, for any $\alpha<2 / 3$,

$$
\beta \leq f(N, \alpha)
$$

To conclude, under uniform treatment we have $\mathbb{P}\left(p_{\text {naive-LTO }} \leq \alpha\right)=\mathbb{P}(I \in \mathcal{S})=\beta$. The bound in equation (10) shows that $p_{\text {naive-LTO }}$ is an approximate $p$-value. A full proof with further computational details may be found in Appendix B.

# 2.2 Improved LTO Placebo test 

Notice that in defining the notion of strange unit, we have great flexibility in choosing the threshold. Call a unit $k$ c-strange if

$$
\sum_{\substack{i, j \in[N] \\ i \neq j} \backslash k} \mathbb{I}(k>i, j) \geq(1-\alpha-c)(N-1)(N-2)
$$

This results in a new $p$-value $p_{\text {naive-LTO }}-c$. By Theorem 2.2,

$$
\mathbb{P}\left(p_{\text {naive-LTO }}-c \leq \alpha\right)=\mathbb{P}\left(p_{\text {naive-LTO }} \leq \alpha+c\right) \leq \frac{\lfloor N f(N, \alpha+c)\rfloor}{N}
$$

As a result, for any $c>0$ such that

$$
\frac{\lfloor N f(N, \alpha+c)\rfloor}{N}=\frac{\lfloor N f(N, \alpha)\rfloor}{N}
$$

the adjusted $p$-value $p_{\text {naive-LTO }}-c$ has the same bound on the rejection probability as the naive LTO $p$-value $p_{\text {naive-LTO }}$ but is strictly more powerful than the latter, since it is strictly smaller. This motivates the improved LTO placebo $p$-value.

Theorem 2.3. Let $\delta$ be any positive number (e.g., $\delta=10^{-10}$ ). Define

$$
p_{\text {powered-LTO }}(\alpha)=p_{\text {naive-LTO }}-c(N, \alpha)+\delta
$$

where

$$
c(N, \alpha)=\min \{c: f(N, \alpha+c)=(\lfloor N f(N, \alpha)\rfloor+1) / N\}
$$

Then

$$
\mathbb{P}\left(p_{\text {powered-LTO }}(\alpha) \leq \alpha\right) \leq \frac{\lfloor N f(N, \alpha)\rfloor}{N}
$$

The power improvement is enabled by the discrete Type-I error bound. Since the improved LTO placebo test has the same Type-I error bound as the naive one, Theorem 2.1 applies to $p_{\text {powered-LTO }}(\alpha)$. As a result, $p_{\text {powered-LTO }}(\alpha)$ has the same theoretical guarantee as $p_{\text {app-placebo }}$ when $N \alpha<4(1-2 /(N-1)(N-2))$. Note that $p_{\text {powered-LTO }}(\alpha)$ depends on $\alpha$ and hence, unlike $p_{\text {naive-LTO }}$, cannot be viewed as a $p$-value. It can only be used for testing $H_{0}$ at level $\alpha$.

While the expression of $c(N, \alpha)$ is complicated, we can easily compute it by a binary search. For the Proposition 99 dataset, $N=39, c(N, 0.02)=0.006$ and $c(N, 0.05)=0.002$. For the application in [AG03], $N=17$ and $c(N, 0.05)=0.0125$.

Further power improvement by enforcing decision monotonicity. One undesirable feature of $p_{\text {powered-LTO }}(\alpha)$ is that the procedure is not decision-monotonic in $\alpha$. That is, if the test based on $p_{\text {powered-LTO }}(\alpha)$ rejects at some level $\alpha$, the test using $p_{\text {powered-LTO }}\left(\alpha^{\prime}\right)$ may not reject at an $\alpha^{\prime}$ with $\alpha^{\prime}>\alpha$. This is due to the step-like behavior of the Type-I error upper bound.

We can enforce decision monotonicity and also improve power of the procedure by considering instead

$$
I_{\alpha}=\max _{\alpha^{\prime} \leq \alpha} \mathbf{1}\left\{p_{\text {powered-LTO }}\left(\alpha^{\prime}\right) \leq \alpha^{\prime}\right\}
$$

rejecting at level $\alpha$ if $I_{\alpha}=1$. Clearly this procedure is more powerful than rejecting only when $p_{\text {powered-LTO }}(\alpha) \leq \alpha$. The procedure also controls Type-I error. To see this,

$$
\mathbb{P}\left(I_{\alpha}=1\right)=\mathbb{P}\left(\exists \alpha^{\prime}: p_{\text {powered-LTO }}\left(\alpha^{\prime}\right) \leq \alpha^{\prime}\right)=\mathbb{P}\left(\exists \alpha^{\prime}: p_{\text {naive-LTO }} \leq \alpha^{\prime}+c\left(N, \alpha^{\prime}\right)-\delta\right)
$$

The last line may be written

$$
\mathbb{P}\left(p_{\text {naive-LTO }} \leq \max _{\alpha^{\prime} \leq \alpha} \alpha^{\prime}+c\left(N, \alpha^{\prime}\right)-\delta\right)=\mathbb{P}\left(p_{\text {naive-LTO }} \leq g(\alpha)+c(N, g(\alpha))-\delta\right)
$$

where $g(\alpha)=\underset{\alpha^{\prime} \leq \alpha}{\operatorname{argmax}} \alpha^{\prime}+c\left(N, \alpha^{\prime}\right)-\delta$. The latter is equal to $\mathbb{P}\left(p_{\text {powered-LTO }}(g(\alpha)) \leq g(\alpha)\right) \leq$ $\frac{|N f(N, g(\alpha))|}{N} \leq \frac{|N f(N, \alpha)|}{N}$, which is the same Type-I error bound as using just $p_{\text {powered-LTO }}(\alpha)$. We do not use this procedure in our simulations, leaving this method for theoretical interest.

# 2.3 Related Work 

The existing literature on synthetic controls is vast: see the survey article [Aba21] for a review. Accordingly, there has been much work addressing inference for synthetic controls. Among the first inferential procedures for synthetic controls was the placebo test, first described in [AG03], and formalized by Firpo and Possebom [FP18] into a permutation test. They work in the same design-based framework where the probability of treatment is uniform. Several extensions are made, including to a non-equally weighted inference procedure which is useful for sensitivity analysis.

Other works have also considered the design-based setup and randomization or permutationbased inference. See [BISW21] for a design-based analysis of the synthetic controls procedure under a similar uniform treatment assumption, and also a random treatment time assumption. [ST21] gives a related randomization inference procedure in a staggered adoption setting where there are multiple treated units, under the additional assumption that the time at which each unit adopts the treatment that follows a Cox proportional hazards model. In general, design-based inference proves to be a powerful tool for panel data analysis [AILL21, RR20, AAIW20, AI22, RS23, Xu23].

Inference procedures have also been developed assuming underlying factor model structure and related time series assumptions on the data. [CWZ21] develop an inference procedure assuming a regularity condition on the residual process in time. The paper contains an extensive discussion on the various choices for creating the counterfactual or synthetic control. Another line of research [CFT21, CFPT22] provides prediction intervals via non-asymptotic concentration bounds, under stationarity and weak dependence time series assumptions on the data. See also [HS17, ASS18, AAH^21, AH23, Li20, CWZ18] for related asymptotic analyses of the syntheticcontrol type estimators under time series assumptions.

For many of these results, consistency of the estimator and the resulting inference procedure is shown in the limit as the number of controls and units goes to infinity. Although many of these works offer finite sample bounds, it is difficult to contextualize these results when the applications of synthetic controls are usually small $N$, small $T$ settings. Furthermore, many of these asymptotic results require simplifications on the synthetic control procedure to carry out analysis. The LTO

placebo test can use other counterfactual models beyond the standard synthetic control method, and we require no conceptual simplifications to do inference. As a result, the LTO procedure may be used with generalizations of the original synthetic control procedure such as those outlined in [DI16, ASS18, CWZ21, AAH^21, Gun23]. Furthermore, it can be applied with non-syntheticcontrol methods developed for inference with few clusters; see Section 6.4 for further discussion.

# 3 Non-Uniform Assignments
### 3.1 Weighted LTO placebo test

Outside of experimental settings, the assumption of uniform treatment is questionable. To handle non-uniform treatment assignments, we first propose a weighted analog of the LTO $p$-value. Suppose that the probability of treating unit $k$ is given by $\pi_{k}, k=1, \ldots, N$. In some applications, these probabilities can be estimated. For example, when assessing the effect of earthquakes [CGNP13, dIN15], the probability of earthquake in each region can be estimated via statistical seismology [VJBZZ05]. Then the following weighted approximate $p$-value generalizes the LTO $p$-value of Section 2.

Definition 3.1 (Weighted LTO $p$-values).

$$
p_{\mathrm{w}-\mathrm{LTO}}(\pi):=\sum_{\substack{j \neq k \\ j, k \neq I}} \frac{\pi_{j} \pi_{k}}{\left(1-\pi_{I}\right)^{2}-\sum_{l \neq I} \pi_{l}^{2}} \mathbf{I}\{I \neq j, k\}
$$

Note that $p_{\text {w-LTO }}(\pi)=p_{\text {naive-LTO }}$ when $\pi_{i}=1 / N$ for all $i$. We prove the following upper bound for the Type-I error of $p_{\text {w-LTO }}(\pi)$ as the value of an integer programming problem. The proof is deferred to Appendix B.3.

Theorem 3.1. For any $\alpha \in(0,1), \mathbb{P}_{H_{0}}\left(p_{\mathrm{w}-\mathrm{LTO}}(\pi) \leq \alpha\right)$ is upper bounded by the value of the following integer programming problem:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \max L_{1}(z) \\
& \text { s.t. } \frac{1}{3} L_{1}(z)^{3}-L_{1}(z)^{2}+L_{1}(z)\left[L_{2}(z)+\alpha\left(1-\sum_{l=1}^{N} \pi_{l}^{2}\right)\right] \geq\left(\frac{4}{3}-2 \alpha\right) L_{3}(z)-(1-2 \alpha) L_{2}(z) \\
& \quad L_{k}(z)=\sum_{i=1}^{N} \pi_{i}^{k} z_{i}, z_{i} \in\{0,1\}, i=1, \ldots, N, k=1,2,3
\end{aligned}
$$

When $\pi_{i} \equiv 1 / N$, it can be shown via tedious algebra that Theorem 3.1 recovers Theorem 2.2. In the general non-uniform case, while the bound is given by a complicated nonconvex integer programming problem, it can be solved approximately by off-the-shelf solvers when $N$ is small, e.g., $N<50$.

### 3.2 Sensitivity analysis

When assignment probabilities are not controlled by the researcher, it is useful to have procedures to analyze sensitivity of our approach to the uniform assignment assumption. The goal of sensitivity analysis is to answer the question: by how much would our statistical conclusions change if the treatment were non-uniform? Phrased another way, by how much would the treatment probabilities have to change to overturn a conclusion of statistical significance? Firpo and Possebom [FP18] give

an approach to do sensitivity analysis for the exact placebo test under non-uniform treatments. We can extend their approach to the naive LTO placebo test using the weighted LTO placebo $p$-values as the building block. The procedure is as follows.

1. Compute $p_{\text {naive-LTO }}$. If $p_{\text {naive-LTO }}>\alpha$, claim insignificance; otherwise, proceed with the following steps.
2. Fix a $\Gamma \in[1, N]$. Solve the optimization problem

$$
\begin{gathered}
\operatorname{maximize}_{\pi} p_{\mathrm{w}-\mathrm{LTO}}(\pi) \\
\text { subject to } \pi_{i} \in\left[\frac{1}{\Gamma N}, \frac{\Gamma}{N}\right] \forall i, \text { and } \sum_{i} \pi_{i}=1
\end{gathered}
$$

3. Find the smallest value of $\Gamma$ by a binary search such that the solution to the above optimization problem is greater than $\alpha$.

Remark 3.1. The choice of the bound $\left[\frac{1}{\Gamma N}, \frac{\Gamma}{N}\right]$ is not essential. We can handle any bounds on $\left(\pi_{1}, \ldots, \pi_{N}\right)$ defined by a single parameter. For example, the uncertainty set considered in the sensitivity analysis of [FP18] can be equivalently formulated as $\max _{i} \pi_{i} / \min _{i} \pi_{i} \leq \Gamma$ for some $\Gamma \geq 1$.

By Theorem 3.1, it remains to further add the constraint (13) into the integer programming problem. By carefully analyzing it, we end up with a much simpler result. The proof is presented in Appendix B.3.

Theorem 3.2. Assume that $\alpha \leq 1 / 3, \Gamma \leq N / 4$, and $\pi_{i} \in\left[\frac{1}{\Gamma N}, \frac{\Gamma}{N}\right]$ for all $i$. Then

$$
\mathbb{P}_{H_{0}}\left(p_{\mathrm{w}-\mathrm{LTO}}(\pi) \leq \alpha\right) \leq \frac{3-\frac{3 \Gamma}{N}-\sqrt{9\left(1-\frac{\Gamma}{N}\right)^{2}-12\left(-\frac{4 \Gamma^{2}}{3 N^{2}}+\frac{\Gamma}{N}+\alpha\left(1-\frac{2 \Gamma+1}{N}+\frac{2 \Gamma^{2}}{N^{2}}\right)\right)}}{2}
$$

When $\Gamma=1$, Theorem 3.2 recovers Theorem 2.2. When $N$ is large, the bound has the same limit $\frac{3-\sqrt{9-12 \alpha}}{2}$ as obtained for the $\Gamma=1$ setting. Unlike Theorem 2.2, we cannot sharpen it by taking advantage of the discreteness of the Type-I error because $\pi_{i}$ can take a range of values.

Computational Details. The optimization problem in step 2) may pose challenges. To optimize this problem, we use the following trick. Fixing a $\Gamma$, let $B_{\Gamma}$ be the set of $\pi$ satisfying the constraints in Equation (12). We only need to check if $\max _{\pi \in B_{\Gamma}} p_{\mathrm{w}-\mathrm{LTO}}(\pi)$ is greater than $\alpha$. This is equivalent to checking that

$$
\max _{\pi \in B_{\Gamma}} \sum_{\substack{j, k \neq I \\ j \neq k}} \pi_{j} \pi_{k} \mathbb{1}\{I \not j, k\}-\alpha\left(\left(1-\pi_{I}\right)^{2}-\sum_{l \neq I} \pi_{l}^{2}\right)>0
$$

The optimization problem can be written as a simple quadratic program. Namely, we must check whether

$$
\max _{\pi \in B_{\Gamma}} \pi^{\top} A \pi+2 \alpha \pi^{\top} e_{I}>\alpha
$$

where $A:=\left(G+\alpha I_{N}-2 \alpha e_{I} e_{I}^{\top}\right)$, with $G \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}$ is a zero-one matrix with entries $G_{j k}=\mathbb{1}\{I \not j, k\}$, whenever $j$ and $k$ do not equal $I$ and zero otherwise. Here $e_{I}$ is the standard basis vector for index $I$. If the goal is to calculate $\max _{\pi \in B_{\Gamma}} p_{\mathrm{w}-\mathrm{LTO}}(\pi)$, we can do a binary search over $c \in[0,1]$ that satisfy $\max _{\pi \in B_{\Gamma}} p_{\mathrm{w}-\mathrm{LTO}}(\pi)>c$, and apply the same trick as above.

The objective is not necessarily concave as $A$ is not guaranteed to be positive semidefinite. As it turns out, the resulting optimization problem is a fundamental NP-hard problem known as quadratic nonconvex programming with box constraints. In practice, non-convex quadratic programming problems are solved using heuristics such as branch-and-bound techniques. As the dimensionality of the problems that we are solving is rather small, we expect off-the-shelf solvers should do well on this problem. Moreover, whenever the computed LTO $p$-value is small, the matrix $G+I_{N}-2 e_{I} e_{I}^{\top}$ is usually rather sparse, which would help with computation.

# 4 Simulation Results 

Thus far we have explored theoretical properties of the LTO placebo tests. In this section, we compare empirical properties of the LTO placebo tests on semisynthetic examples based on the California Proposition 99 on tobacco consumption [ADH10], the Basque Country terrorism on local economic prosperity [AG03], and the German Reunification on West Germany's GDP. [ADH15]. All simulation results rely on the R package Synth [ADH11], and may be found at https://github. com/tsudijon/LeaveTwoOutSCI.

The structure of the semisynthetic simulations is the same for each dataset. Firstly, we subsample $N$ units (smaller than the size of the original data) from each dataset, excluding the original treated unit. As described briefly in the introduction, on each subsample of the data, a unit is chosen uniformly at random to be treated. For each treated unit $I$, we posit a time-invariant treatment effect $\tau$, which we add on to the observed outcomes $Y_{I, t}$ following $T_{0}$. A range of values of $\tau$ are used, corresponding approximately to $(0,-1,-2,-3)$ times the standard deviation of the outcome variable measured at $T_{0}$. For each subsample, several methods are compared in terms of power: the exact placebo, approximate placebo, randomized placebo, the naive LTO, and powered LTO.

The setting for $\tau=0$ amounts to comparing the Type-I errors for all methods. We compare the methods in two regimes: one in which $\alpha<1 / N$ and one in which $\alpha \geq 1 / N$. In the $\alpha<1 / N$ regime case we omit the results for the exact placebo, which will be powerless in this setting. Each method uses the widely-adopted RMSPE statistic defined in (6), and the synthetic controls are constructed in ways which follow the original analyses of each dataset. Finally, we average the powers and Type-I errors for all methods over 20 Monte Carlo subsamples of the original dataset, and report the resulting mean. This is an estimate of the unconditional Type-I error and power of each method.

Broadly, we observe the following empirical results. The LTO procedures demonstrate higher power in large signal to noise ratios, compared against the three placebo $p$-values. In intermediate signal to noise ratios, the naive LTO procedure often has lower or comparable power than the placebo procedures, but the powered LTO fares better. In the $\alpha<1 / N$ setting, we observe that the unconditional Type-I errors of the LTO methods are below $\alpha$ in all cases, whereas that of the approximate placebo $p$-value is always $1 / N$. This suggests that the upper bound on the conditional Type-I error established in Theorem 2.2 is not tight, and in practice, the LTO procedures often satisfy Type-I error constraints. The randomized placebo test does control the Type-I error, but its power is quite poor relative to the other procedures.

It is worth revisiting the classic case studies of the synthetic control method in [AG03, ADH10, ADH15] using the LTO and placebo inference procedures. For each use of the LTO $p$-value, we analyze the sensitivity of the conclusion to the equal weights assumption. For all sensitivity analyses, we use Gurobi 10.0 [Gur23] to solve the nonconvex quadratic optimization problem for optimizing $p_{\mathrm{w}-\mathrm{LTO}}(\pi)$. A similar sensitivity analysis for the exact and approximate placebo test may be done using the results of [FP18], which we do not replicate here.

### 5.1 Rank-sum LTO placebo test

Instead of labeling a unit as strange if it wins too many matches, we can consider a unit to be strange if its rank across all matches is too large. This leads to a variant of the procedure which has similar coverage guarantees as the original Jackknife+ procedure; see Theorem 1 of [BCRT21]. Define $\operatorname{rank}\{I ; I, i, j\}:=\mathbb{I}\{I>j\}+\mathbb{I}\{I>i\}$, where in this context, $\{I>j\}:=\left\{R_{I, i, j ; I}>R_{I, i, j ; j}\right\}$ and similarly for $\{I>i\}$. We can define a $p$-value

$$
p_{r \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{LTO}}:=\frac{1}{(N-1)(N-2)} \sum_{\substack{i, j \in[N] \backslash I \\ i \neq j}}\left(2-\operatorname{rank}\{I ; I, i, j\}\right)
$$

Using a similar argument as in Theorem 2.2, we derive an upper bound on the Type-I error of $p_{r \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{LTO}}$. The proof is presented in Appendix B.4.

Theorem 5.1 (Rank-sum LTO placebo test). The Type-I error of $p_{\text {rs-LTO }}$ has the following bound

$$
\mathbb{P}\left(p_{\text {rs-LTO }} \leq \alpha\right) \leq \frac{\lfloor(N-1) \alpha\rfloor+1}{N}
$$

The bound is equal to or $1 / N$ larger than that of $p_{\text {naive-LTO }}$ and $p_{\text {powered-LTO }}(\alpha)$. In particular, when $\alpha<1 /(N-1)$, all three choices have Type-I error controlled at $1 / N$, as shown in Theorem 2.1. However, it is easy to see that $2-\operatorname{rank}\{I ; I, i, j\} \geq \mathbb{I}\{I \neq i, j\}$ and thus $p_{\text {s-LTO }} \leq p_{\text {rs-LTO }}$ almost surely. Thus, $p_{\text {rs-LTO }}$ is less favorable because it always gives more conservative inference.

# 5.2 Leave- $r$-out placebo test 

We may consider an analogous procedure for the placebo test where $r$-tuples of data points are left out. The analysis of the method in this setting clarifies the essential features of the LTO case.

Extending the LTO procedure (Remark 2.1), we consider a tournament between all $N$ units where matches are held for every $(r+1)$ units. For each match among $\left\{i_{0}, i_{1}, \ldots, i_{r}\right\}$, we leave out all participants and construct synthetic controls for each of them. The score $R_{\left\{i_{0}, i_{1}, \ldots, i_{r}\right\} ; i_{j}}$ of participant $i_{j}$ is defined via the same form as (5). For example, we can calculate the RMSPE by using $[N] \backslash\left\{i_{0}, i_{1}, \ldots, i_{r}\right\}$ as the donor pool. We say $i_{0}$ wins a match among $\left\{i_{0}, i_{1}, \ldots, i_{r}\right\}$, denoted by $i_{0}>i_{1}, \ldots, i_{r}$, if $R_{\left\{i_{0}, i_{1}, \ldots, i_{r}\right\} ; i_{0}}>\max _{j \in[r]} R_{\left\{i_{0}, i_{1}, \ldots, i_{r}\right\} ; i_{j}}$. Extending the LTO p-value, define

Anagolously, we can also define the rank-sum LRO p-value:

$$
p_{\mathrm{rs}-\mathrm{LRO}}=\frac{(N-r-1)!}{(N-1)!} \sum_{\substack{i_{1}, \ldots, i_{r} \in[N] \backslash I \\ i_{1}, \ldots, i_{r} \text { mutually distinct }}} \frac{2}{r}\left(r-\operatorname{rank}\left\{I ; I, i_{1}, \ldots, i_{r}\right\}\right)
$$

where $\operatorname{rank}\left\{I ; I, i_{1}, \ldots, i_{r}\right\}=\sum_{j=1}^{r} \mathbb{I}\left\{I>i_{j}\right\}$ and, in this context, $I>i_{j}$ iff $R_{\left\{I, i_{1}, \ldots, i_{r}\right\} ; I}>$ $\max _{j \in[r]} R_{\left\{I, i_{1}, \ldots, i_{r}\right\} ; i_{j}}$. Note that they recover $p_{\text {LTO }}$ and $p_{\text {rs-LTO }}$ when $r=2$. Above, the normalization factor $2 / r$ is chosen to make the Type-I error to converge to $\alpha$ when $N$ tends to infinity, as evidenced by Theorem 5.3 below.

The following two results generalize Theorem 2.2 and Theorem 5.1 to $p_{\text {LRO }}$ and $p_{\text {rs-LRO }}$, respectively. Both proofs are presented in Appendix B.5. Via tedious algebra, we can show that they recover Theorem 2.2 and Theorem 5.1 when $r=2$.

Theorem 5.2. Let

$$
s \mapsto A_{N, r}(s)=\frac{1}{s}\left[\binom{N}{r+1}-\binom{N-s}{r+1}\right]
$$

Then $A_{N, r}(\cdot)$ is decreasing on $[N]$ and, for any $\alpha<r /(r+1)$,

$$
\mathbb{P}_{H_{0}}\left(p_{\mathrm{LRO}} \leq \alpha\right) \leq \frac{1}{N} \max \left\{k: A_{N, r}(k) \geq(1-\alpha)\binom{N-1}{r}\right\}
$$

Theorem 5.3. For any $\alpha \in(0,1)$,

$$
\mathbb{P}_{H_{0}}\left(p_{\mathrm{rs}-\mathrm{LRO}} \leq \alpha\right) \leq \frac{\lfloor(N-1) \alpha\rfloor+1}{N}
$$

# 6 Conclusion and discussion 

We propose a novel leave-two-out placebo test for the synthetic control method under uniform assignments. Compared to the standard placebo test based on permutation tests, the LTO placebo test has two advantages. First, it has the same theoretical conditional Type-I error guarantee for practical $(N, \alpha)$ pairs; further, it often achieves a strictly lower unconditional Type-I error in practice, unlike the standard placebo test for which the guarantee is tight. In particular, this permits valid inference even in the challenging $\alpha<1 / N$ case. Second, we find empirically that it has higher power for moderately large signal-to-noise ratio and comparable power otherwise. In most of our experimental settings, the powered-LTO placebo test almost Pareto-improves the standard placebo test.

While the design assumption is strong, it does not require large sample size and/or long time series, which are crucial for outcome-based approaches but uncommon in applications of the synthetic control method. Moreover, unlike most outcome-based approaches, the LTO placebo test does not artificially simplify the important steps, like weight matrix estimation and model selection, in the construction of synthetic controls. On the other hand, we propose a weighted variant of the LTO placebo test and a sensitivity analysis to account for non-uniform assignments. Below we discuss a few other related issues.

### 6.1 In-time placebo test

The placebo tests discussed in previous sections all exploit variation in which units are treated. An alternative approach is to exploit the variation of treatment timing. In the literature, in-time placebo tests are often performed by choosing a pre-treatment period, pretending it to be the time when the treatment occurs, and running the same synthetic control procedure to eyeball check if the treated unit differs from its synthetic control between the fake and actual treatment time [FHR13, ADH15, CGMS17, CEL20, Cha23]. To formalize it into a rigorous statistical inference procedure, we can follow [FP18] verbatim by assuming the treatment time $T_{0}$ is uniform in $\left[t_{1}, t_{2}\right]$ for some $1 \leq t_{0}<t_{1} \leq T$. Then the standard and LTO placebo tests can both be directly applied in this setting. For in-time placebo tests, the small sample issue is arguably more pronounced because it is typically only reasonable to choose a small time window $\left[t_{1}, t_{2}\right]$. Based on the promising performance of LTO placebo tests in unit-level placebo tests when the sample size is small, we expect they continue to excel for in-time placebo tests, though we leave the formal investigation for future work.

### 6.2 Testing non-sharp null and constructing confidence regions

In previous sections we focus on the sharp null (4). Following [FP18, CWZ21], it is straightforward to test any postulated effect vector $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{T-T_{0}}$ :

$$
H_{0}(\theta): \tau_{I, T_{0}+1}=\theta_{1}, \tau_{I, T_{0}+2}=\theta_{2}, \ldots, \tau_{I, T}=\theta_{T-T_{0}}
$$

In fact, by subtracting $\theta_{j}$ from $Y_{I\left(T_{0}+j\right)}(1)$, we are back to the case of sharp null. Phrased differently, we can redefine the RMSPE $R_{i, j, I, I}(\theta)$ for the treated unit within the triple $\{i, j, I\}$ by replacing $Y_{I t}$ by $Y_{I t}-\theta_{t-T_{0}}$ for $t=T_{0}+1, \ldots, T$ and reject $H_{0}(\theta)$ if $p_{\text {naive-LTO }}(\theta) \leq \alpha$, where

$$
p_{\text {naive-LTO }}(\theta):=\frac{1}{(N-1)(N-2)} \sum_{\substack{i, j \in[N] \backslash I \\ i \neq j}} \mathbb{I}\left\{R_{i, j, I ; I}(\theta) \leq \max \left(R_{i, j, I ; i}, R_{i, j, I ; j}\right)\right\}
$$

Note that the other RMSPEs remain the same because the synthetic control weights only depend on pre-treatment outcomes which are unchanged.

By inverting the test, we can obtain a confidence region for $\left(\tau_{I, T_{0}+1}, \tau_{I, T_{0}+2}, \ldots, \tau_{I, T}\right)$ :

$$
\mathcal{C}_{1-\alpha}=\left\{\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{T-T_{0}}: p_{\text {naive-LTO }}(\theta)>\alpha\right\}
$$

When the RMSPE is used, by (16), there exists $A_{i j} \in \mathbb{R}^{T-T_{0}}$ and $b_{i j} \in \mathbb{R}$ for $i, j \in[N] \backslash I$ such that

$$
\mathcal{C}_{1-\alpha}=\left\{\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{T-T_{0}}: \sum_{i, j \in[N] \backslash I} \mathbb{I}\left\{\left\|\theta-A_{i j}\right\|_{2}^{2} \leq b_{i j}\right\}>\alpha(N-1)(N-2)\right\}
$$

Each indicator gives a ball and $\mathcal{C}_{1-\alpha}$ is an union of intersections of these balls that lie in at least $\alpha(N-1)(N-2)$ balls. By Theorem 2.2,

$$
\mathbb{P}\left(\left(\tau_{I, T_{0}+1}, \tau_{I, T_{0}+2}, \ldots, \tau_{I, T}\right) \in \mathcal{C}_{1-\alpha}\right) \geq 1-\frac{|N f(N, \alpha)|}{N}
$$

# 6.3 Consistency of LTO and inconsistency of approximate placebo test 

Power analysis is challenging in our setting as it typically relies on large sample approximation that tends to be uninformative when $N$ and $T$ are small, particularly given the complexity of the synthetic control procedure. In this section, we make an attempt to explain the empirically observed power improvement over the approximate placebo test.

In the classical asymptotic regime where $N$ tends to infinity, a test is consistent if its power converges to 1 for any fixed alternative. Qualitatively, consistency ensures that the null hypothesis will be rejected with high probability as the problem becomes sufficiently easy. We migrate the notion of consistency to the fixed- $N$ setting by defining a test to be consistent if the power converges to 1 as the signal strength under the alternative grows to infinity. Consistency serves as a minimal requirement for a desirable test. We show that, if $\alpha<1 / N$, the LTO placebo test is uniformly consistent, whereas the approximate placebo test is not. In fact, the latter may have nearly zero power even with an arbitrarily large signal strength.

Theorem 6.1. Let $G$ denote the distribution of $\left(Y_{i, t}(0)\right)_{i \leq N, t \leq T}$. Consider an alternative hypothesis parametrized by a single parameter $\eta>0$ :

$$
H_{1, \eta}: Y_{i, t}(1)=Y_{i, t}(0)+\eta \tau_{i, t}
$$

for some deterministic matrix $\tau=\left(\tau_{i, t}\right)_{i \leq N, t \leq T} \in \Gamma$ where $\Gamma$ includes all matrices $\tau$ with $\min _{i} \sum_{t} \tau_{i t}^{2} \geq$ 1. Assume $N, T$ are fixed and $\alpha<1 / N$. Consider the LTO and approximate placebo tests based on the post-RMSPE statistic defined in (6). Then, for any tight collection of distributions $\mathcal{G}$ on $\mathbb{R}^{N \times T}$,

$$
\lim _{\eta \rightarrow \infty} \inf _{G \in \mathcal{G}, \tau \in \Gamma} \mathbb{P}_{G, \tau, H_{1, \eta}}\left(p_{\text {naive-LTO }} \leq \alpha\right)=1
$$

Furthermore, there exists infinitely many pairs $(G, \tau) \in \mathcal{G} \times \Gamma$ that do not vary with $\eta$ such that

$$
\lim _{\eta \rightarrow \infty} \mathbb{P}_{G, \tau, H_{1, \eta}}\left(p_{\text {app-placebo }} \leq \alpha\right) \begin{cases}=0 & \text { if } N \text { is even } \\ \leq 1 / N & \text { if } N \text { is odd }\end{cases}
$$

A proof may be found in Section B.6.

# 6.4 Leave-two-out techniques for other problems 

The theory of LTO placebo tests only relies on the uniform assignment assumption but not the choice of summary statistics. Thus, it can be applied beyond synthetic control applications to any problem with the same structure. Here, we discuss one instance in the context of inference with few clusters [BDM04, DL07, IM10, CT11, BCH11, IK16, IM16, CRS17, MW18, Hag19, CMM20, Hag20, CSS21, Hag23, Web23]; see also the surveys by [CM15] and [MNW23]. With a few exceptions that assumes normality of errors (e.g., [DL07]), existing approaches with theoretical guarantees rely on large clusters in order for the within-cluster test statistics to be close to normally distributed (e.g., [IM10, IM16, Hag20]) or at least close to be symmetrically distributed (e.g., [CRS17, CSS21, Hag23]) - these distributional assumptions are hard to justify in most applications of synthetic controls. Other procedures work well empirically when the cluster sizes are moderate, e.g., $\geq 30$ [CGM08], though the theory still requires the sample size tends to infinity in each cluster. Moreover, to do inference on treatment effects, many methods that can accommodate small clusters require at least two [CT11, Hag19] or $\log (1 / \alpha)$ [CRS17] treated clusters if the total number of clusters is below $1 / \alpha$.

In sum, when there is a single treated cluster, which is common in applications, existing methods rely on either large-sample approximation, or that the number of control clusters is at least $1 / \alpha-1$. In this case, if the data comes from a cluster randomized experiment [SD21, LLLD23] or if the researcher is willing to assume uniform assignment, the LTO procedure can be applied to test Fisher's sharp null. We leave the details for future research.

### 6.5 Leave-two-out test as a new type of randomization inference

Classical randomization inference [Fis37, Rom89, Rom90, Din17] exploits exchangeability, or group invariance more generally, by comparing the test statistic for the observed data with those for permuted or group-transformed data. Despite a century of development, nearly all randomization inference procedures are variants of this generic form with different choices of test statistics or group of transformations. The LTO placebo tests, or the more general leave- $r$-out tests, are new types of randomization inference that move beyond classical permutation or rank-based inference.

Our tests generalize the Jackknife+ [BCRT21], which also differs from classical randomization inference. It is an extension of conformal prediction [VGS05] and the Jackknife procedure [Wu86] to construct prediction intervals with guaranteed coverage under exchangeability. Let $Z_{1}=\left(X_{1}, Y_{1}\right), \ldots, Z_{N}=\left(X_{N}, Y_{N}\right)$ be exchangeable observations where $X_{i}$ denotes a set of covariates and $Y_{i}$ denotes the outcome. The goal is to construct a prediction interval $\hat{C}\left(X_{N+1}\right)$ such that $\mathbb{P}\left(Y_{N+1} \in \hat{C}\left(X_{N+1}\right)\right) \geq 1-\alpha$ assuming $Z_{N+1}=\left(X_{N+1}, Y_{N+1}\right)$ is exchangeable with $\left\{Z_{1}, \ldots, Z_{N}\right\}$. Jackknife+ essentially inverts a leave-one-out test:

$$
p\left(Y_{N+1}\right)=\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{I}\left\{R_{i, N+1 ; N+1} \leq R_{i, N+1 ; i}\right\}
$$

where $R_{i, j ; j}=\left|Y_{j}-\hat{\mu}_{-i,-j}\left(X_{j}\right)\right|$ where $\hat{\mu}_{-i,-j}(\cdot)$ can be any model fit on $\left(Z_{k}\right)_{k \in[N] \backslash\{i, j\}}$. However, Jackknife+ suffers from the same granularity issue as the standard placebo tests because it can only take multiples of $1 / N$.

Another exception is the prediction inference of model transfer errors by [AFL^23]. They consider a setting with data from $N$ exchangeable training domains (e.g., countries), denoted as $Z_{1}, \ldots, Z_{N}$. For a given economic or machine learning model and a pair of domains $Z_{i}, Z_{j}$, let $e\left(Z_{i}, Z_{j}\right)$ denote the accuracy (e.g., the mean square error) of the model fit on the $i$-th domain and evaluated on the $j$-th domain. Let $Z_{N+1}$ denote an unobserved target domain that is exchangeable

with $\left\{Z_{1}, \ldots, Z_{N}\right\}$. The goal is to construct a prediction interval for the random transfer error $e\left(Z_{I}, Z_{n+1}\right)$ where $I$ is chosen from $[N]$ uniformly at random. Their interval is constructed based on quantiles of $\left\{e\left(Z_{i}, Z_{j}\right): i, j \in[N], i \neq j\right\}$ even if they are no longer exchangeable as in classical randomization inference due to the bivariate structure.

# A A Survey of Applications of Synthetic Controls 

In this section, we conduct a literature survey on empirical applications of synthetic controls. The results are summarized in the Tables 2 and 3. We focused on the size of the datasets in the studies, along with the use of placebo inference. Several findings are of interest:

- Small Datasets. For the overwhelming majority of studies surveyed, the synthetic control was used to analyze one treated unit, with a donor pool on the order of a few dozen. In table 2, several studies use a staggered adoption framework, although the synthetic control method is still applied to analyze one treated unit at a time. In only a few studies [Liu15, And15, AJK $\left.{ }^{+} 16\right]$ was the donor pool near 100 or more, which enables inference at traditional levels like $\alpha=0.01$. Furthermore, the number of pre- and post-treatment time periods is typically also on the order of a few dozen. In only a handful of studies [FHR13, Cha23, Liu15, CGMS17, AJK $\left.{ }^{+} 16\right, \mathrm{BSG} 23]$ were the number of time periods substantial. On the other hand, several studies used the placebo test when $N_{0}, T_{0}$ or $T_{1}$ were less than 5 [CN12, BLR14]. Asymptotic inferential procedures seem difficult to justify in these settings.
- Resulting Issues with Placebo Inference. The predominant robustness check / inference methodology used among papers in the survey was the unit-level placebo test. Only a few papers did not use the placebo procedure. Moreover, some authors note that the placebobased $p$-value cannot be created when using small levels $\alpha$, and more broadly, convey the difficulties in doing inference when the donor pool is small. For example, [Cha23] notes "The two-sided RI $p$-value cannot get any smaller than $0.04[\ldots]$. Thus, the coefficients of interest in this study cannot be significant at a $1 \%$ level of significance." Newiak and Williams [NW17] remark similarly: "Typically, however, there are not many countries in the control group for which we can construct counterfactuals with a fit that is at least as good as for the treated country, which limits the informativeness of this exercise (and prevents us from calculating meaningful p-values)."
- Alternative Inference Procedures. Alternative inferential procedures (methods to calculate $p$-values) were few. Several papers [AA16, AW12] in the staggered adoption setting use a placebo method for average treatment effects for grouped data outlined in [CGNP13]. Otherwise, in-time placebo robustness checks were occasionally used [FHR13, ADH15, CGMS17, CEL20, Cha23, DAW19, And15, BMFR22]. A few papers attempted a bootstrap-based approach [SHK ${ }^{+} 15$, BMFR22], justified using asymptotic theory with assumptions on the data generating process.

These numbers reflect our best estimate of the study design from reading the corresponding papers. Some papers conduct multiple analyses using different outcome variables, for which the size of the donor pool or time periods change. As a result, the number of units in the donor pool and the number of pre- and post-treatment periods change per analysis.
