\sup _{r \in(0,1)}(1-r) D\left(M_{i}, B_{r}\right)^{-1} \rightarrow \infty
$$

Choose $r_{i} \in(0,1)$ so that

$$
\left(1-r_{i}\right) D\left(M_{i}, B_{r_{i}}\right)^{-1} \geqslant \frac{1}{2} \sup _{r \in(0,1)}(1-r) D\left(M_{i}, B_{r}\right)^{-1}
$$

Choose $z_{i} \in \overline{B_{r_{i}}}$ with $d\left(z_{i}, \partial M_{i}\right)=D\left(M_{i}, B_{r_{i}}\right)$ so that

$$
\text { either } \frac{1}{2 c_{0}} \tan \theta_{i} d\left(\pi\left(z_{i}\right), \partial M_{i}\right)=z_{i, 1}, \quad \text { or } \quad z_{i, 1}=2 \tan \theta_{i} d\left(\pi\left(z_{i}\right), \partial M_{i}\right)
$$

$\left(z_{i, 1}\right.$ being the first component of $\left.z_{i}\right)$. Set $d_{i}=d\left(\pi\left(z_{i}\right), \partial M\right) \leqslant D\left(M_{i}, B_{r_{i}}\right)$. Clearly we have $d_{i} \rightarrow 0$ and

$$
d\left(\pi\left(z_{i}\right), \partial B_{1}\right) d_{i}^{-1} \geqslant\left(1-r_{i}\right) d_{i}^{-1} \geqslant\left(1-r_{i}\right) D\left(M_{i}, B_{r_{i}}\right)^{-1} \rightarrow \infty
$$

Define the dilated/translated domains $\Omega_{i}^{\prime}=\left(\Omega_{i}-\pi\left(z_{i}\right)\right) / d_{i}$, surfaces $M_{i}^{\prime}=\left(M_{i}-\pi\left(z_{i}\right)\right) / d_{i}$, and points $z_{i}^{\prime}=\left(z_{i}-\pi\left(z_{i}\right)\right) / r_{i}$. Given any fixed $R>0$, then for $i \gg 1$ the $\Omega_{i}^{\prime}$ will be minimizers of $\mathscr{A}^{\theta_{i}}$ in $B_{R}(0) \equiv B_{R}\left(\pi\left(z_{i}^{\prime}\right)\right)$ with

$$
D\left(M_{i}^{\prime}, B_{R}\right) \geqslant 1 / 4
$$

but for which

$$
\text { either } \frac{1}{2 c_{0}} \tan \theta_{i} d\left(0, \partial M_{i}\right)=z_{i, 1}^{\prime} \quad \text { or } \quad z_{i, 1}^{\prime}=2 \tan \theta_{i} d\left(0, \partial M_{i}^{\prime}\right)
$$

From (4.48), Lemma 4.8, Proposition 4.11, for any $R>0$ there are $i_{0}(R)$ and $\varepsilon(R)$ and Lipschitz functions $u_{i}: R_{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ so that $M_{i}^{\prime} \cap B_{R}=\operatorname{graph}_{\mathbf{R}^{n}}\left(u_{i}\right)$ in $\left\{0<x_{1}<\varepsilon(R)\right\}$ whenever $i>i_{0}(R)$. Taking a diagonal subsequence, the functions $u_{i} / \tan \theta_{i}$ converge in $\left(C_{l o c}^{\alpha} \cap\right.$ $\left.W_{l o c}^{1,2}\right\}\left(\mathbf{R}^{n}\right)$ to an entire Lipschitz minimizer $v: \mathbf{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ of the Alt-Caffarelli functional $J$. Lemma 2.1 and the local Hausdorff convergence $\partial\left\{u_{i}>0\right\} \rightarrow \partial\{v>0\}$ imply that

$$
\frac{1}{2 c_{0}} \tan \theta_{i} d\left(z, \partial\left\{u_{i}>0\right\}\right)<u_{i}(z)<2 \tan \theta_{i} d\left(z, \partial\left\{u_{i}>0\right\}\right)
$$

for all $z \in B_{1}^{n}$ and all $i \gg 1$, which contradicts (4.49) since $z_{i, 1}^{\prime}=u_{i}(0)$ and $\partial\left\{u_{i}>0\right\}=$ $\partial M_{i}^{\prime}$.

Most importantly for us, in low dimensions (whereever 1-homogenous minimizers of the Alt-Caffarelli energy are linear) we can obtain a priori curvature estimates near the boundary also.

Lemma 4.14 (Curvature estimates). There are absolute constants $C, \varepsilon, \theta_{0}$ so that the following holds. Take $n \leqslant 4$, and let $\Omega$ be a smooth minimizer of $\mathscr{A}^{\theta}$ in $B_{1} \subset \mathbf{R}^{n+1}$, for $\theta \in$ $\left(0, \theta_{0}\right)$, and write $M=\partial \Omega \cap \mathbf{R}_{+}^{n+1}$. Then, assuming $0 \in \partial M$, we have the curvature bound $\sup _{M \cap B_{c}\left(\mathbf{R}^{n}\right) \cap B_{1 / 8}} \theta^{-1}\left|A_{M}\right| \leqslant C$.

Proof. After replacing $B_{1}$ with $B_{1 / 4}$ there will be no loss in assuming that $\sup _{B_{1}}\left|A_{M}\right|<\infty$. Moreover, ensuring $\theta_{0}$ is sufficiently small, by Lemmas $4.13,4.8$ there is also no loss in assuming we have a Lipschitz function $u: B_{1}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ and absolute constant $\varepsilon>0$ so that $M=\operatorname{graph}_{\mathbf{R}^{n}}(u)$ in $B_{1} \cap\left\{0<x_{1}<\varepsilon\right\}$ and $\operatorname{Lip}(u) \leqslant c \theta$ and

$$
\frac{1}{c} \theta d(z, \partial\{u>0\}) \leqslant u(z) \leqslant 2 c \theta d(z, \partial\{u>0\})
$$

for all $z \in B_{1}^{n} \cap\{u>0\}$. Here $c$ is an absolute constant.
We will show that $\theta^{-1}\left|A_{M}(x)\right|(1-|x|)$ is bounded by an absolute constant, provided $\theta$ is sufficiently small, which will clearly prove the Lemma. Suppose otherwise: there are sequences $\theta_{i} \rightarrow 0, \Omega_{i}$ minimizing $\mathscr{A}^{\theta_{i}}$ in $B_{1}$, so that if $M_{i}=\partial \Omega_{i} \cap \mathbf{R}_{+}^{n+1}$ then

$$
\sup _{x \in B_{1}} \theta_{i}^{-1}\left|A_{M_{i}}(x)\right|(1-|x|) \rightarrow \infty
$$

as $i \rightarrow \infty$. Choose $x_{i} \in B_{1}$ satisfying

$$
\theta_{i}^{-1}\left|A_{M_{i}}\left(x_{i}\right)\right|\left(1-\left|x_{i}\right|\right) \geqslant \frac{1}{2} \sup _{x \in B_{1}} \theta_{i}^{-1}\left|A_{M_{i}}(x)\right|(1-|x|)
$$

Let $\lambda_{i}=\theta_{i}^{-1}\left|A_{M_{i}}\left(x_{i}\right)\right|$. Write $u_{i}$ for the graphing functions for $M_{i}$ as in the first paragraph. We break into two cases.

Case 1: $\sup _{i} \lambda_{i}^{-1} d\left(x_{i}, \partial M_{i}\right)<\infty$. Define the rescaled domains $\Omega_{i}^{\prime}=\lambda_{i}\left(\Omega_{i}-\pi\left(x_{i}\right)\right)$, surfaces $M_{i}^{\prime}=\lambda_{i}\left(M_{i}-\pi\left(x_{i}\right)\right) \equiv \partial \Omega_{i}^{\prime} \cap \partial \mathbf{R}_{+}^{n+1}$, and points $x_{i}^{\prime}=\lambda_{i}\left(x_{i}-\pi\left(x_{i}\right)\right)$. Then for a suitable $R_{i} \rightarrow \infty$, the $\Omega_{i}^{\prime}$ are minimizers of $\mathscr{A}^{\theta_{i}}$ in $B_{R_{i}}(0) \equiv B_{R_{i}}\left(\pi\left(x_{i}^{\prime}\right)\right)$, satisfying

$$
\sup _{i} d\left(0, \partial M_{i}^{\prime}\right)<\infty, \quad \theta_{i}^{-1}\left|A_{M_{i}^{\prime}}\left(x_{i}^{\prime}\right)\right|=1, \quad \sup _{B_{R_{i}}} \theta_{i}^{-1}\left|A_{M_{i}^{\prime}}\right| \leqslant 2
$$

Moreover, if we let $u_{i}^{\prime}(z)=\lambda_{i} u_{i}\left(\left(z-\pi\left(x_{i}\right)\right) / \lambda_{i}\right)$, then $M_{i}^{\prime} \cap B_{R_{i}}=\operatorname{graph}_{\mathbf{R}^{n}}\left(u_{i}^{\prime}\right), \operatorname{Lip}\left(u_{i}^{\prime}\right) \leqslant$ $c(\rho) \theta_{i}$, and the $u_{i}^{\prime}$ continue to satisfy (4.50) on $B_{R_{i}}^{n} \cap\left(u_{i}^{\prime}>0\right)$.

We can therefore apply Proposition 4.11 (after passing to a subsequence as necessary) to deduce there is entire Lipschitz minimizer $v: \mathbf{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ of the Alt-Caffarelli functional $J$ so that $\theta_{i}^{-1} u_{i}^{\prime} \rightarrow v$ in $\left(C_{l o c}^{a} \cap W_{l o c}^{1,2}\right)\left(\mathbf{R}^{n}\right)$. By Lemma 2.2 and our restriction $n \leqslant 4$, we must have $v(z)=\left(\left(z-z_{0}\right) \cdot n\right)_{+}$for some $z_{0} \in \mathbf{R}^{n}$ and unit vector $n$, and in particular we have $D^{2} v \equiv 0$. On the other hand, (4.51) and Proposition 4.11 imply we have

$$
\sup _{B_{1}} \theta_{i}^{-1}\left|A_{M_{i}^{\prime}}\right| \rightarrow \sup _{B_{1}^{n} \cap\{v>0\}}\left|D^{2} v\right| \equiv 0
$$

which contradicts our choice of $\theta_{i}^{-1}\left|A_{M_{i}^{\prime}}(0)\right|=1$.

Case 2: $\sup _{i} \lambda_{i}^{-1} d\left(x_{i}, \partial M_{i}\right)=\infty$. After passing to a subsequence we can assume

$$
\lim _{i} \lambda_{i}^{-1} d\left(x_{i}, \partial M_{i}\right)=\infty
$$

Define the functions

$$
u_{i}^{\prime}(z)=\lambda_{i}\left(u_{i}\left(\left(z-\pi\left(x_{i}\right)\right) / \lambda_{i}\right)-x_{i, 1}\right)
$$

so that $M_{i}^{\prime}:=\lambda_{i}\left(M_{i}-x_{i}\right)$ are the graphs of $u_{i}^{\prime}$. Then for a sequence $R_{i} \rightarrow \infty$, the $u_{i}^{\prime}$ are smooth solutions to the minimal surface equation in $B_{R_{i}}^{n}$ satisfying

$$
\operatorname{Lip}\left(u_{i}^{\prime}\right) \leqslant c \theta_{i}, \quad u_{i}^{\prime}(0)=0, \quad \theta_{i}^{-1}\left|D^{2} u_{i}^{\prime}(0)\right|=1+O\left(\theta_{i}\right)
$$

By standard interior estimates and the structure of the minimal surface equation, after passing to a further subsequence, we have $C_{l o c}^{2}\left(\mathbf{R}^{n}\right)$ convergence of $\theta_{i}^{-1} u_{i}^{\prime} \rightarrow v$ for some harmonic function $v: \mathbf{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ satisfying

$$
\operatorname{Lip}(v) \leqslant c, \quad v(0)=0, \quad\left|D^{2} v(0)\right|=1
$$

But now the Liouville theorem for harmonic functions together with the first condition implies $v$ is linear, which is a contradiction with the third condition.
4.4. Proof of Theorems 1.3, 1.4, 4.1. Theorems 1.3, 4.1 follow by directly combining Lemma 4.10, Proposition 4.11, with Lemmas 4.13 and 4.14. The basic strategy for Theorem 1.4 involves three key ingredients: we can write $M_{i}=\operatorname{graph}\left(u_{i}\right)$ and blow-up $\theta_{i}^{-1} u_{i} \rightarrow v$ for some 1-homogenous $v$ minimizing the Alt-Caffarelli energy (Theorem 1.3); in low dimensions $v$ must be linear, and so the improved convergence of Proposition 4.11 implies $\theta_{i}^{-1}\left|A_{M_{i} \cap \partial B_{1}}\right| \rightarrow$ 0 ; a rigidity-type theorem for minimal capillary surfaces $\Sigma$ in the sphere with small angle $\theta$ and $\theta^{-1}\left|A_{\Sigma}\right|$ small. We first state the rigidity theorem.

Lemma 4.15 (Rigidity of almost-planar capillary cones with small angle). There is a $\delta(n)$ so that the following holds. Let $M$ be a stationary capillary cone in $\mathbf{R}_{+}^{n+1}$ making contact angle $\theta>0$ with $\mathbf{R}^{n}$, and with smooth link $\Sigma$. Suppose the outward conormal $\eta_{\Sigma}$ and second fundamental form $A_{\Sigma}$ of $\Sigma$ satisfy

$$
\left|\eta_{\Sigma}+e_{n+1}\right| \leqslant \delta, \quad \theta^{-1}\left|A_{\Sigma}\right| \leqslant \delta
$$

Then $M$ is a half-plane.
(Note, though we don't explicitly state it as a hypothesis, $\eta_{\Sigma}$ can only be close to $-e_{n+1} \in$ $\mathbf{R}^{n} \equiv \partial \mathbf{R}_{+}^{n+1}$ provided $\theta$ is very small.)

Proof. We first note the following trace-type inequality: provided $\delta$ is small, we have for any non-negative $f \in C^{1}(\Sigma)$ :

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\partial \Sigma} f & \leqslant 2 \int_{\partial \Sigma} f\left(-\eta \cdot e_{n+1}\right)=2 \int_{\Sigma} \operatorname{div}_{\Sigma}\left(-f e_{n+1}\right) \\
& =2 \int_{\Sigma} \nabla_{\Sigma} f \cdot\left(-e_{n+1}\right)+f \operatorname{div}_{\Sigma}\left(-e_{n+1}\right) \leqslant 2 \int_{\Sigma}\left|\nabla_{\Sigma} f\right|+(n-1) f
\end{aligned}
$$

having used that $\operatorname{div}_{\Sigma}\left(e_{n+1}\right)=n-1$.

We now take Simons' equation on $\Sigma$ (see $\{25$, Theorem 5.3.1]*),

$$
\Delta_{\Sigma} A_{\Sigma}=\left(n-1-\left|A_{\Sigma}\right|^{2}\right) A_{\Sigma}
$$

and inner product with $A_{\Sigma}$ and integrate over $\Sigma$, to obtain

$$
\int_{\Sigma}\left|\nabla_{\Sigma} A_{\Sigma}\right|^{2}+\left(n-1-\left|A_{\Sigma}\right|^{2}\right)\left|A_{\Sigma}\right|^{2}=\int_{\partial \Sigma} \frac{1}{2} \frac{\partial\left|A_{\Sigma}\right|^{2}}{\partial \eta}
$$

By $\{21$, Lemma C.2\}, we have

$$
\left|\frac{\partial\left|A_{\Sigma}\right|^{2}}{\partial \eta}\right| \leqslant c(n) \cot \theta\left|A_{\Sigma}\right|^{3}
$$

Therefore, combining (4.54) with (4.53), and using (4.52) with our assumption $\theta^{-1}\left|A_{\Sigma}\right| \leqslant \delta$, we get

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\Sigma}\left|\nabla_{\Sigma} A_{\Sigma}\right|^{2}+(n-1)\left|A_{\Sigma}\right|^{2} & \leqslant c(n) \delta \int_{\partial \Sigma}\left|A_{\Sigma}\right|^{2}+\int_{\Sigma}\left|A_{\Sigma}\right|^{4} \\
& \leqslant c(n) \delta \int_{\Sigma} 2\left|A_{\Sigma}\right|\left|\nabla_{\Sigma}\right| A_{\Sigma}| |+(n-1)\left|A_{\Sigma}\right|^{2}+\delta \theta \int_{\Sigma}\left|A_{\Sigma}\right|^{2} \\
& \leqslant c(n) \delta \int_{\Sigma}\left|\nabla_{\Sigma} A_{\Sigma}\right|^{2}+\left|A_{\Sigma}\right|^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

having used Kato's inequality $\left|\nabla_{\Sigma}\right| A_{\Sigma}| | \leqslant\left|\nabla_{\Sigma} A_{\Sigma}\right|$ in the last line. Ensuring $\delta(n)$ is small, we deduce $A_{\Sigma} \equiv 0$, as desired.

Proof of Theorem 1.4. Suppose, towards a contradiction, there are sequences $\theta_{i} \rightarrow 0$ and cones $\Omega_{i}$ minimizing $\mathscr{A}^{\theta_{i}}$, so that each $M_{i}:=\partial \Omega_{i} \cap \mathbf{R}_{+}^{n+1}$ is smooth away from 0 but nonplanar. For $i \gg 1$, we can apply Lemma 4.13 at points in $\partial M_{i} \cap \partial B_{1}$ to get the non-degeneracy condition (4.13) with some absolute constant $\gamma$. We can then use Lemma 4.10 and Proposition 4.11 to deduce $M_{i}=\operatorname{graph}_{\mathbf{R}^{n}}\left(u_{i}\right)$, where $u_{i} / \theta_{i} \rightarrow v$ for some 1-homogenous minimizer $v$.

By our dimensionality hypothesis $v$ must be linear, and without loss of generality $v(x)=$ $\left(e_{n+1} \cdot x\right)_{+}$. By Lemma 4.14 and Proposition 4.11, we have $\sup _{\partial B_{1}} \theta_{i}^{-1}\left|A_{M_{i}}\right| \rightarrow \sup _{\partial B_{1}}\left|D^{2} v\right|=$ 0 , and that $\theta_{i}^{-1} u_{i} \rightarrow\left(e_{n+1} \cdot x\right)_{+}$in $C_{l n c}^{2, a}\left(B_{2} \backslash B_{1 / 2}\right)$ at the level of Hodograph transform, which implies that the conormals $\eta_{M_{i}} \rightarrow-e_{n+1}$ uniformly in $\partial B_{1}$. Lemma 4.15 then tells us that the $M_{i}$ are planar for $i \gg 1$, which is a contradiction.

# 5. THE CASE $4 \leqslant n \leqslant 6$ WITH $\theta$ CLOSE TO $\pi / 2$ 

In this section we prove case (3) of Theorem 1.2. In fact, we prove the following slightly stronger result.

Theorem 5.1. Suppose $4 \leqslant n \leqslant 6$. There exists $\theta_{1}=\theta_{1}(n)$ such that if $\Omega \subset \mathbf{R}^{n+1}$ is a stable minimal cone for $\mathscr{A}^{\theta}, M=\partial \Omega \cap \mathbf{R}_{+}^{n+1}$ is smooth away from 0 , and $\theta \in\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta_{1}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]$, then $M$ is flat.

Remark 5.2. It will be clear from the proof that $\theta_{1}$ can be explicitly computed depending on $n$ (compare to $\{21$, Appendix C $\}$ ).

We first recall the following consequence of the Simons's equation on a minimal cone.
Lemma 5.3. Let $M^{n} \subset \mathbf{R}^{n+1}$ be a minimal cone. Then for every $\lambda \in(0,1)$, we have that

$$
\Delta\left(\frac{1}{2}|A|^{2}\right)+|A|^{4} \geqslant 2 \lambda|x|^{-2}|A|^{2}+\left[(1-\lambda)\left(1+\frac{2}{n}\right)+\lambda\right]|\nabla| A \|^{2}
$$

on $M$.
Proof. Fix a point $p \in M$ and take normal coordinates $\left\{x_{i}\right\}_{i=1}^{n}$ around $p$, then the Simons equation gives

$$
\Delta\left(\frac{1}{2}|A|^{2}\right)+|A|^{4}=\sum_{i, j, k=1}^{n} A_{i j, k}^{2}
$$

We write the right hand side of the above as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Delta\left(\frac{1}{2}|A|^{2}\right)+|A|^{4}=\lambda\left(\sum_{i, j, k=1}^{n} A_{i j, k}^{2}-\sum_{i, j, k=1}^{n}|A|^{-2}\left(A_{i j} A_{i j, k}\right)^{2}\right) \\
&+(1-\lambda) \sum A_{i j, k}^{2}+\lambda \sum|A|^{-2}\left(A_{i j} A_{i j, k}\right)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Note that $\sum|A|^{-2}\left(A_{i j} A_{i j, k}\right)^{2}=|\nabla| A \|^{2}$. On one hand, since $M$ is a cone, we have that (see $[24, \mathrm{~B} .9])$

$$
\sum_{i, j, k=1}^{n} A_{i j, k}^{2}-\sum_{i, j, k=1}^{n}|A|^{-2}\left(A_{i j} A_{i j, k}\right)^{2} \geqslant 2|x|^{-2}|A(x)|^{2}
$$

On the other hand, we have the following Kato type inequality (see [8, (2.22)]):

$$
\sum A_{i j, k}^{2} \geqslant\left(1+\frac{2}{n}\right)|\nabla| A \|^{2}
$$

(5.1) follows from combining the above two estimates.

Next, we establish the following trace type inequality on $M$. This was proven in [21, Lemma C.3]. We include it here for completeness. (Compare to Lemma 4.15, where a similar trace type inequality holds when $\theta$ is close to 0 ).
Lemma 5.4. Suppose $M^{n} \subset \mathbf{R}_{+}^{n+1}$ is a smoothly immersed hypersurface, meeting $\partial \mathbf{R}_{+}^{n+1}$ at constant angle $\theta$. Then for any $u \in W^{1,1}(M)$, we have

$$
\int_{\partial M} u \leqslant \frac{1}{\sin \theta} \int_{M}|\nabla u|+\left|H_{M} u\right|
$$

Proof. For $R>0$, let $\varphi_{R}:[0, \infty) \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ be a smooth function such that $\varphi_{R}(t)=1$ when $t \leqslant$ $R, \varphi_{R}(t)=0$ when $t \geqslant 2 R$, and $\left|\varphi_{R}^{\prime}\right| \leqslant \frac{2}{R}$. Consider the vector field $\zeta=-\varphi_{R}\left(x_{1}\right) \partial_{1}$. By assumption, we have that $\eta \cdot \zeta=\sin \theta$ along $\partial M$. Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\partial M} u & =\frac{1}{\sin \theta} \int_{\partial M} u \eta \cdot \zeta \\
& =\frac{1}{\sin \theta} \int_{M} \operatorname{div}_{M}\left(u \zeta^{T}\right) \leqslant \frac{1}{\sin \theta} \int_{M}|\nabla u||\zeta|+u \operatorname{div}_{M} \zeta+\left|u H_{M}\right| \\
& \leqslant \frac{1}{\sin \theta} \int_{M}|\nabla u|+\frac{2}{R} u+\left|H_{M} u\right|
\end{aligned}
$$

Sending $R \rightarrow \infty$ gives the result.
Proof of Theorem 5.1. For $p \in\left(\frac{1}{2}, 1\right)$ to be chosen later, by (5.1), we have that (here and after we use $r=|x|$ )

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{2} \Delta\left(|A|^{2 p}\right)= & p|A|^{2 p-1} \Delta|A|+p(2 p-1)|A|^{2 p-2}|\nabla| A\left\|^{2}\right. \\
\geqslant & p|A|^{2 p-1}\left[-|A|^{3}+2 \lambda r^{-2}|A|+|A|^{-1}\left((1-\lambda) \frac{2}{n}\right)|\nabla| A\left\|^{2}\right]\right. \\
& +p(2 p-1)|A|^{2 p-2}|\nabla| A\left\|^{2}\right. \\
= & p\left(2 p-2+\lambda+(1-\lambda)\left(1+\frac{2}{n}\right)\right)|A|^{2 p-2}|\nabla| A\left\|^{2}-p|A|^{2 p+2}+2 p \lambda|A|^{2 p} r^{-2}\right.
\end{aligned}
$$

For a compactly supported radial Lipschitz function $f=f(r)$, plug $f|A|^{p}$ into (2.2) and obtain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \cot \theta \int_{\partial M} A(\eta, \eta)|A|^{2 p} f^{2}+\int_{M}|A|^{2 p+2} f^{2} \leqslant \int_{M}\left|\nabla\left(f|A|^{p}\right)\right|^{2} \\
& =\int_{M} p^{2}|A|^{2 p-2}|\nabla| A\left\|^{2} f^{2}+|A|^{2 p}|\nabla f|^{2}+\frac{1}{2}\left\langle\nabla|A|^{2 p}, \nabla\left(f^{2}\right)\right\rangle\right. \\
& =\int_{M} p^{2}|A|^{2 p-2}|\nabla| A\left\|^{2} f^{2}+|A|^{2 p}|\nabla f|^{2}-\int_{M} f^{2} \frac{1}{2} \Delta\left(|A|^{2 p}\right)+\int_{\partial M} f^{2} \frac{\partial|A|^{2 p}}{\partial \eta}\right.
\end{aligned}
$$

We note that each integral on the right hand side of the above inequality is finite. Here one only needs to check the integral involving $\left|A^{2 p-2}\right||\nabla| A\left\|^{2}\right.$ is finite. To see this, recall that the Simons equation implies that

$$
c(n)|\nabla| A\left\|^{2} \leqslant|A| \Delta|A|+|A|^{4}\right.
$$

for some $c(n)>0$. Thus, we have that

$$
c(n)|A|^{2 p-2}|\nabla| A\left\|^{2} \leqslant|A|^{2 p-1} \Delta|A|+|A|^{2 p+3}\right.
$$

Now both terms on the right hand side are integrable on $M \cap S^{n}(1)$, and hence $|A|^{2 p-2}|\nabla| A\left\|^{2} f^{2}\right.$ is integrable on $M$ if $f$ is compactly supported.

We treat boundary term in (5.3) first. By [21, Lemma C.2], we have that

$$
\left|\frac{\partial|A|}{\partial \eta}\right| \leqslant 3 \sqrt{n-1}|\cot \theta||A|^{2}
$$

Thus, using (5.2) we estimate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left|\cot \theta \int_{\partial M}\right. & A(\eta, \eta)|A|^{2 p} f^{2}-\int_{\partial M} f^{2} \frac{|A|^{2 p}}{\eta} \mid \\
& \leqslant c(n)|\cot \theta| \int_{\partial M} f^{2}|A|^{2 p+1} \\
& \leqslant c(n)\left|\frac{\cot \theta}{\sin \theta}\right| \int_{M}\left|\nabla\left(f^{2}|A|^{2 p+1}\right)\right| \\
& \leqslant c(n)\left|\frac{\cot \theta}{\sin \theta}\right| \int_{M} f^{2}|\nabla| A\left\|^{2}|A|^{2 p-2}+f^{2}|A|^{2 p+2}+|\nabla f|^{2}|A|^{2 p}\right.
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $c(n)$ is a constant that may change from line to line, but depends only on $n$. We have used that $p \leqslant 1$ and the Young's inequality $2 a b<a^{2}+b^{2}$ above.

Plug this into (5.3), and use (5.1), we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 & \leqslant \int_{M}\left((p-1)+c(n)\left|\frac{\cot \theta}{\sin \theta}\right|\right)|A|^{2 p+2} f^{2} \\
& +\int_{M}\left(p^{2}-p((1-\lambda)(1+2 / n)+\lambda+2(p-1))+c(n)\left|\frac{\cot \theta}{\sin \theta}\right|\right)|A|^{2 p-2}|\nabla| A\left\|^{2} f^{2}\right. \\
& \left.+\int_{M}\left(1+c(n)\left|\frac{\cot \theta}{\sin \theta}\right|\right)|A|^{2 p}|\nabla f|^{2}-2 \lambda p|A|^{2 p} f^{2} / r^{2}\right. \\
& =: I+I I+I I I
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\varepsilon>0$ to be chosen later, set

$$
f= \begin{cases}r^{1+\varepsilon} & r \leqslant 1 \\ r^{2-n / 2-\varepsilon} & r>1\end{cases}
$$

Though $f$ is not compactly supported, $\int_{0}^{\infty} r^{n-3-2 p} f(r) d r$ is finite (since $p \in[1 / 2,1]$ and $n \geqslant 2$ ), and thus the right hand side of (5.3) is finite with this choice of $f$. Morever, we see that $I I I$ becomes

$$
\begin{aligned}
I I I= & \int_{M \cap\{r \leqslant 1\}}\left((1+c(n)\left|\frac{\cot \theta}{\sin \theta}\right|)(1+\varepsilon)^{2}-2 \lambda p\right)|A|^{2 p} f^{2} / r^{2} \\
& +\int_{M \cap\{r \geqslant 1\}}\left((1+c(n)\left|\frac{\cot \theta}{\sin \theta}\right|)(2-n / 2-\varepsilon)^{2}-2 \lambda p\right)|A|^{2 p} f^{2} / r^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

If we can make the coefficients in integrals I, II, II all $<0$, it will follow that $A \equiv 0$, proving our Theorem. We therefore seek to find $p \in(1 / 2,1), \varepsilon \in(0,1), \lambda \in(0,1), \theta_{1}>0$ to ensure the following inqualities hold whenever $\theta \in\left(\pi / 2-\theta_{1}, \pi / 2\right]$ and $2 \leqslant n \leqslant 6$ :

$$
\begin{aligned}
& p-1+c(n)\left|\frac{\cot \theta}{\sin \theta}\right|<0 \\
& p^{2}-p((1-\lambda)(1+2 / n)+\lambda+2 p-2)+c(n)\left|\frac{\cot \theta}{\sin \theta}\right|<0 \\
& (1+c(n)\left|\frac{\cot \theta}{\sin \theta}\right|)(1+\varepsilon)^{2}-2 \lambda p<0 \\
& (1+c(n)\left|\frac{\cot \theta}{\sin \theta}\right|)(2-n / 2-\varepsilon)^{2}-2 \lambda p<0
\end{aligned}
$$

One can readily check that when $\theta=\pi / 2$ (and $2 \leqslant n \leqslant 6$ ), then taking $p=1-\varepsilon, \lambda=$ $1-10 \varepsilon, \varepsilon=1 / 100$ will make all of the above inequalities true. Therefore by continuity there is a $\theta_{1}>0$ so that all inequalities remain sharp if $\theta \in\left(\pi / 2-\theta_{1}, \pi / 2\right]$. This finishes the proof.

#### Abstract

The main goal of this article is to expand the theory of invariants of ArtinSchreier curves by giving a complete classification in genus 3 and 4 . To achieve this goal, we first establish standard forms of Artin-Schreier curves and determine all isomorphisms between curves in this form. We then compute reconstructing systems of invariants for curves in each connected component of the strata of the moduli spaces for Artin-Schreier curves of genus 3 and 4 for $p>2$.

## 1. InTRODUCTION

Artin-Schreier theory derives from a 1927 paper of Emil Artin and Otto Schreier characterizing degree $p$ Galois extensions of a field of characteristic $p$ [AS27]. Artin and Schreier proved that such an extension $K(y) / K$ is precisely the splitting field of a polynomial in $y$ of the form $y^{p}-y-\alpha$, where $\alpha \in K$ and $\alpha \neq \beta^{p}-\beta$ for any $\beta \in K$. Such extensions are now referred to as Artin-Schreier extensions, and the varieties defined by the associated polynomials are called Artin-Schreier curves. In this article, we aim to parameterize moduli spaces of Artin-Schreier curves in characteristic $p>2$ by computing invariants of the curves.

It is well-known that elliptic curve isomorphism classes are given by the their $j$-invariants. In genus 2, we can describe isomorphism classes of curves using Igusa invariants [Igu60]. There are also examples of invariant computations in higher genus. For example, there are results for some genus 3 curves in [Shi67, Dix87, Ohn07], hyperelliptic curves in [LR12], Picard curves in [KLS20], Ciani curves in [BCK+21], and others. In general, these calculations are done over the complex numbers, but they usually extend well to fields of large characteristic (genus 2 curves in [Liu93], genus 3 curves in [Bas15], [LLLR21, Sec. 4]). The sources $\left[\mathrm{CFA}^{+} 06\right.$, Sec. 14.5] and [Liu02, Sec. 7.4.3] specifically cover Artin-Schreier curves in characteristic 2, which are hyperelliptic. However, there are still many cases missing from the literature, including "small cases" such as non-hyperelliptic curves of genus $g=3$ in characteristic $p=3$.

Artin-Schreier curves have undergone intense research not only as objects of mathematical interest in their own right, but also for their applications to coding theory (see [vdGvdV91] for example). For certain families of Artin-Scheier curves over a rational function field $K=$ $\mathbb{F}_{q}(x)$, where $q$ is a power of $p$, it is possible to provide point counts, find their automorphism groups, and determine their zeta functions [vdGvdV92], [BHM+16]. Such families frequently contain curves with many points, including maximal curves with respect to the Weil bound. Investigations of Artin-Schreier point counts from the perspective of arithmetic statistics can be found in [Ent12],[BDF+12], [BDFL16].

[^0]
[^0]:    2020 Mathematics Subject Classification. 11G20, 14L24, 13A50, 14D20, 14D22, 14G15, 14H10, 14H37, 14H45, 14Q05.

    Key words and phrases. Artin-Schreier curves, geometric invariant theory, moduli spaces, automorphisms.

In this paper, we compute invariants for Artin-Schreier curves of genus 3 and 4 in characteristic $p>2$. Our work builds on that of Pries and Zhu, who determined the strata and dimensions of irreducible components of the moduli spaces of Artin-Schreier curves [PZ12]. We seek to characterize these moduli spaces concretely by parameterizing them via invariants. Our main ideas are taken from Geometric Invariant Theory (GIT), which was first developed by Mumford in 1965 [Mum65]. This theory provides techniques for forming the "quotient" of an algebraic variety (or scheme) $X$ by a group $G$ and is especially useful for constructing moduli spaces as quotients of schemes parameterizing objects. We provide an overview of invariant theory in Section 2.1 and of Artin-Schreier curves, their moduli spaces, and their isomorphisms in Section 2.2.

As in the genus 1 and 2 cases, it is useful to work with a standard form for Artin-Schreier curves in order to determine their invariants. Section 3 develops a standard form for ArtinSchreier curves. We also describe the isomorphisms between standard forms in this section. In Section 4, we determine reconstructing systems of invariants for all Artin-Schreier curves of genus 3 and 4 for primes $p>2$, as listed in Table 2. This allows us to prove our main result, given in the following theorem.

Theorem 1. A system of reconstructing invariants for all Artin-Schreier curves of genus $g=3,4$ in characteristic $p>2$ is given in Table 1.

For these curves, the strata and dimensions of the irreducible components of their moduli spaces are completely characterized in Table 2. The standard forms (see Theorem 3.3) and invariants listed in Table 1 are derived in the examples in Section 4.

The remainder of this paper is devoted to the proof of Theorem 1. We start by computing sets of invariants directly. We then show that these sets allow us to reconstruct standard forms of Artin-Schreier curves. Finally, standard invariant theoretic results summarized in Section 2 establish that these sets must generate the full invariant ring.
