We conclude, by invariance to support monotonic transformations (from $\bar{P}$ to $P$ ), that for each $l \in\{i, j\}, f_{l}(s, P)=d_{l}^{\left(i, j, \alpha\right)}(s, P)$.
(c) Partial Conflict $\left(a_{i}=a_{j}=a\right.$ and $\left.b_{i} \neq b_{j}\right)$ : By neutrality, it is without loss of generality to suppose that for each $k \in N^{\prime}, \bar{P}_{k} \in \mathcal{P}$ where $\bar{P}_{k}$ ranks $a$ first, $b_{i}$ second, and $b_{j}$ third. Let $\pi^{\prime}=f\left(s,\left(P_{j}, \bar{P}_{N} \backslash\{j\}\right)\right.$. By bounded invariance,

$\pi_{i a}^{\prime}=\bar{\pi}_{i a}=\alpha$ and $\pi_{j a}^{\prime}=\bar{\pi}_{j a}=1-\alpha$. By strategy-proofness and unambiguous efficiency, $\pi_{j b_{j}}^{\prime}=\alpha$. Since $\pi_{i a}^{\prime}>0$, by Lemma $2, \pi_{i b_{i}}^{\prime}=1-\pi_{i a}^{\prime}=1-\alpha$.
Now let $\bar{P} \in \mathcal{P}^{N^{\prime}}$ such that $\bar{P}_{i}=P_{i}, \bar{P}_{j}=P_{j}$, and for each $k \in N^{\prime}, \bar{P}_{k}$ ranks $a$ first, $b_{i}$ second, $b_{j}$ third, and agrees with $P_{k}$ on the remaining objects. Setting $\bar{\pi}=f(s, \bar{P})$, by bounded invariance, if $\bar{\pi}_{i}=\pi_{i}^{\prime}$ and $\bar{\pi}_{j}=\pi_{j}^{\prime}$. Then, by invariance to support monotonic transformations, $f_{i}(s, P)=\pi_{i}^{\prime}=d_{i}^{(i, j, \alpha)}(s, P)$ and $f_{j}(s, P)=$ $\pi_{j}^{\prime}=d_{j}^{(i, j, \alpha)}(s, P)$.

Next, suppose that $\operatorname{int}(s)=0$. Unlike the case of $\operatorname{int}(s)=1$, neutrality does not render it without loss of generality to choose $\bar{P}$ arbitrarily. However, the rest of our properties do ensure that this is the case. We prove first that there is $i \in N^{\prime}$ such that $\bar{\pi}_{i}=m_{i}^{i}(s, \bar{P})$ for any identical preference profile $\bar{P}$. Since $\operatorname{int}(s)=0$ and $\sum_{o \in A} s_{o}=\left|N^{\prime}\right|,|\underline{A}(s)| \leq 2$ implies that $|\underline{A}(s)|=2$. So, $\underline{A}(s)=\{a, b\}$ for some pair $a, b \in A$. Let $c \in A(s) \backslash\{a, b\}$.

Consider $\bar{P} \in \mathcal{P}^{N^{\prime}}$ such that each agent ranks $c$ first, $a$ second, and $b$ third. Let $\bar{\pi}=$ $f(s, \bar{P})$. Since $s_{a}, s_{b} \in(0,1)$, by Lemma 2 and feasibility, there is $i \in N^{\prime}$ such that $\bar{\pi}_{i}=m_{i}^{i}(s, \bar{P})$ so that $\bar{\pi}_{i c}=1$. Even if each agent $j \neq i$ lowers $c$ while keeping the relative rankings of the remaining objects as in $\bar{P}_{j}$, agent $i$ is still assigned probability 1 of $c$ by invariance to support monotonic transformations (denote this statement by $(\dagger)$ ).

Consider another identical preference profile $\bar{P}^{\prime} \in \mathcal{P}^{N^{\prime}}$ such that each agent ranks $a$ first, $c$ second, and $b$ third. Let $\bar{\pi}^{\prime}=f\left(s, \bar{P}^{\prime}\right)$. We show that $\bar{\pi}_{i}^{\prime}=m_{i}^{i}\left(s, \bar{P}^{\prime}\right)$ so that $\bar{\pi}_{i a}^{\prime}=s_{a}$ and $\bar{\pi}_{i c}^{\prime}=1-s_{a}$. Consider $\left(\bar{P}_{i}^{\prime}, \bar{P}_{N^{\prime} \backslash\{i\}}\right)$ and compare it with $\bar{P}$. Let $\bar{\pi}=f\left(s,\left(\bar{P}_{j}^{\prime}, \bar{P}_{N^{\prime} \backslash\{i\}}\right)\right)$. By strategy-proof, $\bar{\pi}_{i b}=0$ and $\bar{\pi}_{i a}+\bar{\pi}_{i c}=1$. If $\bar{\pi}_{i a}<s_{a}$, then $\bar{\pi}_{i c}>0$ and by feasibility, there is $j \neq i$ with $\bar{\pi}_{j a}>0$. Agent $i$ prefers $a$ to $c$ and agent $j$ prefers $c$ to $a$, contradicting unambiguous efficiency. Therefore, $\bar{\pi}_{i a}=s_{a}$ and $\bar{\pi}_{i c}=1-s_{a}$. Since all other agents have the same preferences, by Lemma 2 and feasibility, there is $k \in N^{\prime}$ such that $\bar{\pi}_{k c}=s_{a}$ and $\bar{\pi}_{k b}=1-s_{a}$. Now, consider $\left(\bar{P}_{i k}^{\prime}, \bar{P}_{N^{\prime} \backslash\{i, k\}}\right)$ and compare it with $\left(\bar{P}_{i}^{\prime}, \bar{P}_{N^{\prime} \backslash\{i\}}\right)$. Let $\bar{\pi}^{\prime}=f\left(s,\left(\bar{P}_{i k}^{\prime}, \bar{P}_{N^{\prime} \backslash\{i, k\}}\right)\right)$. By strategy-proofness, $\bar{\pi}_{k b}^{\prime}=1-s_{a}$ and $\bar{\pi}_{k a}^{\prime}+\bar{\pi}_{k c}^{\prime}=s_{a}$. Also, by strategy-proonfess, $\bar{\pi}_{i a}^{\prime}+\bar{\pi}_{i c}^{\prime}=1$, because if agent $i$ changes their preference to $\bar{P}_{i}$ at $\left(\bar{P}_{i k}^{\prime}, \bar{P}_{N^{\prime} \backslash\{i, k\}}\right)$, they will be assigned probability 1 of $c$ by $(\dagger)$. We claim that $\bar{\pi}_{k a}^{\prime}=0$. Otherwise, both $i$ and $k$ rank $a, c$ and $b$ in order whereas $\bar{\pi}_{k a}^{\prime}, \bar{\pi}_{k b}^{\prime}, \bar{\pi}_{i c}^{\prime}>0$, contradicting Lemma 2. Altogether, $\bar{\pi}_{i a}^{\prime}=s_{a}$ and $\bar{\pi}_{i c}^{\prime}=1-s_{a}$. Since each other agent $l$ is assigned zero probabilities of $a, b$, and $c, \bar{P}_{l}^{\prime}$ is a support monotonic transformation of $\bar{P}_{l}$ at $\bar{\pi}_{l}^{\prime}$. Therefore, $f\left(s, \bar{P}^{\prime}\right)=\bar{\pi}^{\prime}$. By the same argument, agent $i$ is the monarch at the identical preference profile such that each agent ranks $b$ first, $c$ second, and $a$ third.

Lastly, consider an identical preference profile $\bar{P}^{\prime \prime} \in \mathcal{P}^{N^{\prime}}$ such that each agent ranks $a$ first, $b$ second, and $c$ third. Let $\bar{\pi}^{\prime \prime}=f\left(s, \bar{P}^{\prime \prime}\right)$. We show that the same agent $i$ receives

$\bar{\pi}_{i}^{\prime \prime}=m_{i}^{i}\left(s, \bar{P}^{\prime \prime}\right)$ so that $\bar{\pi}_{i a}^{\prime \prime}=s_{a}$ and $\bar{\pi}_{i b}^{\prime \prime}=1-s_{a}$. Consider $\left(\bar{P}_{i}^{\prime \prime}, \bar{P}_{N^{\prime} \backslash\{i\}}^{\prime \prime}\right)$ and compare it with $\bar{P}^{\prime}$. Let $\bar{\pi}=f\left(s,\left(\bar{P}_{i}^{\prime \prime}, \bar{P}_{N^{\prime} \backslash\{i\}}^{\prime}\right)\right)$. By bounded invariance and unambiguous efficiency, $\bar{\pi}_{i a}=s_{a}$ and $\bar{\pi}_{i b}=1-s_{a}$. Choose any $j \in N \backslash\{i\}$, consider $\left(\bar{P}_{i j}^{\prime \prime}, \bar{P}_{N^{\prime} \backslash\{i, j\}}^{\prime \prime}\right)$, and let $\bar{\pi}^{\prime}=f\left(s,\left(\bar{P}_{i j}^{\prime \prime}, \bar{P}_{N^{\prime} \backslash\{i, j\}}^{\prime}\right)\right)$. By $(\dagger)$ and strategy-proofness, $\bar{\pi}_{i a}^{\prime}+\bar{\pi}_{i b}^{\prime}+\bar{\pi}_{i c}^{\prime}=1$. By bounded invariance, $\bar{\pi}_{i a}=\bar{\pi}_{i a}^{\prime}=s_{a}$. We claim that $\bar{\pi}_{i c}^{\prime}=0$. Otherwise, $\bar{\pi}_{i c}^{\prime}>0$ and $\bar{\pi}_{i b}^{\prime}<1-s_{a}=s_{b}$. By feasibility, there exists $k \in N \backslash\{i\}$ such that $\bar{\pi}_{k b}^{\prime}>0$. If $k=j$, both agents $i$ and $j$ (with $\bar{P}_{i}^{\prime \prime}=\bar{P}_{j}^{\prime \prime}$ ) rank $a, b, c$, in order and $\bar{\pi}_{i a}^{\prime}, \bar{\pi}_{i c}^{\prime}, \bar{\pi}_{j b}^{\prime}>0$, contradicting Lemma 2. If $k \neq j$, agent $i$ (with $\bar{P}_{i}^{\prime \prime}$ ) prefers $b$ to $c$, agent $k$ (with $\bar{P}_{k}^{\prime}$ ) prefers $c$ to $b$ and $\bar{\pi}_{i c}^{\prime}, \bar{\pi}_{k b}^{\prime}>0$, contradicting unambiguous efficiency. Altogether, $\bar{\pi}_{i c}^{\prime}=0$ and therefore, $\bar{\pi}_{i b}^{\prime}=1-s_{a}$. Next, from $\left(\bar{P}_{i j}^{\prime \prime}, \bar{P}_{N^{\prime} \backslash\{i, j\}}^{\prime}\right)$, choose any other $l \in N^{\prime} \backslash\{i, j\}$ and change $\bar{P}_{l}^{\prime}$ to $\bar{P}_{l}^{\prime \prime}$. By the same argument as above, we conclude that agent $i$ receives $s_{a}$ and $s_{b}$ of $a$ and $b$, respectively. Repeat this process until we reach $\bar{P}^{\prime \prime}$. We then obtain $f_{i}\left(s, \bar{P}^{\prime \prime}\right)=\bar{\pi}_{i}$. By the same argument, agent $i$ is the monarch at the identical preference profile such that each agent ranks $b$ first, $a$ second, and $c$ third.

We have now established that for any ordering of $a, b$, and $c$, at the top of an identical preference profile, $i$ is the monarch. By neutrality, $c$ can be any object other than $a$ or $b$. Thus, we have shown this as long as $a$ and $b$ are among the top three objects in $A(s)$. From here, bounded invariance ensures that $i$ is the monarch for all identical profiles.

Now, we turn to $P$ and show $f_{i}(s, P)=m_{i}^{i}(s, P)$. Let $\bar{P} \in \mathcal{P}^{N^{\prime}}$ be an identical preference profile such that, without loss of generality, for each $k \in N^{\prime}, \bar{P}_{k}=P_{i}$ and let $\bar{\pi}=f(\bar{P})$. Let $\bar{A}=\left\{o \in \operatorname{supp}\left(\bar{\pi}_{i}\right): \bar{\pi}_{i o}=s_{o}\right\}$. If either $i$ ranks $a$ and $b$ above the other objects in $A(s)$ at $P_{i}$, or $i$ ranks another object $c$ distinct from $a$ and $b$ on top at $P_{i}$, then $\operatorname{supp}\left(\bar{\pi}_{i}\right)=\bar{A}$. If $i$ ranks one of $a$ or $b$ first and $c \notin\{a, b\}$ second at $P_{i}$, then $\operatorname{supp}\left(\bar{\pi}_{i}\right) \neq \bar{A}$.

Let $\bar{P} \in \mathcal{P}^{N^{\prime}}$ such that for each $k \neq i$ and each pair $c, d \in A(s), c \bar{P}_{k} d$ if and only if either

1. $c \in \bar{A}$ and $d \notin \bar{A}$,
2. $c, d \in \bar{A}$ and $c P_{k} d$, or
3. $c, d \in A(s) \backslash \bar{A}$ and $c P_{k} d$.

Let $\bar{\pi}=f\left(s,\left(P_{i}, \bar{P}_{N^{\prime} \backslash\{i\}}\right)\right.$. Since $f_{i}\left(s,\left(P_{i}, \bar{P}_{N^{\prime} \backslash\{i\}}\right)\right)=\bar{\pi}_{i}$, by bounded invariance, for each $o \in \bar{A}$, $\bar{\pi}_{i o}=\bar{\pi}_{i o}$. There are two cases to consider.
(i) If $\operatorname{supp}\left(\bar{\pi}_{i}\right)=\bar{A}$, then for each $a \in \bar{A}$, and each $j \in N \backslash\{i\}, \bar{\pi}_{j a}=0$, so $P_{j}$ is a support monotonic transformation of $\bar{P}_{j}$ at $\bar{\pi}_{j}$.
(ii) If $\operatorname{supp}\left(\bar{\pi}_{i}\right) \neq \bar{A}$, then there are $o \in \bar{A}$ and $o^{\prime} \in A(s) \backslash \bar{A}$ with $s_{o^{\prime}}=1$, such that $o$ and $o^{\prime}$ are the best and second best objects in $A(s)$ according to $P_{i}$ respectively. By bounded invariance, $\bar{\pi}_{i o}=\bar{\pi}_{i o}$. We show that $\bar{\pi}_{i o^{\prime}}=\bar{\pi}_{i o^{\prime}}$. Otherwise, by feasibility, there is

some $o^{\prime \prime} \in A(s)$ such that $o^{\prime} P_{i} o^{\prime \prime}$ and $\tilde{\pi}_{i o^{\prime \prime}}>0$. Moreover, there is $j \in N^{\prime}$ such that $\tilde{\pi}_{j o^{\prime}}>0$. If $o^{\prime} \tilde{P}_{j} o^{\prime \prime}$, then we have contradicted Lemma 2. If $o^{\prime \prime} \tilde{P}_{j} o^{\prime}$, then we have contradicted unambiguous efficiency. Thus, $\tilde{\pi}_{i}=\bar{\pi}_{i}$. Since for each $j \in N^{\prime} \backslash\{i\}, \tilde{P}_{j}$ differs from $P_{j}$ only in the position of $o$ and $\tilde{\pi}_{j o}=0$, we then have that $P_{j}$ is a support monotonic transformation of $\tilde{P}_{j}$ at $\tilde{\pi}_{j}$.

Thus, by invariance to support monotonic transformations, $f(s, P)=\tilde{\pi}$, so $f_{i}(s, P)=$ $m_{i}^{t}(s, P)$.

Proof of Theorem 1. From Lemma 7, it follows that there is $\sigma$ such that $\varphi$ coincides with $\varphi^{\sigma}$ where for each $\eta \in \mathcal{H}^{N T}, \sigma(\eta) \in \mathcal{D}$.

To complete the proof that any $\varphi$ satisfying the listed properties is a hierarchy of monarchies and diarchies, it remains to show that $\sigma$ is equal for equivalent histories.

We prove, by induction on histories along possible execution paths, that $\sigma$ is equal for equivalent histories. The base case for the induction, that $\sigma(()) \in \mathcal{D}$ is implied by Lemma 7. For the induction step, for some $t>0$, consider the $t^{\text {th }}$ recursive call to $\Psi$ with histories $\eta$ and $\eta^{\prime}$, residual supplies $s$ and $s^{\prime}$, respectively. Let $P, P^{\prime} \in \mathcal{P}^{N}$ be a pair of preference profiles that produce the equivalent histories $\eta$ and $\eta^{\prime}$ respectively up to this call. Let $\pi^{k}=\sigma(\eta)(s, P)$ and $\pi^{\prime k}=\sigma\left(\eta^{\prime}\right)\left(s^{\prime}, P^{\prime}\right)$. Let $\bar{s}=r\left(s, \pi^{k}\right)$ and $\bar{s}^{\prime}=r\left(s^{\prime}, \pi^{\prime k}\right)$. If $\sigma$ is not equal for equivalent histories, then there would be such $P$ and $P^{\prime}$ where:

- the same agents are assigned objects from both $\pi^{k}$ and $\pi^{\prime k}$,
- those agents have the same integrality to their allocations from both $\pi^{k}$ and $\pi^{\prime k}$
- and both $\bar{s}$ and $\bar{s}^{\prime}$ have the same integrality,
but $\sigma\left(\eta++\pi^{k}\right)(\bar{s}, P) \neq \sigma\left(\eta^{\prime}++\pi^{\prime k}\right)\left(\bar{s}^{\prime}, P^{\prime}\right)$. Denote by $\bar{N}=N \backslash N\left(\eta++\pi^{k}\right)\left(=N \backslash N\left(\eta^{\prime}++\pi^{\prime k}\right)\right)$ the set of agents who have not been assigned any object at $\eta++\pi^{k}$ (and $\left(\eta^{\prime}++\pi^{\prime k}\right)$ ). Let $\bar{P} \in \mathcal{P}^{N}$ such that (i) for each $i \notin \bar{N}, \bar{P}_{i}=P_{i}$ and (ii) for each $i \in \bar{N}, \bar{P}_{i}=\bar{P}_{0}$ where for each $a, b \in A, a \bar{P}_{0} b$ if and only if $\bar{s}_{a} \geq \bar{s}_{b}$. That is, all agents in $\bar{N}$ have an identical preference ordering, which ranks the objects in the weakly decreasing order of supplies of the objects at $\bar{s}$ (that is, the objects with integral supplies on top, those with fractional supplies next, and unavailable objects at the bottom). Let $\bar{\pi}=\varphi^{\sigma}(\bar{P})$. By Lemma $7, \sigma\left(\eta++\pi^{k}\right)(\bar{s}, \bar{P})=$ $\sigma\left(\eta++\pi^{k}\right)(\bar{s}, P)$ and for each $i \notin \bar{N}, \bar{\pi}_{i}=\varphi_{i}^{\sigma}(P)$. Note that this allocation is invariant to the relative rankings over $A \backslash A(\bar{s})$ at $\bar{P}_{0}$. Similarly, let $\bar{P}^{\prime} \in \mathcal{P}^{N}$ such that (i) for each $i \notin \bar{N}$, $\bar{P}_{i}^{\prime}=P_{i}^{\prime}$ and (ii) for each $i \in \bar{N}, \bar{P}_{i}^{\prime}=\bar{P}_{0}^{\prime}$ where for each $a, b \in A, a \bar{P}_{0}^{\prime} b$ if and only if $\bar{s}_{a}^{\prime} \geq \bar{s}_{b}^{\prime}$. Let $\bar{\pi}^{\prime}=\varphi^{\sigma}\left(\bar{P}^{\prime}\right)$. By the same reasoning, $\sigma\left(\eta^{\prime}++\pi^{\prime k}\right)\left(\bar{s}^{\prime}, \bar{P}^{\prime}\right)=\sigma\left(\eta^{\prime}++\pi^{\prime k}\right)\left(\bar{s}^{\prime}, P^{\prime}\right)$ and for each $i \notin \bar{N}, \bar{\pi}_{i}^{\prime}=\varphi_{i}^{\sigma}\left(P^{\prime}\right)$. Again, this allocation is invariant to the relative rankings over $A \backslash A\left(\bar{s}^{\prime}\right)$ at $\bar{P}_{0}^{\prime}$. Since $\sigma\left(\eta++\pi^{k}\right)(\bar{s}, P) \neq \sigma\left(\eta^{\prime}++\pi^{\prime k}\right)\left(\bar{s}^{\prime}, P^{\prime}\right)$, we have $\sigma\left(\eta++\pi^{k}\right)(\bar{s}, \bar{P}) \neq \sigma\left(\eta^{\prime}++\pi^{\prime k}\right)\left(\bar{s}^{\prime}, \bar{P}^{\prime}\right)$.

We further modify $\bar{P}$ into $\overline{\bar{P}} \in \mathcal{P}^{N}$ such that (i) for each $i \in \bar{N}, \overline{\bar{P}}_{i}=\bar{P}_{i}$ and (ii) for each $i \notin \bar{N}$, each $a \in \operatorname{supp}\left(\bar{\pi}_{i}\right)$ and each $b \notin \operatorname{supp}\left(\bar{\pi}_{i}\right), a \overline{\bar{P}}_{i} b$ and

$$
\left.\overline{\bar{P}}_{i}\right|_{\operatorname{supp}\left(\bar{\pi}_{i}\right)}=\left.\bar{P}_{i}\right|_{\operatorname{supp}\left(\bar{\pi}_{i}\right)} \text { and }\left.\overline{\bar{P}}_{i}\right|_{A \backslash \operatorname{supp}\left(\bar{\pi}_{i}\right)}=\left.\bar{P}_{0}\right|_{A \backslash \operatorname{supp}\left(\bar{\pi}_{i}\right)}
$$

For each $i \in N, \overline{\bar{P}}_{i}$ is a support monotonic transformation of $\bar{P}_{i}$ at $\bar{\pi}_{i}$, and thus, by invariance to support monotonic transformation, $\varphi^{\sigma}(\overline{\bar{P}})=\bar{\pi}$. Similarly, let $\overline{\bar{P}}^{\prime} \in \mathcal{P}^{N}$ such that for each $i \in \bar{N}, \overline{\bar{P}}_{i}^{\prime}=\bar{P}_{i}^{\prime}$ and for each $i \notin \bar{N}$, each $a \in \operatorname{supp}\left(\bar{\pi}_{i}^{\prime}\right)$ and each $b \notin \operatorname{supp}\left(\bar{\pi}_{i}^{\prime}\right), a \overline{\bar{P}}_{i}^{\prime} b$ and

$$
\left.\overline{\bar{P}}_{i}^{\prime}\right|_{\operatorname{supp}\left(\bar{\pi}_{i}^{\prime}\right)}=\left.\bar{P}_{i}^{\prime}\right|_{\operatorname{supp}\left(\bar{\pi}_{i}^{\prime}\right)} \text { and }\left.\overline{\bar{P}}_{i}^{\prime}\right|_{A \backslash \operatorname{supp}\left(\bar{\pi}_{i}^{\prime}\right)}=\left.\bar{P}_{0}^{\prime}\right|_{A \backslash \operatorname{supp}\left(\bar{\pi}_{i}^{\prime}\right)}
$$

By the same reasoning, $\varphi^{\sigma}\left(\overline{\bar{P}}^{\prime}\right)=\bar{\pi}^{\prime}$. Finally, $\overline{\bar{P}}$ and $\overline{\bar{P}}^{\prime}$ are ordinally equivalent and identical up to relabelling objects, but $\overline{\bar{\pi}}$ and $\overline{\bar{\pi}}^{\prime}$ are not identical up to the same relabelling. This contradicts neutrality.

We now show that a hierarchy of monarchies and diarchies with respect to $\sigma, \varphi^{\sigma}$, satisfies the properties listed in the statement of Theorem 1.

- Unambiguous Efficiency: Given an input $P \in \mathcal{P}^{N}$, let $\eta=\left(\pi^{k}\right)_{k=1}^{K} \in \mathcal{H}^{T}$ be the history accumulated along the execution path of a hierarchy of monarchies and diarchies, $\varphi^{\sigma}$. Let $\pi=\varphi^{\sigma}(P)$. For each $t \in\{1, \cdots, K\}$ and each $i \in N\left(\pi^{t}\right), \pi_{i}$ beats every lottery in

$$
\left\{\bar{\pi}_{i} \in \Delta(A): \text { for each } a \in A, \bar{\pi}_{i a} \leq \vec{1}-\sum_{\substack{k \in N\left(\pi^{t^{\prime}}\right) \backslash\{i\}} \pi_{j a}\right\}
$$

according to $R_{i}^{s d}$. If $N\left(\pi^{t}\right)=\{i\}$, this means for any allocation $\pi^{\prime}$ such that it is not the case that $\pi_{i} R_{i}^{s d} \pi_{i}^{\prime}$, there is $j \in N\left(\pi^{t^{\prime}}\right)$ where $t^{\prime}<t$ such that $\pi_{j} P_{j}^{s d} \pi_{j}^{\prime}$.
If there is $j \in N\left(\pi^{t}\right) \backslash\{i\}$, by definition of $\sigma, \vec{1}-\sum_{\substack{k \in N\left(\pi^{t^{\prime}}\right) \\ t^{\prime}<t}} \pi_{k}$ is integral. By definition of a diarchy, this means $P_{i}$ and $P_{j}$ have the same best object $a_{1}$ in $A\left(\overrightarrow{1}-\sum_{\substack{k \in N\left(\pi^{t^{\prime}}\right) \\ t^{\prime}<t}} \pi_{k}\right)$. Any allocation that assigns more of $a_{1}$ to $i$ necessarily makes $j$ worse off according to $P_{j}$. Also by definition of a diarchy, if $a_{2}$ is the second best object in $A\left(\overrightarrow{1}-\sum_{\substack{k \in N\left(\pi^{t^{\prime}}\right) \\ t^{\prime}<t}} \pi_{k}\right)$ according to $P_{i}$, then $\pi_{i a_{2}}=1-\pi_{i a_{1}}$. Thus, for any $\pi^{\prime}$ such that it is not the case that $\pi_{i} R_{i}^{s d} \pi_{i}^{\prime}$, there is $k \in N\left(\pi^{t^{\prime}}\right)$ where $t^{\prime} \leq t$ such that $\pi_{k} P_{k}^{s d} \pi_{k}^{\prime}$.

- Neutrality: The definition of a hierarchy of monarchies and diarchies makes no references to the labels of the objects. Therefore, such rules are, by definition, neutral.

- Strategy-proofness: Let $P \in \mathcal{P}^{N}$ and $\pi=\varphi^{\sigma}(P)$. Let $i \in N$. At a history $\eta=\left(\pi^{k}\right)_{k=1}^{i}$, where $\sigma(\eta)=m^{i}$ or $\sigma(\eta)=d^{(i, j, \alpha)}$ for some $j \in N$ and $\alpha \in(0,1)$, $i$ 's assignment is chosen to be the best allocation, according to $P_{i}^{\text {sd }}$, in

$$
\left\{\bar{\pi}_{i} \in \Delta(A): \text { for each } a \in A, \bar{\pi}_{i a} \leq \overrightarrow{1}-\sum_{\substack{k \in N\left(\pi^{i^{\prime}}\right) \backslash\{i\} \\ t^{\prime} \leq t}} \pi_{k a}\right\}
$$

If $\sigma(\eta)=m^{i}$, this option set is unresponsive to $i$ 's reported preferences. If $\sigma(\eta)=$ $d^{(i, j, \alpha)}$, this option set only depends on whether $i$ 's reported top object in $A(\overrightarrow{1}-$ $\left.\sum_{\substack{k \in N\left(\pi^{i^{\prime}}\right) \\ t^{\prime}<t}} \pi_{k}\right)$ coincides with that of $j$. Let $a_{1}$ be the top object in this set according to $P_{j}$. If $a_{1}$ is also the top object in this set according to $i$ 's report, $P_{i}$, then $\pi_{j a_{1}}=1-\alpha$. If $a_{1}$ is not the top object in this set according to $P_{i}$, then $\pi_{j a_{1}}=1$. Thus, if $a_{1}$ is the true top object in this set for $i, i$ receives it with probability $\alpha$ if they report it as such, but receives it with probability zero if they do not.

- Non-bossiness: Given an input preference profile $P \in \mathcal{P}^{N}$, let $\eta=\left(\pi^{k}\right)_{k=1}^{K} \in \mathcal{H}^{T}$ be the history accumulated along the execution path of a hierarchy of monarchies and diarchies, $\varphi^{\sigma}$. Let $\pi=\varphi^{\sigma}(P)$.
Let $i \in N$ and $\bar{P}_{i} \in \mathcal{P}$ be such that $\varphi_{i}^{\sigma}\left(\bar{P}_{i}, P_{-i}\right)=\varphi_{i}^{\sigma}(P)$. Let $\bar{\eta}=\left(\bar{\pi}^{k}\right)_{k=1}^{L} \in \mathcal{H}^{T}$ be the history accumulated along the execution path of the hierarchy at $\left(\bar{P}_{i}, P_{-i}\right)$.
Suppose $i \in N\left(\pi^{t}\right)$. By definition of $\varphi^{\sigma}$, for each $t^{\prime}<t, \pi^{t^{\prime}}=\bar{\pi}^{t^{\prime}}$, and for each $j \in N\left(\pi^{t^{\prime}}\right), \varphi_{j}^{\sigma}\left(\bar{P}_{i}, P_{-i}\right)=\varphi_{j}^{\sigma}(P)$. Since $\varphi_{i}^{\sigma}\left(\bar{P}_{i}, P_{-i}\right)=\varphi_{i}^{\sigma}(P), \pi^{t}=\bar{\pi}^{t}$. Thus, $\sigma\left(\pi^{1}, \ldots, \pi^{t}\right)=\sigma\left(\bar{\pi}^{1}, \ldots, \bar{\pi}^{t}\right)$. Since the residual supply vector is the same, $\pi^{t+1}=\bar{\pi}^{t+1}$ and for each $j \in N\left(\pi^{t+1}\right), \varphi_{j}^{\sigma}\left(\bar{P}_{i}, P_{-i}\right)=\varphi_{j}^{\sigma}(P)$. Repeating this argument, we conclude that $\varphi^{\sigma}\left(\bar{P}_{i}, P_{-i}\right)=\varphi^{\sigma}(P)$.
- Bounded invariance: Let $P \in \mathcal{P}^{N}, i \in N, a \in A$ and $\bar{P}_{i} \in \mathcal{P}$ such that $U\left(P_{i}, a\right)=U\left(\bar{P}_{i}, a\right)$ and $\left.\bar{P}_{i}\right|_{U\left(P_{i}, a\right)}=\left.P_{i}\right|_{U\left(P_{i}, a\right)}$, where $U\left(P_{i}, a\right)=\left\{b \in A: b P_{i} a\right\}$ and $U\left(\bar{P}_{i}, a\right)=\left\{b \in A: b \bar{P}_{i} a\right\}$. Let $\pi=\varphi^{\sigma}(P)$. Let the history along the execution path at $P$ be $\eta=\left(\pi^{1}, \ldots, \pi^{K}\right)$ and at $\left(\bar{P}_{i}, P_{-i}\right)$ be $\bar{\eta}=\left(\bar{\pi}^{1}, \ldots, \bar{\pi}^{L}\right)$. If $i \in N\left(\pi^{t}\right)$, then, as argued above, for all $t^{\prime}<t$, $\pi^{t^{\prime}}=\bar{\pi}^{t^{\prime}}$ and for each $j \in N\left(\pi^{t^{\prime}}\right), \varphi_{j}^{\sigma}\left(\bar{P}_{i}, P_{-i}\right)=\varphi_{j}^{\sigma}(P)$.
Under the profile $P$, at the $t^{\text {th }}$ call to $\Psi$, if the residual supply of $a$ is zero, then it is zero at $\left(\bar{P}_{i}, P_{-i}\right)$ as well and in both cases $a$ is allocated only to agents in $N\left(\pi^{1}, \ldots \pi^{t-1}\right)$. As we have shown above, for each such $j, \varphi_{j}^{\sigma}\left(\bar{P}_{i}, P_{-i}\right)=\varphi_{j}^{\sigma}(P)$.
If $\varphi_{i a}^{\sigma}(P)=1$, then $a$ is the best object that has positive availability at the $t^{\text {th }}$ call

according to $P_{i}$. Since the residual supply is the same with input $\left(\bar{P}_{i}, P_{-i}\right)$, and since $\left.\bar{P}_{i}\right|_{U\left(P_{i}, a\right)}=\left.P_{i}\right|_{U\left(P_{i}, a\right)}, a$ is also the best object that has positive availability according to $\bar{P}_{i}$, so $\varphi_{i a}^{\sigma}\left(\bar{P}_{i}, P_{-i}\right)=1$. By non-bossiness, $\varphi^{\sigma}\left(\bar{P}_{i}, P_{-i}\right)=\varphi^{\sigma}(P)$.
If the residual supply of $a$ at the $t^{\text {th }}$ call at $P$ is positive, but $\varphi_{i a}^{\sigma}(P)=0$, then $\operatorname{supp}\left(\varphi_{i}^{\sigma}(P)\right) \subseteq U\left(P_{i}, a\right)$. So, since $\left.\bar{P}_{i}\right|_{U\left(P_{i}, a\right)}=\left.P_{i}\right|_{U\left(P_{i}, a\right)},\left.\varphi_{i}^{\sigma}\left(\bar{P}_{i}, P_{-i}\right)=\varphi_{i}^{\sigma}(P)\right.$ and we conclude by non-bossiness.

Finally, if the residual supply of $a$ at the $t^{\text {th }}$ call at $P$ is positive, and $0<\varphi_{i a}^{\sigma}(P)<1$, then $a$ is either the best object or the second best object according to $P_{i}$ among those with positive residual supplies. If $a$ is the best object at $P_{i}$, then by definition of $\varphi^{\sigma}, a$ is allocated entirely to the agents in $N\left(\pi^{1}, \ldots, \pi^{t}\right)$, regardless of whether $a$ is allocated to a monarchy or to a diarchy at the $t^{\text {th }}$ call at $P$. Since $a$ is still $i$ 's best object at the $t^{\text {th }}$ call at $\bar{P}_{i}$, the allocation of $a$ is the same at both $P$ and $\left(\bar{P}_{i}, P_{-i}\right)$. If $a$ is the second best object at $P_{i}$, then there are two cases. If $a$ 's supply is exhausted at the $t^{\text {th }}$ call at $P$, then the same argument as above applies. So, suppose that $a$ 's supply is not exhausted at the $t^{\text {th }}$ call at $P$. Since $i$ 's preference remains the same down to $a$ at $\bar{P}_{i}$ as in $P_{i}$, the allocation of $t^{\text {th }}$ call coincides at $P$ and $\left(\bar{P}_{i}, P_{-i}\right)$. By definition of $\varphi^{\sigma}$, the subsequent selections by $\sigma$ do not change, keeping the allocation of $a$ unchanged.

# D Independence of all but bounded invariance 

First, a mechanism selecting the random serial dictatorship allocation for each profile satisfies all the properties but unambiguous efficiency. Second, suppose that each object is endowed a strict priority ordering over agents. The (deterministic) Boston mechanism (also known as the "immediate acceptance mechanism") under the profile of priorities satisfies all the properties but strategy-proofness. Third, consider a mechanism that (i) selects the serial dictatorship allocation with respect to 1-2-3-4 $\cdots \cdots n$ if agent 1's favorite object is $a$ and (ii) selects the serial dictatorship allocation with respect to 1-3-2-4 $\cdots \cdots n$, otherwise. This mechanism satisfies all the properties but neutrality. Fourth, consider a mechanism that assigns degenerate lotteries as follows: for each profile, it allocates agent 1 their favorite object, say $a_{1}$, with probability 1 ; among the remaining objects, (i) if agent 2 's favorite object is not $a_{1}$, it mechanism selects the serial dictatorship with respect to 2-3-4 $\cdots \cdots n$; (ii) if agent 2 's favorite object is $a_{1}$, the it selects the serial dictatorship with respect to $2-4-3 \cdots \cdots n$. This mechanism satisfies all the properties but non-bossiness.

#### Abstract

Adhesive and quasiadhesive categories provide a general framework for the study of algebraic graph rewriting systems. In a quasiadhesive category any two regular subobjects have a join which is again a regular subobject. Vice versa, if regular monos are adhesive, then the existence of a regular join for any pair of regular subobjects entails quasiadhesivity. It is also known that (quasi)adhesive categories can be embedded in a Grothendieck topos via a functor preserving pullbacks and pushouts along (regular) monos.

In this paper we extend these results to $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive categories, a concept which generalizes the notion of (quasi)adhesivity. We introduce the notion of $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive morphism, which allows us to express $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesivity as a condition on the subobjects' posets. Moreover, $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive morphisms allows us to show how an $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive category can be embedded into a Grothendieck topos, preserving pullbacks and $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-pushouts.

## 1. INTRODUCTION

Since their introduction [LS05], adhesive and quasiadhesive categories have provided a powerful and elegant framework for studying and guaranteeing useful properties of algebraic graph rewriting systems [BHK22, CMR ${ }^{+} 97$, EEPT06]. Besides being useful for the study of rewriting systems, adhesivity and quasiadhesivity entail other properties of the underlying categories [JLS07, LS05]. In a quasiadhesive category any two regular subobjects (i.e. subobjects represented by a regular mono) have a join which is again a regular subobject. Vice versa, if regular monos are adhesive, then the existence of a regular join for any pair of regular subobjects entails quasiadhesivity [GL12]. Moreover, any (quasi)adhesive category can be embedded in a Grothendieck topos via a functor preserving pullbacks and pushouts along (regular) monomorphisms [GL12, Lac11], thus justifying the slogan that "a category is (quasi)adhesive if pushouts of (regular) monomorphisms exist and interact with pullbacks as they do in a topos".

In order to deal with situations with other conditions on the rewriting rules or on the allowed matchings, adhesivity and quasiadhesivity have been generalized with the introduction of $\mathcal{M}$ - and $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive categories [ACR19, CGM22, HP12, PH16], where $\mathcal{M}$ and $\mathcal{N}$ are various classes of monomorphisms. While results concerning rewriting, like the Church-Rosser Theorem and the Parallelism Theorem, can be proved also in this

Key words and phrases: graph rewriting, adhesive categories, DPO approach.

setting, a study of the consequences on the underlying categorical structure entailed by $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesivity is lacking.

To this end, in this paper we first establish a relationship between $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesivity and the existence of some binary suprema in the poset of subobjects of a given object. Generalizing the approach of [GL12], we introduce the notion of $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive morphism; then we show that, if $\mathcal{M}$ and $\mathcal{N}$ are nice enough, $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesivity entails the existence of suprema for some pairs of subobjects; vice versa, the existence of these suprema, together with every arrow in $\mathcal{M}$ being $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive, is enough to guarantee $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesivity.

Moreover, the framework of $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive morphisms allows us to generalize also the embedding results provided in [GL12, Lac11]. As in the (quasi)adhesive cases, and under some hypotheses on the classes $\mathcal{M}$ and $\mathcal{N}$, an $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive category admits a full and faithful functor into a Grothendieck topos, which preserves pullbacks and $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-pushouts. Thus, this suggests the new slogan that "a category is $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive if $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-pushouts exist and interact with pullbacks as they do in a topos".

Synopsis. In Section 2 we will recall the notion of Van Kampen square and the definition of $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive category, showing how it relates with $\mathcal{M}$-adhesivity and (quasi)adhesivity. Section 3 introduces the notion of $\mathcal{N}$-(pre)adhesive morphism, which is used in Section 4 to provide a formula to compute certain suprema in the poset of subobjects of an $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-category. Section 5 goes on the other direction: starting from the existence of certain suprema we can prove $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesivity. Using the results of these sections, in Section 6 we will show that, under some hypotheses on $\mathcal{M}$ and $\mathcal{N}$, every $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive category can be embedded in a Grothendieck topos via a functor preserving pullbacks and $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-pushouts. Conclusions and suggestions for further work are in Section 7.

For the sake of brevity, proofs of straightforward results are omitted, but they can be found in the extended version of this paper [CM24].

# 2. $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-ADHESIVE CATEGORIES 

In this section we recall the basic definitions and results about $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive categories. We begin with some notational conventions which will be used throughout this paper.

- Given a category $\mathbf{X}$ we will not distinguish notationally between $\mathbf{X}$ and its class of objects: so that " $X \in \mathbf{X}$ " means that $X$ belongs to the class of objects of $\mathbf{X}$.
- If 1 is a terminal object in a category $\mathbf{X}$, the unique arrow $X \rightarrow 1$ from another object $X$ will be denoted by $!_{X}$. Similarly, if 0 is initial in $\mathbf{X}$ then $?_{X}$ will denote the unique arrow $0 \rightarrow X$. Moreover, when $\mathbf{X}$ is Set and 1 is a singleton, $\delta_{x}$ will denote the arrow $1 \rightarrow X$ with value $x \in X$.
- $\operatorname{Mor}(\mathbf{X}), \operatorname{Mono}(\mathbf{X})$ and $\operatorname{Reg}(\mathbf{X})$ will denote the class of all arrows, monos and regular monos of $\mathbf{X}$, respectively.
- If $X$ is an object of a category $\mathbf{X}, \operatorname{Sub}(X)$ will denote the collection of its subobjects, i.e. equivalence classes of monos with codomain $X$ with respect to the relation which identifies $m: M \rightarrow X$ and $n: N \rightarrow X$ if and only if there exists an isomorphism $\phi: M \rightarrow N$ such that $n \circ \phi=m$. More generally, we will say that $[m] \leq[n]$ if and only if there exists an arrow $f: M \rightarrow N$ such that $n \circ f=m$. The resulting poset will be denoted by $(\operatorname{Sub}(X), \leq)$.

- If $\mathcal{M} \subseteq \operatorname{Mon}(X)$ is a class of monomorphisms closed under composition with isomorphisms, we define $\operatorname{Sub}_{\mathcal{M}}(X)$ to be the subclass of $\operatorname{Sub}(X)$ given by classes of monos belonging to $\mathcal{M},\left(\operatorname{Sub}_{\mathcal{M}}(X), \leq\right)$ will denote the induced poset.
2.1. The Van Kampen condition. The key property that $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive categories enjoy is given by the so-called Van Kampen condition [BJ97, JLS07, LS05]. We will recall it and examine some of its consequences.

Definition 2.1. Let $\mathbf{X}$ be a category and consider the two diagrams below
![img-0.jpeg](img-0.jpeg)

We say that the left square is a Van Kampen square if:
(1) it is a pushout square;
(2) in every commutative cube as the one above, with pullbacks as back and left faces, the top face is a pushout if and only if the front and right faces are pullbacks.
Pushout squares which enjoy the "if" half of this condition are called stable.
Remark 2.2. Van Kampen property and stability are invariant under isomorphism in two ways:

- if a stable (Van Kampen) stable pushout square of $m$ along $n$ exists, then every other pushout square of $m$ along $n$ is stable (Van Kampen);
- to prove that a given pushout square is stable (Van Kampen), it is enough to verify stability (the Van Kampen property) for a cube in which we have chosen representatives for the pullback squares given by the vertical (back and left) faces.

Before proceeding further, we need to recall a classical result about pullbacks [Mac13, McL92].

Lemma 2.3. Let $\mathbf{X}$ be a category, and consider the following diagram in which the right square is a pullback.
![img-1.jpeg](img-1.jpeg)

Then the whole rectangle is a pullback if and only if the left square is one.

Corollary 2.4. Let $\mathbf{X}$ be a category and let the solid part of the following cube be given
![img-2.jpeg](img-2.jpeg)

If the front face is a pullback then there is a unique $q^{\prime}: Y^{\prime} \rightarrow B^{\prime}$ completing the cube. If, moreover, the right and back faces are pullbacks, then the left face is a pullback too.

We can dualize Lemma 2.3 to get half of the following.
Lemma 2.5. Let $\mathbf{X}$ be a category, and consider the following diagram in which the left square is a pushout.
![img-3.jpeg](img-3.jpeg)

Then the whole rectangle is a pushout if and only if the right square is one.
Moreover, if $\mathbf{X}$ has pullbacks and the left square is stable, then stability of the rectangle is equivalent to that of the right square.

We recall another property of Van Kampen squares from [LS05].
Proposition 2.6. Let $m: A \rightarrow C$ be a monomorphism in a category $\mathbf{X}$, then every Van Kampen square
![img-4.jpeg](img-4.jpeg)
is also a pullback square and $n$ is a monomorphism.
Finally, some kind of left cancellation property holds for pullbacks [GL12, Lemma 2.2].
Lemma 2.7. Let $\mathbf{X}$ be a category with pullbacks, given the following diagrams:
![img-5.jpeg](img-5.jpeg)
if the first square is a stable pushout and the whole rectangles and their left halves are pullbacks, then their common right half is a pullback too.

2.2. Definition of $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesivity. In this section we will define the notion of $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$ adhesivity and explore some of the consequences of such a property. Let us start fixing some terminology.

Definition 2.8. Let $\mathbf{X}$ be a category and $\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}$ two classes of arrows, we say that $\mathcal{A}$ is - stable under pushouts (pullbacks) if for every pushout (pullbacks) square
![img-6.jpeg](img-6.jpeg)
if $m \in \mathcal{A}(n \in \mathcal{A})$ then $n \in \mathcal{A}(m \in \mathcal{A})$;

- closed under composition if $g, f \in \mathcal{A}$ implies $g \circ f \in \mathcal{A}$ whenever $g$ and $f$ are composable;
- closed under $\mathcal{B}$-decomposition if $g \circ f \in \mathcal{A}$ and $g \in \mathcal{B}$ implies $f \in \mathcal{A}$;
- closed under decomposition if it is closed under $\mathcal{A}$-decomposition.

Remark 2.9. Clearly, "decomposition" corresponds to "left cancellation", but we prefer to stick to the name commonly used in literature (see e.g. [HP12]).

Remark 2.10. In the literature, when studying stable systems of monos, stability under pullbacks (pushouts) is frequently assumed to imply the existence of those (co)limits. In this paper we will not make this assumption, because it would lead to a definition of $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesivity more restrictive than the one found in the literature (see Remark 2.15).

Example 2.11. In every category $\mathbf{X}$, split monomorphisms (i.e. those arrows which have a left inverse) are stable under pushouts. Indeed, take a pushout square
![img-7.jpeg](img-7.jpeg)
with $m$ a split monomorphism. Let $r: C \rightarrow A$ be a left inverse of $m$, then

$$
f \circ r \circ m=f \circ \mathrm{id}_{A}=f=\mathrm{id}_{B} \circ f
$$

This equality in turn entails the existence of a unique $t: D \rightarrow B$ as in the diagram below.
![img-8.jpeg](img-8.jpeg)

Lemma 2.12. Let $\mathcal{M}$ be a class of monos in a category $\mathbf{X}$ which is stable under pullbacks and contains all split monos. If pushouts along arrows in $\mathcal{M}$ exist and are Van Kampen, then $\mathcal{M}$ is stable under pushouts.

We are now ready to give the definition of $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive category
Definition 2.13 [HP12, PH16]. Let $\mathbf{X}$ be a category, $\mathcal{M} \subseteq \operatorname{Mono}(\mathbf{X})$ and $\mathcal{N} \subseteq \operatorname{Mor}(\mathbf{X})$, we say that the pair $(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N})$ is a preadhesive structure on $\mathbf{X}$ if the following conditions hold.

(1) $\mathcal{M}$ and $\mathcal{N}$ contain all isomorphisms and are closed under composition and decomposition;
(2) $\mathcal{N}$ is closed under $\mathcal{M}$-decomposition;
(3) $\mathcal{M}$ and $\mathcal{N}$ are stable under pullbacks and pushouts.

Given a preadhesive structure $(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N})$, we say that $\mathbf{X}$ is $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive if
(1) for every $m: X \rightarrow Y$ in $\mathcal{M}$ and $g: Z \rightarrow Y$, a pullback square
![img-9.jpeg](img-9.jpeg)
exists, such pullbacks will be called $\mathcal{M}$-pullbacks;
(2) for every $m: X \rightarrow Y$ in $\mathcal{M}$ and $n: X \rightarrow Z$ in $\mathcal{N}$, a pushout square
![img-10.jpeg](img-10.jpeg)
exists, such pushouts will be called $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-pushouts;
(3) $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-pushouts are Van Kampen squares.

Remark 2.14. Our notion of $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesivity is different from the one of [HP12]: in that paper, $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-pushouts are required to satisfy the Van Kampen condition only for cubes in which the vertical arrows belong to $\mathcal{M}$.

Remark 2.15. As noted in Remark 2.10, we do not assume that stability under pullbacks or pushouts of $\mathcal{M}$ and $\mathcal{N}$ implies the existence of those (co)limits. Indeed, if we were to make this assumption, an $\mathcal{M}, \operatorname{Mor}(\mathbf{X})$-adhesive category would have all pushouts, but this does not correspond to the definition of $\mathcal{M}$-adhesivity in the literature [ACR19] (see also Section 2.3).

Proposition 2.6 yields at once the following fact.
Proposition 2.16. If $\mathbf{X}$ is an $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive category, then $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-pushouts are also pullback squares.

It can be shown that the class of $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive categories is closed under the most common categorical constructions [CGM22, CGM24, Cas23].
Lemma 2.17. Let $\left\{\mathbf{X}_{i}\right\}_{i \in I}$ be a family of categories such that each $\mathbf{X}_{i}$ is $\mathcal{M}_{i}, \mathcal{N}_{i}$-adhesive. Then the product category $\prod_{i \in I} \mathbf{X}_{i}$ is $\prod_{i \in I} \mathcal{M}_{i}, \prod_{i \in I} \mathcal{N}_{i}$-adhesive, where

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \prod_{i \in I} \mathcal{M}_{i}:=\left\{\left(m_{i}\right)_{i \in I} \in \operatorname{Mor}\left(\prod_{i \in I} \mathbf{X}_{i}\right) \mid m_{i} \in \mathcal{M}_{i} \text { for every } i \in I\right\} \\
& \prod_{i \in I} \mathcal{N}_{i}:=\left\{\left(n_{i}\right)_{i \in I} \in \operatorname{Mor}\left(\prod_{i \in I} \mathbf{X}_{i}\right) \mid n_{i} \in \mathcal{N}_{i} \text { for every } i \in I\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Lemma 2.18. Let $\mathbf{X}$ be an $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive category with all pullbacks and pushouts. Then for every other category $\mathbf{Y}$, the category of functors $\mathbf{X}^{\mathbf{Y}}$ is $\mathcal{M}^{\mathbf{Y}}, \mathcal{N}^{\mathbf{Y}}$-adhesive, where

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{M}^{\mathbf{Y}} & :=\left\{\eta \in \operatorname{Mor}\left(\mathbf{X}^{\mathbf{Y}}\right) \mid \eta_{Y} \in \mathcal{M} \text { for every object } Y \text { of } \mathbf{Y}\right\} \\
\mathcal{N}^{\mathbf{Y}} & :=\left\{\eta \in \operatorname{Mor}\left(\mathbf{X}^{\mathbf{Y}}\right) \mid \eta_{Y} \in \mathcal{N} \text { for every object } Y \text { of } \mathbf{Y}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Lemma 2.19. Let $\mathbf{Y}$ be a full subcategory of an $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive category $\mathbf{X}$ with all pullbacks. If $\left(\mathcal{M}^{\prime}, \mathcal{N}^{\prime}\right)$ is a preadhesive structure on $\mathbf{Y}$ such that $\mathcal{M}^{\prime} \subseteq \mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}^{\prime} \subseteq \mathcal{N}$ and $\mathbf{Y}$ is closed in $\mathbf{X}$ under pullbacks and $\mathcal{M}^{\prime}, \mathcal{N}^{\prime}$-pushouts, then $\mathbf{Y}$ is $\mathcal{M}^{\prime}, \mathcal{N}^{\prime}$-adhesive.

Lemma 2.20. Let $\mathbf{X}$ and $\mathbf{Y}$ be respectively $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive and $\mathcal{M}^{\prime}, \mathcal{N}^{\prime}$-adhesive categories with all pushouts and pullbacks. Let also $L: \mathbf{X} \rightarrow \mathbf{Z}$ be a functor that preserves $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$ pushouts, and $R: \mathbf{Y} \rightarrow \mathbf{Z}$ a functor which preserves pullbacks. Then $L \downarrow R$ is $\mathcal{M} \downarrow \mathcal{M}^{\prime}, \mathcal{N} \downarrow \mathcal{N}^{\prime}$ adhesive, where

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{M} \downarrow \mathcal{M}^{\prime} & :=\left\{(h, k) \in \operatorname{Mor}(L \downarrow R) \mid h \in \mathcal{M}, k \in \mathcal{M}^{\prime}\right\} \\
\mathcal{N} \downarrow \mathcal{N} & :=\left\{(h, k) \in \operatorname{Mor}(L \downarrow R) \mid h \in \mathcal{N}, k \in \mathcal{N}^{\prime}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

These three results, in turn, allow us to provide examples of graphical and hypergraphical $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive categories (see again [CGM22, CGM24] for details and other examples).

Example 2.21. Take the category Graph of graphs and let SGraph and DAG be its full subcategories of given by simple graphs and directed acyclic graphs. We say that a monomorphism $(f, g): \mathcal{G} \rightarrow \mathcal{H}$ in Graph downward closed if, for all $e \in E_{\mathcal{H}}, t_{\mathcal{H}}(e) \in g\left(V_{\mathcal{G}}\right)$ implies $e \in f\left(E_{\mathcal{G}}\right)$. We denote by $\mathrm{dcl}, \mathrm{dcl}_{\mathrm{s}}$ and $\mathrm{dcl}_{\mathrm{d}}$ the classes of downward closed morphisms in Graph, SGraph and DAG respectively. Then we have:

- SGraph is Reg(SGraph), Mono(SGraph)-adhesive and Mono(SGraph), Reg(SGraph)adhesive;
- DAG is $\mathrm{dcl}_{\mathrm{d}}$, Mono(DAG)-adhesive.

Example 2.22. Let Tree be the category of tree orders, i.e. partial orders $(E, \leq)$ such that for every $e \in E$, the set $\downarrow e:=\left\{e^{\prime} \in E \mid e^{\prime} \leq e\right\}$ is finite and totally ordered by the restriction of $\leq$. The category HGraph of hierarchical graphs [Pal04] is defined as the comma category $L \downarrow R$ where $L$ : Tree $\rightarrow$ Set is the forgetful functor and $R$ : Set $\rightarrow$ Set is the functor sending $X \mapsto X \times X$. Then, HGraph is adhesive.

Example 2.23. More generally, if we want to use simple or directed acyclic graphs to model the hierarchy between edges, we can define the categories of SGraph-graphs respectively DAG-graphs as the comma categories $L \downarrow R$ where

- $L$ is one of the two forgetful functors $U_{\text {SGraph }}: \mathbf{S G r a p h} \rightarrow$ Set or $U_{\mathbf{D A G}}: \mathbf{D A G} \rightarrow$ Set; - $R: \mathbf{S e t} \rightarrow$ Set is the functor sending $X \mapsto X \times X$.

Then we get the following results:

- the categories of SGraph-graphs is $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive, where

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{M} & :=\left\{\left(\left(h_{1}, h_{2}\right), k\right) \in U_{\mathbf{S G r a p h}} \downarrow R \mid\left(h_{1}, h_{2}\right) \in \operatorname{Reg}(\mathbf{S G r a p h}), k \in \operatorname{Mono}(\text { Set })\right\} \\
\mathcal{N} & :=\left\{\left(\left(h_{1}, h_{2}\right), k\right) \in U_{\mathbf{S G r a p h}} \downarrow R \mid\left(h_{1}, h_{2}\right) \in \operatorname{Mono}(\mathbf{S G r a p h})\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

- the categories of DAG-graphs is $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive where

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{M} & :=\left\{\left(\left(h_{1}, h_{2}\right), k\right) \in U_{\mathbf{D A G}} \downarrow R \mid\left(h_{1}, h_{2}\right) \in \mathrm{dcl}_{\mathrm{d}}, k \in \operatorname{Mono}(\mathbf{S e t})\right\} \\
\mathcal{N} & :=\left\{\left(\left(h_{1}, h_{2}\right), k\right) \in U_{\mathbf{D A G}} \downarrow R \mid\left(h_{1}, h_{2}\right) \in \operatorname{Mono}(\mathbf{D A G})\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Example 2.24. We can further modify Examples 2.22 and 2.23 to get hypergraphical versions of it: it is enough to replace the functor Set $\rightarrow$ Set sending $X$ to $X \times X$, with the functor sending $X$ to $X^{*} \times X^{*}$, where $(-)^{*}: \mathbf{S e t} \rightarrow \mathbf{S e t}$ is the Kleene star.

2.3. Relation with $\mathcal{M}$-adhesivity. We will end this section proving that, under suitable hypothesis, $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesivity subsumes $\mathcal{M}$-adhesivity as defined in [ACR19].

Definition 2.25. Let $\mathbf{X}$ be a category, a stable system of monos $\mathcal{M}$ is a class of monomorphisms closed under composition, containing all isomorphisms and stable under pullbacks.

Lemma 2.26. Let $f: X \rightarrow Y$ be an arrow in a category $\mathbf{X}$ equipped of a stable system of monos $\mathcal{M}$. For any mono $m: Y \rightarrow Z$, if $m \circ f \in \mathcal{M}$ then $f \in \mathcal{M}$.

Definition 2.27 [ACR19]. Given a stable system of monos $\mathcal{M}$ on a category $\mathbf{X}$, we say that $\mathbf{X}$ is $\mathcal{M}$-adhesive if
(1) it has $\mathcal{M}$-pullbacks;
(2) for every $m: X \rightarrow Y$ in $\mathcal{M}$ and for any other arrow $f: X \rightarrow Z$, a pushout square
![img-11.jpeg](img-11.jpeg)
exists and it is a Van Kampen square.
Remark 2.28. We will stick to the notion of $\mathcal{M}$-adhesivity as defined in [ACR19], as noted in Remark 2.14. Other authors have introduced weaker notions of $\mathcal{M}$-adhesivity, where the Van Kampen condition is required to hold only for some cubes; see, e.g., [BHK22, EEPT06, $\mathrm{EGH}^{+} 14$, EHPP04, SB20], where our $\mathcal{M}$-adhesive categories are called adhesive HLR categories.

On the other hand, in [ACR19] no requirement about the existence of pullbacks or $\mathcal{M}$-pullbacks is made, while in [GL12, JLS07, LS05] adhesive and quasiadhesive categories are required to have all pullbacks. Mimicking the definition of $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesivity, for us an $\mathcal{M}$-adhesive category must have $\mathcal{M}$-pullbacks.

Proposition 2.29. Let $\mathbf{X}$ be an $\mathcal{M}$-adhesive category and suppose that every split mono is in $\mathcal{M}$, then $\mathcal{M}$ is stable under pushouts.

Example 2.30. The first, and fundamental, example is when $\mathcal{M}$ is the class of all monomorphisms: in this case $\mathcal{M}$-adhesivity is simply called adhesivity.

One would weaken the previous example using regular monos instead of simple monomorphisms. The problem is that $\operatorname{Reg}(\mathbf{X})$ is not in general closed under composition. Anyway, the Van Kampen property and the existence of $\operatorname{Reg}(\mathbf{X})$-pushouts ensure the closure of regular monos under composition.

Proposition 2.31. For $\mathbf{X}$ a category with $\operatorname{Reg}(\mathbf{X})$-pullbacks, the following are equivalent:
(1) $\operatorname{Reg}(\mathbf{X})$ is a stable system of monos and $\mathbf{X}$ is $\operatorname{Reg}(\mathbf{X})$-adhesive;
(2) pushouts along regular monos exist and are Van Kampen.

Remark 2.32. A category with pullbacks and pushouts along regular monos and in which such pushouts are Van Kampen is what in the literature is usually called a quasiadhesive category, a notable exception is [GL12], in which rm-adhesive is used.

Lemma 2.33. Let $\mathcal{M}$ be a stable system of monos in a category $\mathbf{X}$ which is also stable under pushouts, then the following are equivalent:
(1) $\mathbf{X}$ is $\mathcal{M}$-adhesive;

(2) $\mathbf{X}$ is $\mathcal{M}, \operatorname{Mor}(\mathbf{X})$-adhesive.

We can apply Proposition 2.29 to get the following corollary at once.
Corollary 2.34. Let $\mathcal{M}$ be a stable system of monos in a category $\mathbf{X}$ and suppose that it contains all split monomorphisms., then the following are equivalent:
(1) $\mathbf{X}$ is $\mathcal{M}$-adhesive;
(2) $\mathbf{X}$ is $\mathcal{M}, \operatorname{Mor}(\mathbf{X})$-adhesive.

If we specialize the previous results to the classes of monos and regular monos we get the following.

Corollary 2.35. A category $\mathbf{X}$ is adhesive if and only if it is $\operatorname{Mono}(\mathbf{X}), \operatorname{Mor}(\mathbf{X})$-adhesive and it is quasiadhesive if and only if it is $\operatorname{Reg}(\mathbf{X}), \operatorname{Mor}(\mathbf{X})$-adhesive).

# 3. $\mathcal{N}$-(PRE)ADHESIVE MORPHISMS 

In [GL12, JLS07] a criterion to establish quasiadhesivity is provided, involving the closure of regular monos under unions. The aim of this section is to adapt those results to the setting of $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesivity.

The first step that we need to take is to generalize the notions of adhesive and preadhesive morphism provided in [GL12].

Definition 3.1. Given a class $\mathcal{N}$ of arrows of a category $\mathbf{X}$, we say that $\mathcal{N}$ is a matching class if
(1) it contains all isomorphisms;
(2) is closed under composition and decomposition;
(3) is stable under pullbacks and pushouts.

Given a matching class $\mathcal{N}$, a morphism $m: X \rightarrow Y$ in $\mathbf{X}$ is $\mathcal{N}$-preadhesive if for every $n: X \rightarrow Z$ in $\mathcal{N}$, a stable pushout square
![img-12.jpeg](img-12.jpeg)
exists and it is also a pullback for $p$ along $q$. The arrow $m$ will be called $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive if all its pullbacks are $\mathcal{N}$-preadhesive.

We will denote by $\mathcal{N}_{\text {pa }}$ and by $\mathcal{N}_{a}$ the classes of, respectively, $\mathcal{N}$-preadhesive and $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive morphisms.

Notation 3.2. Instead of "Mor(X)-(pre)adhesive" we will use "(pre)adhesive".
Example 3.3. If $\mathbf{X}$ is an $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive category then $\mathcal{N}$ is a matching class. Moreover, $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-pushouts are Van Kampen squares, so every $m \in \mathcal{M}$ is preadhesive. Since $\mathcal{M}$ is closed under pullback this implies that every arrow in $\mathcal{M}$ is also adhesive.

The following proposition collects some useful facts about $\mathcal{N}$-(pre)adhesive morphisms.
Proposition 3.4. Let $\mathcal{N}$ be a matching class on a category $\mathbf{X}$, then the following hold true:
(1) if $m$ is $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive then it is $\mathcal{N}$-preadhesive;
(2) every isomorphism is $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive;

(3) if $n \in \mathcal{N}$ is $\mathcal{N}$-preadhesive then it is a regular mono;
(4) the class $\mathcal{N}_{\mathrm{pa}}$ is closed under composition;
(5) $\mathcal{N}_{\mathrm{a}}$ is stable under pullbacks;
(6) if $\mathbf{X}$ has pullbacks along $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive arrows, then $\mathcal{N}_{\mathrm{a}}$ is closed under composition.

Corollary 3.5. In any category $\mathbf{X}, \operatorname{Mor}(\mathbf{X})_{\mathrm{a}} \subseteq \operatorname{Reg}(\mathbf{X})$.
Corollary 3.6. Let $\mathcal{N}$ be a matching class on a category $\mathbf{X}$ with pullbacks, then:
(1) $\mathcal{N}_{\mathrm{a}} \cap \operatorname{Mono}(\mathbf{X})$ is a stable system of monos;
(2) if $\mathcal{N}_{\mathrm{a}} \cap \operatorname{Mono}(\mathbf{X})$ is stable under pushouts, then $\left(\mathcal{N}_{\mathrm{a}} \cap \operatorname{Mono}(\mathbf{X}), \mathcal{N}\right)$ is a preadhesive structure.

In general we cannot guarantee closure of $\mathcal{N}_{\mathrm{a}}$ under all pushouts, nonetheless we can still establish some result along this line.

Lemma 3.7 (Cfr. [GL12], Prop. 2.3). Let $\mathcal{N}$ be a matching class in a category $\mathbf{X}$ with pullbacks and consider the following pushout
![img-13.jpeg](img-13.jpeg)
with $n \in \mathcal{N}$. If $m_{1}$ is mono and $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive, then:
(1) $m_{2}$ is mono;
(2) $m_{2}$ is $\mathcal{N}$-preadhesive;
(3) $m_{2}$ is $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive.

Proof. (1) Since $\mathbf{X}$ has pullbacks, we have a diagram
![img-14.jpeg](img-14.jpeg)
in which the square is a pullback, so that the dotted $h$ exists because of its universal property. We aim to prove that $p$ and $q$ are isomorphisms. To get the thesis it is now enough to prove that $p$ is an isomorphism. Indeed, when taking the pullback of an arrow along itself, the fact that one projection is an isomorphism entails that also the other one is so. Therefore, if $p$ is invertible then $q$ is an isomorphism too and thus $m_{2}$ a monomorphism.

To this end, we can build the following cube, starting from the bottom face (the hypothesis) and the right face (the pullback above):
![img-15.jpeg](img-15.jpeg)

By Proposition 3.4(1) the bottom and front faces are stable pushouts and pullbacks because $m_{1}$ is $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive, and the right square is a pullback by hypothesis. Lemma 2.3 entails that the rectangle
![img-16.jpeg](img-16.jpeg)
are pullbacks, thus the back faces of the two cubes are pullbacks too. The left faces are pullbacks because $m_{1}$ is mono, therefore, by stability of the bottom faces, it follows that the top faces pushouts and thus $p$ is an isomorphism.
(2) Let $k: Z \rightarrow Q$ be another arrow in $\mathcal{N}$ and consider the diagram
![img-17.jpeg](img-17.jpeg)
in which the left square and the external rectangle are stable pushouts and pullbacks. Since

$$
f \circ k \circ n=t \circ m_{1}
$$

the universal property of the left square yields the dotted $s$. By Lemma 2.5 the square so obtained is a stable pushout, thus we are left with showing that it is a pullback. Given the solid part of the diagram
![img-18.jpeg](img-18.jpeg)
we have

$$
f \circ l_{1}=s \circ m_{2} \circ l_{2}=f \circ k \circ l_{2}
$$

By the previous point $f$ is mono and thus the following rectangle is a pullback
![img-19.jpeg](img-19.jpeg)

The thesis now follows applying the previous point and Lemma 2.7 to the following diagrams.
![img-20.jpeg](img-20.jpeg)

(3) Take an arrow $w: W^{\prime} \rightarrow W$ and consider the following cube, in which the solid faces are pullbacks
![img-21.jpeg](img-21.jpeg)

By Corollary 2.4 the arrow $m_{1}^{\prime}: X^{\prime} \rightarrow Y^{\prime}$ exists and the added face is a pullback. Since the bottom face is a stable pushout then the top face is a pushout too. By point 5 of $3.4 m_{1}^{\prime}$ is $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive and, since $\mathcal{N}$ is matching, $n^{\prime}$ is in $\mathcal{N}$. The previous point of this lemma implies that $m_{2}^{\prime}$ is $\mathcal{N}$-preadhesive and we can conclude.
Corollary 3.8. If $\mathbf{X}$ is a category with pullbacks then $\left(\operatorname{Mor}(\mathbf{X})_{\mathrm{a}}, \operatorname{Mor}(\mathbf{X})\right)$ is a preadhesive structure.

Finally, $\mathcal{N}$-adhesivity allows us to compute suprema of certain pairs of subobjects.
Proposition 3.9 (Cfr. [GL12], Prop. 2.4). Let $\mathcal{N}$ be a matching class in a category $\mathbf{X}$ with pullbacks. Given an $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive mono $m: M \rightarrow X$ and another mono $n: N \rightarrow X$ in $\mathcal{N}$, consider the diagram
![img-22.jpeg](img-22.jpeg)
in which the outer boundary form a pullback and the inner square a pushout. Then the dotted arrow $u: U \rightarrow X$ is a monomorphism and, in $(\operatorname{Sub}(X), \leq)$

$$
[u]=[m] \vee[n]
$$

Remark 3.10. Notice that the $p_{2}$ and $p_{1}$ are both monos, moreover, $p_{2}$ is $\mathcal{N}$-preadhesive while $p_{1} \in \mathcal{N}$, as the pullback of $n$, thus the inner pushout exists.
Proof of Proposition 3.9. Consider the following two pullback squares
![img-23.jpeg](img-23.jpeg)

Since $n \circ p_{2}$ is equal to $m \circ p_{1}$, we get arrows $f_{1}: N \rightarrow Q, f_{2}: P \rightarrow Q, g_{1}: M \rightarrow W$, $g_{2}: P \rightarrow W$ making the following diagrams commute.
![img-24.jpeg](img-24.jpeg)

Their outer edges are pullbacks, thus in the following cubes, the vertical faces are pullbacks
![img-25.jpeg](img-25.jpeg)
$p_{2}$ is $\mathcal{N}$-preadhesive, so the top faces are pushouts and therefore $f_{1}$, and $g_{1}$ are isomorphisms with inverses given by $q_{1}$ and $w_{1}$. But then, since $u_{1}=q_{2} \circ f_{1}$ and $u_{2}=w_{2} \circ g_{1}$, we can further deduce that the squares below are both pullbacks.
![img-26.jpeg](img-26.jpeg)

We have three diagrams
![img-27.jpeg](img-27.jpeg)
and we have just proved that the rectangles are pullbacks, thus we can apply Lemma 2.7 to deduce that the following square is a pullback-which means exactly that $u$ is a mono.
![img-28.jpeg](img-28.jpeg)

For the second half: suppose that $k: K \rightarrow X$ is an upper bound for $m$ and $n$, thus there exists $k_{1}: M \rightarrow K$ and $k_{2}: N \rightarrow K$ such that $m=k \circ k_{1}$ and $n=k \circ k_{2}$ so that

$$
k \circ k_{1} \circ p_{1}=m \circ p_{1}=n \circ p_{2}=k \circ k_{2} \circ p_{2}
$$

$k$ is mono, thus this implies that there exists a unique $h: U \rightarrow K$ such that

$$
k_{2}=h \circ u_{1} \quad k_{1}=h \circ u_{2}
$$

and we have

$$
k \circ h \circ u_{1}=k \circ k_{2}=n=u \circ u_{1} \quad k \circ h \circ u_{2}=k \circ k_{1}=m=u \circ u_{2}
$$

showing that $u=k \circ h$, i.e. $u \leq k$.

# 4. From $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-unions to $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesivity 

Given a preadhesive structure $(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N})$ such that $\mathcal{M} \subseteq \mathcal{N}_{\mathfrak{a}}$, in this section we will show how to deduce $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesivity from the closure of $\mathcal{M}$ under some kind of unions.
Definition 4.1. Let $(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N})$ be a preadhesive structure, a monomorphism $u: U \rightarrow X$ is an $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-union if there exist $m \in \mathcal{M}$ and $n \in \mathcal{M} \cap \mathcal{N}$ such that, in the poset $(\operatorname{Sub}(X), \leq$ ),

$$
[u]=[m] \vee[n]
$$

We will say that $\mathcal{M}$ is closed under $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-unions, if it contains all such monos.
We will need a technical lemma involving kernel pairs. In order to state it, we need some preliminary observations.

Take a category $\mathbf{X}$ with pullbacks endowed with a preadhesive structure $(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N})$, and take also the following $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-pushout square
![img-29.jpeg](img-29.jpeg)

Pulling back $n$ and $q$ along themselves, we get two diagrams
![img-30.jpeg](img-30.jpeg)
with the dotted arrows $\gamma_{n}: Z \rightarrow K_{n}$ and $\gamma_{q}: Y \rightarrow K_{q}$. Moreover, we have

$$
q \circ m \circ x_{1}=p \circ n \circ x_{1}=p \circ n \circ x_{2}=q \circ m \circ x_{2}
$$

thus we have an arrow $k: K_{n} \rightarrow K_{q}$ as in the following squares.
![img-31.jpeg](img-31.jpeg)

We can also construct another commutative square. From the following chains of equalities

$$
\begin{aligned}
& y_{1} \circ \gamma_{q} \circ m=\mathrm{id}_{Y} \circ m=m=m \circ \mathrm{id}_{X}=m \circ x_{1} \circ \gamma_{n}=y_{1} \circ k \circ \gamma_{n} \\
& y_{2} \circ \gamma_{q} \circ m=\mathrm{id}_{Y} \circ m=m=m \circ \mathrm{id}_{X}=m \circ x_{2} \circ \gamma_{n}=y_{2} \circ k \circ \gamma_{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

we can deduce that $k \circ \gamma_{n}=\gamma_{q} \circ m$.
Lemma 4.2 (Cfr. [GL12], Lemma 2.5). Let $(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N})$ be a preadhesive structure on a category $\mathbf{X}$ with pullbacks such that $\mathcal{M} \subseteq \mathcal{N}_{\mathfrak{a}}, \mathcal{M} \cap \mathcal{N}$ contains every split mono and $\mathcal{M}$ is closed under $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-unions. Then given an $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-pushout square
![img-32.jpeg](img-32.jpeg)

all the squares in the following diagrams, constructed as above, are stable pushouts and pullbacks.
![img-33.jpeg](img-33.jpeg)

Proof. The rightmost square in both diagrams is a pushout by hypothesis, since it is an $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-pushout and $m$ is $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive. Now, by Lemma 2.3 the rectangles
![img-34.jpeg](img-34.jpeg)
are pullbacks, but then also the following rectangles are pullbacks.
![img-35.jpeg](img-35.jpeg)

Therefore their left halves, which are the central squares of the original diagrams, are pullbacks too. In particular this shows that $k$ belongs to $\mathcal{M}$ and thus it is $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive. We can now consider the following two cubes in which all faces are pullbacks
![img-36.jpeg](img-36.jpeg)
which prove that the two central squares in the original diagram are also pushouts.
We are left with the last square. We can deduce that is a pullback applying Lemma 2.3 to the rectangle
![img-37.jpeg](img-37.jpeg)

By construction $\gamma_{n}$ is a split mono thus it is in $\mathcal{N}$. By hypothesis $m \in \mathcal{M}$ is $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive and we can build the following diagram, in which the inner square is a pushout.
![img-38.jpeg](img-38.jpeg)

We already know that the outer edges form a pullback square. $\gamma_{q}$ is in $\mathcal{N}$ because it is a split mono and $k$ is $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive, thus by Proposition 3.9 we get a mono $e: E \rightarrow K_{q}$ filling the diagram and such that $[e]=[k] \vee\left[\gamma_{q}\right]$. Since $\gamma_{q}$ is also in $\mathcal{M}, e$ is an $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-union and thus it belongs to $\mathcal{M}$. Now, by construction

$$
\operatorname{id}_{Y} \circ m=m=m \circ \operatorname{id}_{X}=m \circ x_{1} \circ \gamma_{n}
$$

thus there exists a $h: E \rightarrow Y$ filling the diagram
![img-39.jpeg](img-39.jpeg)

In this diagram the left square and the whole rectangle are pushouts, thus by Lemma 2.5 the right square is a pushout too. Now, $x_{1} \in \mathcal{N}$ as it is the pullback of $n$, and thus $h$ belongs to $\mathcal{N}$ too. On the other hand we have already proved that in diagram
![img-40.jpeg](img-40.jpeg)
the whole rectangle is a pushout hence, using again Lemma 2.5, it follows that its right half is a pushout too. By hypothesis $e$ is $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive and thus the previous square is also a pullback, showing that $e$ is an isomorphism.

We are left with stability: $n \in \mathcal{N}$ by hypothesis, $\gamma_{n}$ is in $\mathcal{N}$ because it is a split mono and $x_{1}$ and $x_{2}$ belongs to $\mathcal{N}$ as they are pullbacks of $n$. Since we have proved that $m$ and $k$ are in $\mathcal{M}$ we know that they are $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive and we can conclude.

Our next step is proving that if $\mathcal{M}$ contains only $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive morphisms then $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$ pushouts are almost Van Kampen squares.

Lemma 4.3. Let $\mathbf{X}$ be a category with pullbacks and consider the following cube in which the left, back, bottom and top faces are pullbacks.
![img-41.jpeg](img-41.jpeg)

Suppose that $p$ and $p^{\prime}$ are monos and that the top face is a stable pushout. Then the right face is a pullback.

Proof. Since $p$ is a mono, by Lemma 2.3, the rectangle
![img-42.jpeg](img-42.jpeg)
is a pullback. Take now the following three diagrams
![img-43.jpeg](img-43.jpeg)

By hypothesis the first square is a stable pushout and the left half of the first rectangle is a pullback. Since also the bottom face is a pullback by hypothesis, it follows that the whole first rectangle is a pullback too. By the previous observation, the whole second rectangle is a pullback and, since $p^{\prime}$ is a mono, its first half is a pullback square. We can then apply Lemma 2.7 to get the thesis.

Corollary 4.4. Let $(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N})$ be a preadhesive structure on a category $\mathbf{X}$ with pullbacks and suppose that every arrow in $\mathcal{M}$ is $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive. For every $m \in \mathcal{M}, n \in \mathcal{N}$ and cube
![img-44.jpeg](img-44.jpeg)
if the top and bottom faces are pushouts and the left and back ones are pullbacks then the right face is a pullback.

We are now ready to prove our theorem, generalizing [GL12, Prop. 3.1].

Theorem 4.5. Let $(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N})$ be a preadhesive structure on a category $\mathbf{X}$ with pullbacks and suppose that every split mono is in $\mathcal{M} \cap \mathcal{N}, \mathcal{M} \subseteq \mathcal{N}_{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\mathcal{M}$ is closed under $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-unions, then $\mathbf{X}$ is $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-adhesive.

Proof. Every element of $\mathcal{M}$ is $\mathcal{N}$-adhesive, thus we already know that $\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}$-pushouts exist and are stable. Since $\mathbf{X}$ has all pullbacks by hypothesis, all that we have to show is the remaining half of the Van Kampen condition. Take a cube in which $m \in \mathcal{M}, n \in \mathcal{N}$ and such that the top and bottom faces are pushouts and the left and back ones are pullbacks

$m^{\prime}$ and $n^{\prime}$ belong to, respectively, $\mathcal{M}$ and $\mathcal{N}$ thus the top face is a stable pushout square, which is also a pullback. By Corollary 4.4 we already know that the right face is a pullback, let us prove that the other one is a pullback too.

By Lemma 4.2, in the following diagrams all squares are stable pushouts and pullbacks.
![img-46.jpeg](img-46.jpeg)

By Corollary 2.4, there exist the dotted $t_{1}: K_{n^{\prime}} \rightarrow K_{n}$ and $t_{2}: K_{q^{\prime}} \rightarrow K_{q}$ as below:
![img-47.jpeg](img-47.jpeg)

and the left face of the first cube is a pullback square. If we compute we get

$$
\begin{aligned}
& x_{1} \circ t_{1} \circ \gamma_{n^{\prime}}=x \circ x_{1}^{\prime} \circ \gamma_{n^{\prime}}=x \circ \mathrm{id}_{X^{\prime}}=\mathrm{id}_{X} \circ x=x_{1} \circ \gamma_{n} \circ x \\
& y_{1} \circ t_{2} \circ \gamma_{q^{\prime}}=y \circ y_{1}^{\prime} \circ \gamma_{q^{\prime}}=y \circ \mathrm{id}_{Y^{\prime}}=\mathrm{id}_{Y} \circ y=y_{1} \circ \gamma_{q} \circ y \\
& y_{1} \circ t_{2} \circ k^{\prime}=y \circ y_{1}^{\prime} \circ k^{\prime}=y \circ m^{\prime} \circ x_{1}^{\prime}=m \circ x \circ x_{1}^{\prime}=m \circ x_{1} \circ t_{1}=y_{1} \circ k \circ t_{1} \\
& x_{2} \circ t_{1} \circ \gamma_{n^{\prime}}=x \circ x_{2}^{\prime} \circ \gamma_{n^{\prime}}=x \circ \mathrm{id}_{X^{\prime}}=\mathrm{id}_{X} \circ x=x_{2} \circ \gamma_{n} \circ x \\
& y_{2} \circ t_{2} \circ \gamma_{q^{\prime}}=y \circ y_{2}^{\prime} \circ \gamma_{q^{\prime}}=y \circ \mathrm{id}_{Y^{\prime}}=\mathrm{id}_{Y} \circ y=y_{2} \circ \gamma_{q} \circ y \\
& y_{2} \circ t_{2} \circ k^{\prime}=y \circ y_{2}^{\prime} \circ k^{\prime}=y \circ m^{\prime} \circ x_{2}^{\prime}=m \circ x \circ x_{2}^{\prime}=m \circ x_{2} \circ t_{1}=y_{2} \circ k \circ t_{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

therefore the following three squares commute

The first one of the squares above is a pullback: this follows applying Lemma 2.3 to the rectangle below.

We can then use these arrows $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$ to construct the following cube

which has pullbacks as left and back faces and stable pushouts as top and bottom ones. $\gamma_{q}$ and $\gamma_{q^{\prime}}$ are split monos, thus by Lemma 4.3 the right face is a pullback. Switching $\gamma_{n}$ and $m$ we get another cube
![img-51.jpeg](img-51.jpeg)

to which we can apply Corollary 4.4, to get again that the right face is a pullback. Now, by Lemma 2.3 the following rectangle is a pullback

Thus we can apply Lemma 2.7 to the diagrams

to deduce that the square below is a pullback too.

Which, in turn also entails that the following rectangle is a pullback.
